Description: This domain is found at the N-terminal of strictosidine synthase-like (SSL) proteins including Adipocyte plasma membrane- associated proteins (APMAPs) from animals, Protein STRICTOSIDINE SYNTHASE-LIKE (SSLs) from Arabidopsis and SGL proteins, being also present in bacterial sequences. It is about 50 amino acids in length. It contains residues involved in metal coordination in the active site. This domain is also found in Gluconolactonase and Sugar lactone lactonase. These proteins share a six-bladed beta-propeller fold structure and have similar structural and mechanistic features to SS (strictosidine synthase) that involve nucleophilic attack on an electrophilic substrate, although they do not catalyse the SS reaction as they lack the catalytic glutamate required for SS activity; they catalyse hydrolytic reactions instead [[PMID:18513186],[PMID:18280746],[PMID:21948213],[PMID:20033437]]. APMAPs shows similarity with paraoxonases (PON) and has a strong arylesterase activity with beta-naphthyl acetate and phenyl acetate. They are involved in adipocyte differentiation [[PMID:18513186],[PMID:21948213]].

Name: ALTTAQ repeat
Description: This repeat is called after the short repeat ALTTAQ found in uncharacterised proteins from Proteobacteria, some eukaryotes and viruses, and it is functionally uncharacterised.

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF6493)
Description: This presumed domain is functionally uncharacterised. This domain is found in bacteria, and is approximately 470 amino acids in length. This domain may consist of TPRs.

Name: Toxin TumE
Description: This entry represents a family of proteins found in archaea and bacteria. These proteins have been recently described as the toxin component TumE from the novel toxin-antitoxin system TumA, which constitutes a translation-targeting toxic effector [[PMID:37704037]]. These proteins are predicted to adopt a conserved alpha+beta fold, where two alpha-helices pack against an antiparallel beta-sheet with seven strands [[PMID:37704037]].

Name: Protein HHL1-like
Description: This entry represents a group of proteins from plants and Cyanobacteria, including HHL1 from Arabidopsis. HHL1 functions in protecting photosystem II against photodamage [[PMID:24632535]].

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF6528)
Description: This family of proteins is functionally uncharacterised. This family of proteins is found in bacteria and eukaryotes. Proteins in this family are typically between 311 and 340 amino acids in length.

Name: Brassinosteroid receptor island domain
Description: The island domain in BRI1 corresponds to a large insertion in the regular repeat-structure between LRRs 21 and 22. The resulting 70-residue segment forms a small domain that folds back into the interior of the LRR superhelix, where it makes extensive polar and hydrophobic interactions with LRRs 13-25. The domain fold is characterized by an anti-parallel beta-sheet, which is sandwiched between the LRR core and a 3-10 helix and stabilized by a disulphide bridge.  The insertion of a folded domain into the LRR repeat has not been observed in other LRR receptor structures, and is probably an adaptation to the challenge of sensing a small steroid ligand (rather than larger ligands, such as proteins, nucleic acids, or lipids.

Name: C-JID domain
Description: C-terminal jelly roll/Ig-like domain (C-JID) was defined in cryogenic electron microscopy (cryoEM) structures of plant intracellular immune receptors containing Toll/interleukin-1 receptor (TIR, PF01582), nucleotide-binding (NB-ARC, PF00931) and leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domains (TIR-NLRs) [[PMID:33273074],[PMID:33273071]]. Structurally, the C-JID core is represented by a beta-sandwich made up of 8 to 9 beta-strands. C-JID matches the so-called post-LRR or C-terminal non-LRR domain detected earlier via MEME and BLAST searches [[PMID:27129402],[PMID:11158537]]. The domain showed a strong distribution bias towards TIR-NLRs of dicotyledonous plant species despite broader taxonomic distribution of TIR-NLR in plant groups [[PMID:33273074],[PMID:33273071],[PMID:27129402],[PMID:33197377],[PMID:24744375],[PMID:25340333],[PMID:11158537]]. Structure-function analyses of cryoEM structures suggest that C-JID domains play a role in substrate recognition, such as binding to effector proteins from pathogens, and thus are involved in the initiation of signaling by TIR-NLR receptors [[PMID:33273074],[PMID:33273071]]. Presence of C-JID (or post-LRR) and its importance for the function of Arabidopsis TIR-NLR RPS4 that partners with RRS1 for effector recognition suggest that C-JID has additional functions [[PMID:33197377],[PMID:24744375],[PMID:25340333]].

Name: KBTB, W-type LIR motif
Description: LC3 Interacting Regions (LIRs), also known as ATG8-interacting motifs (AIMs), are short-linear motifs (SLiMs) of autophagy receptors and adaptor proteins that facilitates the selective recruitment of autophagy substrates to the autophagosome. ATG8 proteins are highly conserved in eukaryotes and play a key role in selective autophagy as they recognise and bind different autophagy receptors and adaptors containing a specific class of SLiMs. LIRs are characterised by degenerated sequences with a four-residue core central sequence involved in ATG8-binding, with the W/Y/FxxL/I/V pattern. Based on the aromatic amino acid in position 1 they can be classified into W-type, Y-type and F-type. This is the W-type LIR motif from Kelch repeat and BTB domain-containing proteins (KBTBs), characterised by the core LIR sequence WVxV [[PMID:32587856]].

Name: Sister chromatid cohesion protein PDS5 protein
Description: This entry represents the Sister chromatid cohesion protein PDS5. The large PDS5 molecule is exclusively alpha helical, composed of a large number of HEAT-like repeats and helical extensions/additions that deviate from the HEAT repeat pattern [[PMID:26923598]].

Name: Tra1 HEAT repeat central region
Description: This entry represents part of the Tra1 protein composed of alpha solenoid repeats that forms the central region [[PMID:29146944]]. This is named as central due to its position relative to the ring region.

Name: Dermonecrotic toxin, N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of bacterial proteins, such as the dermonecreotic toxin (ToxA) from Pasteurella multocida, an osteolytic toxin that induces bone resorption. It is a potent mitogen. This toxin is associated with the severe progressive form of the atrophic rhinitis a major respiratory disease in pigs. This domain contributes to the membrane localisation of its C-terminal domains [[PMID:17360394]].

Name: Complex III assembly factor UQCC2/CBP6
Description: This family represents mitochondrial respiratory chain complex III (or cytochrome b-c1 complex) assembly factors, including Cbp6 from yeast and its human orthologue UQCC2 (Ubiquinol-Cytochrome c Reductase Complex Assembly Factor 2) [[PMID:24385928],[PMID:21670217]]. These proteins have diverged significantly among eukaryotes and UQCC2/Cbp6 have an overall low amino acid conservation [[PMID:24385928]]. UQCC2 interacts with UQCC1, the human orthologue of the Cbp6 binding partner Cbp3, to form a complex necessary for cytochrome b biogenesis [[PMID:24385928]]. Cbp3-Cbp6 complex interacts with mitochondrial ribosomes for efficient translation of cytochrome b transcript and it also interacts with newly synthesized cytochrome b to assist its correct assembly [[PMID:21670217]]. These proteins contain a LRY-like N-terminal sequence being related to the LYR superfamily of proteins, which function as subunits or assembly factors for respiratory complexes I, II, III, and V [[PMID:28634302]].

Name: Tra1 HEAT repeat ring region
Description: This entry represents part of the Tra1 protein composed of alpha solenoid repeats that form a ring region [[PMID:29146944]].

Name: ARG and Rhodanese-Phosphatase-superfamily-associated Protein domain
Description: ARPP-1 shows a distinguishing group of absolutely conserved residues, including a cysteine and a glutamine, suggestive of enzymatic activity. Structural predictions reveal a core beta-sheet comprised of a duplicated four-stranded beta-meander with a beta-barrel domain bearing the absolutely conserved residues inserted into the first repeat. The unifying biochemical theme across its conserved contextual genome linkages is the presence of molecules with sugar moieties (ADPr, nucleotides, and polysaccharides). In genome contexts linking ARPP-1 with ARG and other counter viral ribosylation domains, ARPP is predicted to act on ribose moieties coming from ADPr. The presence of the conserved cysteine suggests that it could function analogous to the recently characterized SRAP domain by forming covalent cross-links with sugars [[PMID:36146784],[PMID:36968430]].

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF6584)
Description: This family of proteins is functionally uncharacterised. This family of proteins is found in bacteria, mainly Actinobacteria and Firmicutes.  Many members are thought to contain TPR regions There are two conserved motifs, AxRL and LxxY.

Name: VasX toxin N-terminal region
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal region of the VasX toxin [swissprot:Q9KNE5] from Vibrio cholerae.

Name: Protein UNC80 C-terminal region
Description: This family represents the protein UNC80 found in eukaryotes, a component of the NALCN sodium channel complex. NALC is a cation voltage-independent channel activated by substance P, neurotensin, acetylcholine and noradrenaline that controls neuronal excitability. UNC80 forms a complex with UNC79 and both are key regulators of the channel and required for the proper expression and axonal localisation of NALCN. UNC80 is required for NALCN control by GPCRs and essential for its sensitivity to extracellular calcium. This protein acts as a scaffold for Src family of tyrosine kinases (SFK) and UNC-79 to mediate interaction with NALCN [[PMID:24904279],[PMID:21040849],[PMID:31690562]].

Name: LYR motif-containing protein 2-like
Description: This entry represents LYR motif-containing proteins, including LYR motif-containing protein 2 from animals, whose function is not clear, and Cup1 (caffeine unstable phenotype 1, previously known as uncharacterised protein C17G9.13c) from S. pombe [[PMID:32908306]]. Cup1 has been characterised; it is a mitochondrial protein whose silencing by heterochromatin island formation results in caffeine resistance. LYR-motif containing proteins are basic polypeptides carrying a conserved tripeptide L-Y-R (leucine/tyrosine/arginine) sequence close to the N-terminus in addition to some basic arginine/lysine residues and a highly-conserved phenylalanine downstream of the LYR motif [[PMID:25686363]].

Name: CASPASE and TPR Repeat-Associated N-terminal domain
Description: One of two constant domains forming the CATRA module which is in turn C-terminally fused to a diverse range of conflict effector domains. This gene, along with a gene encoding the CATASP domain and a gene encoding a TPR repeat region fused to a CASPASE domain, forms the three gene island making up the CATRA conflict systems. This alpha/beta domain is predicted to be involved in recognition of invasive molecules or in the transmitting of a signal through conformational change, enabling CASPASE-mediated proteolysis to free the fused effector domains.

Name: CASPASE and TPR Repeat-Associated C-terminal domain
Description: One of two constant domains forming the CATRA module which is in turn C-terminally fused to a diverse range of conflict effector domains. This gene, along with a gene encoding the CATASP domain and a gene encoding a TPR repeat region fused to a CASPASE domain, forms the three gene island making up the CATRA conflict systems. This all alpha-helical domain is predicted to be involved in recognition of invasive molecules or in the transmitting of a signal through conformational change, enabling CASPASE-mediated proteolysis to free the fused effector domains.

Name: CATRA-Associated Small Protein
Description: Small, rapidly-evolving domain predicted to possess a compact, four-helix structure. Along with a gene encoding a TPR repeat region fused to a CASPASE domain and a gene encoding the CATRA module (CATRA-N+CATRA-C) C-terminally fused to a diverse range of conflict effector domains, forms the three gene island making up the CATRA conflict systems. Plays a possible inhibitory role in the CATRA system which is relieved upon sensing of an invasive molecule or is directly cleaved by the CASPASE domain.

Name: Prokaryotic STING domain
Description: This is a STING sensor domain found in certain prokaryotic nucleotide-activated effector conflict systems. Binding to a nucleotide activates fused effector proteins in conflict response against invasive elements. Certain versions in prokaryotes may additionally regulate bacterial ion channels. Transferred to eukaryotes on multiple occasions, representatives observed are in animal-choanoflagellate, haptophyte/stramenopile, and fungal lineages [[PMID:32868406]].

Name: Tetratricopeptide Repeats-Sensor
Description: This entry represents a sensor domain consisting of 7 TPR repeats forming a tightly-wound solenoid structure harbouring a deep central pocket. The TPR-S binding pocket is lined with several conserved aromatic and polar residues predicted to bind a NAD+-derived nucleotide in prokaryotic NAD+-derived nucleotide-activated effector conflict systems. It has been acquired at the base of the choanoflagellate-animal lineage as a core component of the ASK signalosome [[PMID:32868406]].

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF6693)
Description: This family of small integral membrane proteins is functionally uncharacterised. This family may be distantly related to [pfam:PF05987]. This family of proteins is found in proteobacteria. Proteins in this family are typically between 104 and 121 amino acids in length.

Name: BLTP1-like family N-terminal region
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal region of a group of very long eukaryotic proteins, including the human BLTP1 (also known as KIAA1109) protein. These proteins are tube-forming lipid transport proteins that belong to the RBG (repeating beta-groove) superfamily. They consist of long hydrophobic grooves made of multiple repeating modules of five beta-sheets followed by a loop [[PMID:35491307],[PMID:35015055]]. BLTP1 is a tube-forming lipid transport protein which provides phosphatidylethanolamine for glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchor synthesis in the endoplasmic reticulum. It plays a role in endosomal trafficking and endosome recycling and it is also involved in the actin cytoskeleton and cilia structural dynamics [[PMID:35491307],[PMID:30906834]]. BLTP1 also acts as a regulator of phagocytosis [[PMID:31540829]]. In humans, it has been associated with rheumatoid arthritis [[PMID:26917059]]. Mutations of the BLTP1 gene has been associated with Alkuraya-Kucinskas syndrome (MIM 617822), a severe disorder of brain development and arthrogryposis [[PMID:29290337]].

Name: U3 small nucleolar RNA-associated protein 20 domain
Description: This domain is found towards the C-terminal the U3 small nucleolar RNA-associated protein 20 (UTP20) from yeast and its human homologue, also known as DRIM (Down-Regulated In Metastasis). DRIM is differentially expressed in metastatic and non-metastatic human breast carcinoma cells [[PMID:9673349]]. Proteins of this entry are involved in processing of non-coding RNA as components of the small-subunit (SSU) processome; SSU processome is involved in the biogenesis of the 18S rRNA [[PMID:12837249],[PMID:17498821]]. UTP20 is a huge alpha-solenoid that functions as a scaffold [[PMID:31378463],[PMID:32943522]].

Name: DHR-2, Lobe C
Description: DOCK (dedicator of cytokinesis) proteins are guanine nucleotide exchange factors (GEFs) that activate some small GTPases, such as Rac or Cdc42, by exchanging bound GDP for free GTP to control cell migration, morphogenesis, and phagocytosis. These proteins share a DOCK-type C2 domain (also termed the DOCK-homology region (DHR)-1) at the N-terminal, and the DHR-2 domain (also termed the DOCKER domain) at the C-terminal. DHR-2 is the GEF catalytic domain organised into three lobes A, B and C, with the Rho-family binding site and catalytic centre generated entirely from lobes B and C. This entry represents Lobe C which form an antiparallel four alpha-helical bundle [[PMID:32651375],[PMID:30853411]] and contains a loop known as the nucleotide sensor characterised by a conserved valine residue essential for catalytic activity [[PMID:30853411]].

Name: Neurobeachin alpha solenoid region
Description: This region is found in any neurobeachin homologues. It forms an extended alpha solenoid structure.

Name: Neurobeachin beta propeller domain
Description: This entry represents the beta propeller domain found at the C-terminus of neurobeachin-like proteins.

Name: RNA-binding protein Tab2/Atab2, C-terminal domain
Description: This is the C-terminal domain of Tab2 from plant and cyanobacteria. Tab2 was first identified in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii as a RNA binding protein required for translation of the chloroplast PsaB photosystem I subunit [[PMID:14633996]]. Later, the Tab2 homologue from Arabidopsis (Atab2) was found involved in the signalling pathway of light-controlled synthesis of photosystem proteins during early plant development, presumably functioning as an activator of translation with targets at PSI and PSII [[PMID:25725436],[PMID:17139246]]. Directed mutagenesis experiments carried out in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, Tab2 [swissprot:Q7X8Y6], indicated the importance of a highly conserved C-terminal tripeptide WLL for normal psaB translation [[PMID:14633996]].

Name: E+ motif
Description: This is the E+ motif found in some plant pentatricopeptide repeat (PPR) proteins [[PMID:25512379],[PMID:23288601]] which contain a C-terminal DYW deaminase domain. The DYW domain is required for RNA editing, a process that deaminates specific cytidines to uridines. This motif, together with the E motif, precedes the DYW domain and, although their role is not clear, they are essential in th RNA editing reaction [[PMID:25512379],[PMID:23288601]]. The E/E+ motifs may contain two degenerate PPR motifs that could be involved in RNA or protein binding.

Name: E motif
Description: This entry represents the E motif found in plant pentatricopeptide repeat (PPR) proteins [[PMID:23288601],[PMID:25512379]] which contain a DYW deaminase domain. The DYW domain is required for RNA editing, a process that deaminates specific cytidines to uridines. This motif, together with the E+ motif, precedes the DYW domain and, although their role is not clear, they are essential in the RNA editing reaction [[PMID:23288601],[PMID:25512379]]. The E/E+ motifs may contain two degenerate PPR motifs that could be involved in RNA or protein binding.

Name: ATPase of the ABC class N-terminal
Description: This is the N-terminal domain of various bacterial predicted ABC class ATPases which shows sequence and structural similarities to the N-terminal domain of MRB1590 (swiss:Q57ZF2) from kinetoplastids, a core protein of the multiprotein mitochondrial RNA binding complex 1 (MRB1) involved in kinetoplastid RNA (kRNA) editing [[PMID:26117548]]. MRB1590 is a dimer and contains a central ABC ATPase fold ([pfam:PF09818]) between novel N- and C-terminal regions. The N-terminal domains combine and form a RNA-binding basic pore which involves an arginine patch that has been suggested to act as a chaperone to bind and stabilize ssRNA during kRNA editing processes. N-terminal MRB1590 shows structural similarity to chaperones [[PMID:26117548]].

Name: Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome 6 protein C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal alpha solenoid domain of the HPS6 protein.

Name: Fungal WD-like
Description: This is an uncharacterised family of proteins found in Fungi, present in S. japonicus but seems to be absent in S. pombe and S. cerevisiae. It may be a divergent WD repeat-like family of proteins. There are eight conserved cysteine residues.

Name: DNA-PKcs, N-terminal
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of DNA-dependent protein kinase catalytic subunit (DNA-PKcs), which is arranged in four supersecondary alpha-helical structures, N1 to N4, that resemble HEAT repeats. Therefore, this domain is also known as N-HEAT. This domain likely mediates DNA binding and, together with the Circular Cradle, forms a ring through which Ku70/80 may present DNA for repair. DNA-PKcs is involved in DNA nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ), required for double-strand break (DSB) repair [[PMID:28154079],[PMID:28652322],[PMID:28840859],[PMID:33077952]]. It is recruited by Ku70/80 heterodimer to DNA ends.

Name: DNA-dependent protein kinase catalytic subunit, CC1/2
Description: DNA-dependent protein kinase catalytic subunit (DNA-PKcs) is involved in DNA nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ) which is recruited by Ku70/80 heterodimer to DNA ends and required for double-strand break (DSB) repair. It folds into three well-defined large structural units, consisting of a N-terminal region, the Circular Cradle (consisting of five supersecondary alpha-helical structures CC1 to CC5), and the C-terminal Head (comprising FAT, FRB, kinase, and FATC). The N-terminal and CCs regions resemble HEAT repeats, and thus, they are also referred to as N-HEAT and M-HEAT ('middle'), respectively. The CCs form a curved elliptical ring that serves as a scaffold to maintain the integrity of the whole complex. This entry represents CC1 and CC2, which contain the Highly conserved region I (HCR-I) [[PMID:28154079],[PMID:28652322],[PMID:28840859],[PMID:33077952]].

Name: Anaphase-promoting complex subunit 1 middle domain
Description: Apc1 is the largest of the subunits of the anaphase-promoting complex or cyclosome. The anaphase-promoting complex is a multiprotein subunit E3 ubiquitin ligase complex that controls segregation of chromosomes and exit from mitosis in eukaryotes. Apc1 consists of a N-terminal WD40 beta-propeller domain, followed by the middle domain (Mid-N), a PC domain and the C-terminal domain (MidC). This entry represents the middle domain of Apc1, MidN (also referred to as the first helical domain), that coaleses with the C-terminal domain to form Apc1Mid that connects Apc1WD40 with Apc1PC [[PMID:27601667],[PMID:26083744]]. Apc1Mid consists of an alpha-solenoid capped by a beta-sandwich [[PMID:26083744]].

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF6770)
Description: This family of proteins is functionally uncharacterised. This family of proteins is found in bacteria. Proteins in this family are typically between 514 and 521 amino acids in length.

Name: Putative pectate lyase-like adhesive domain
Description: This putative domain is found at the N-terminus of a large number of bacterial proteins. Its adhesive function was described in Lactobacillus reuteri serine-rich repeat proteins [[PMID:29507249]]. Structure predictions show it is composed of a beta solenoid repeat related to Pectate lyase.

Name: Putative Ice-binding-like adhesive domain
Description: This domain is found N-terminal to repeating stalk domains in bacterial surface proteins. The structure model of this domain is highly similar to the Ice_binding adhesive domain ([pfam:PF11999]). However, the bacterial species in which this domain can be found, are less likely to be water-based and thus the function seems unlikely to be ice-binding.  This domain is found in a Bacillus anthracis protein with the gene name BA_0871 or BASH2_04951, which was described to be collagen binding and to be involved in the bacterial pathogenicity [[PMID:15456768]].

Name: TAF1C, beta-propeller
Description: This is the beta-propeller domain of TAF1C from mammals, the orthologue of yeast Rrn6 [[PMID:28340337],[PMID:28623663]]. TAF1C is a component of the transcription factor SL1/TIF-IB complex which is involved in the assembly of the PIC (preinitiation complex) during RNA polymerase I-dependent transcription [[PMID:15970593]].

Name: Hpf1 C-terminal YTTT repeat
Description: This entry represents a short sequence repeat found at the C-terminus of the yeast Haze protective factor 1 [swissprot:Q05164]. AlphaFold predicts this repeat to adopt a beta hairpin structure.  This repeat is found in multiple copies in a range of fungal species.

Name: Conserved oligomeric Golgi complex subunit 5, C-terminal
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Cog5, a component of the conserved oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex which is involved in intra-Golgi protein trafficking and consists of eight subunits arranged in two lobes bridged by Cog1 [[PMID:25331899],[PMID:34061181]]. Cog5 is a member of the complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods (CATCHR) fold family, which is homologous to subunits of other multisubunit tethering complexes (MTCs) such as Dsl1, exocyst and Golgi-associated retrograde protein (GARP) complexes [[PMID:25331899],[PMID:34061181]]. COG5 is located in the smaller B lobe together with subunits 6-8, and interacts with subunits 1 and 7. This domain represents the alpha-helical bundle found at the C-terminal of COG5.

Name: Exocyst complex component EXOC6/Sec15, N-terminal
Description: This entry represents a domain found at the N-terminal of Exocyst complex components EXOC6/Sec15, members of the complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods (CATCHR) fold family and homologous to subunits of other multisubunit tethering complexes (MTCs) such as Dsl1, Golgi-associated retrograde protein (GARP) and COG complexes [[PMID:34061181],[PMID:16155582],[PMID:29335562]], characterised by a coiled-coild region at the N-terminal and an alpha-helical bundle at the C-terminal [[PMID:34061181]]. This is a coiled-coil domain of Sec15 that forms an antiparallel zipper with Sec10 and interacts with the antiparallel zipper formed between Exo70-Exo84, which form a four-helical bundle part of the core of the exocyst [[PMID:29335562]].

Name: Exocyst complex component Sec8 C-terminal
Description: Sec8 is a component of the exocyst complex, composed of Sec3, Sec5, Sec5, Sec6, Sec8, Sec10, Sec15, Exo70 and Exo84, which mediates the tethering of post-Golgi secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane. The exocyst promotes the assembly of SNARE complex for membrane fusion and it is also involved in many cellular processes such as cell polarisation, primary ciliogenesis, cytokinesis, pathogen invasion, tumourigenesis and metastasis [[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. Subunits of the exocyst have a rod structure which is a shared feature for the Complex Associated with Tethering Containing Helical Rods (CATCHRs) proteins such as those of the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi complex (COG) and Golgi-Associated Retrograde Protein complex (GARP), consisting of a N-terminal long coiled-coil followed by a region of antiparallel short helical bundle units [[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. This entry represents a domain found at the C-terminal of Sec8 subunit, consisting of alpha-helical bundles.

Name: Conserved Oligomeric Golgi complex subunit 6, C-terminal
Description: COG6 is a component of the peripheral membrane COG complex that is involved in intra-Golgi protein trafficking. COG is located at the cis-Golgi, regulates tethering of retrograde intra-Golgi vesicles and consists of one octamer comprising two tetramers (COG1-4 and COG5-8) [[PMID:34061181]]. COG subunits belong to the Complex Associated with Tethering Containing Helical Rods (CATCHRs) family which also includes subunits from the evolutionary related GARP (Golgi-Associated Retrograde Protein), exocyst and Dsl1 complexes. CATCHRs proteins share a structural fold consisting of alpha-helical bundles in tandem at the C-terminal and a coiled-coil region at the N-terminal [[PMID:34061181]]. This entry represents the alpha-helical bundle found at the C-terminal of COG6.

Name: Exocyst complex component Sec3, C-terminal
Description: Sec3 (also known as ExoC1) is a component of the exocyst complex, which mediates the tethering of post-Golgi secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane and promotes the assembly of the SNARE complex for membrane fusion. The exocyst is also involved in cell polarization, primary ciliogenesis, cytokinesis, pathogen invasion, tumourigenesis and metastasis [[PMID:28098232]]. This complex has an elongated shape consisting of packed long rods, a a structure that is shared among the Complex Associated with Tethering Containing Helical Rods (CATCHRs) proteins from related complex such as Conserved Oligomeric Golgi complex (COG) and Golgi-Associated Retrograde Protein complex (GARP) [[PMID:28098232],[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. Subunits of these complexes, apart of helical bundles, they usually have a coiled-coil (CC) region at the N-terminal. Sec3 is described as a membrane-anchoring component. This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Sec3 which interacts with small GTPases [[PMID:16181645]], which consists of the conserved alpha-helical bundle [[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. Sec3 is recruited to the sites of polarised membrane growth through its interaction with the small GTP-binding protein Rho1p [[PMID:16181645]].

Name: Vps52, C-terminal
Description: Vps52 complexes with Vps53 and Vps54 to form the Golgi-associated retrograde protein (GARP) complex that is involved in retrograde transport from early and late endosomes to the trans-Golgi network, regulating membrane trafficking events [[PMID:10637310],[PMID:15878329],[PMID:18367545],[PMID:12686613]]. It is also part of EARP (Endosome-Associated Recycling Protein) complex, involved in endocytic recycling [[PMID:25799061]]. This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Vps52, which consists of alpha-helical bundles, conserved among subunits of other multisubunit tethering complexes (MTC) such as conserved oligomeric Golgi (COG), Dsl1 and the exocyst, all belong to a family of evolutionarily conserved complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods (CATCHR). This domain may interact with the small GTPase Ypt6p/Rab6 [[PMID:15878329]].

Name: Conserved oligomeric Golgi complex subunit 4, C-terminal
Description: COG4 is a component of the conserved oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex which mediates the proper glycosylation of proteins trafficking through the Golgi apparatus. It is included in the CATCHR (complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods) family, which includes components of the exocyst, COG, GARP, and DSL1 complexes, sharing structural and functional features, such as the alpha-helical bundles at the C-terminal and a N-terminal coiled-coil region [[PMID:34061181],[PMID:28098232]]. This is the C-terminal domain which contains the key Arginine residue that stabilises this domain and it is required for the proper glycosylation of cell surface proteins [[PMID:19651599],[PMID:30290151]].

Name: Conserved oligomeric Golgi complex subunit 4, N-terminal
Description: COG4 is a component of the conserved oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex which mediates the proper glycosylation of proteins trafficking through the Golgi apparatus. It is a member of the CATCHR (complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods) family, which includes components of the exocyst, COG, GARP, and DSL1 complexes, sharing structural and functional features: the alpha-helical bundles at the middle/C-terminal and a N-terminal coiled-coil region [[PMID:28098232],[PMID:30290151]]. This is the N-terminal coiled-coil of COG4.

Name: Centromere/kinetochore protein zw10, middle domain
Description: Zw10 protein, in the context of the conserved Rod-Zwilch-Zw10 (RZZ) complex, is required for correct metaphase check-pointing during mitosis [[PMID:11146659],[PMID:11146660]] and it is also important in vesicle transport between Golgi and ER as part of a second complex together with the conserved conserved RINT1/Tip20p and NAG/Sec39p proteins [[PMID:22685323]]. Zw10 is the metazoan homologue of yeast Dsl1, and are included in the complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods (CATCHR) family, sharing structural features [[PMID:22685323],[PMID:34061181]]. This entry represents the middle domain of zw10, which, from AlphaFold prediction, consists of four beta-strands and four alpha-helices.

Name: Centromere/kinetochore protein zw10, C-terminal
Description: Zw10 protein, in the context of the conserved Rod-Zwilch-Zw10 (RZZ) complex, is required for correct metaphase check-pointing during mitosis [[PMID:34061181],[PMID:11146659]] and it is also important in vesicle transport between Golgi and ER as part of a second complex together with the conserved conserved RINT1/Tip20p and NAG/Sec39p proteins [[PMID:11146660]]. Zw10 is the metazoan homologue of yeast Dsl1, both included in the complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods (CATCHR) family, sharing structural features [[PMID:11146660],[PMID:22685323]]. This entry represents the C-terminal domain of zw10, consisting of the conserved alpha-helical bundle.

Name: Exocyst complex component Sec10, N-terminal
Description: This entry contains the Sec10 component (approximately 650 residues long) of the eukaryotic exocyst complex, which mediates the tethering of post-Golgi secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane and promotes the assembly of the SNARE complex for membrane fusion. It is also involved in cell polarisation, primary ciliogenesis, cytokinesis, pathogen invasion, tumourigenesis and metastasis [[PMID:12665531],[PMID:28098232]]. The exocyst is a member of the Complex Associated with Tethering Containing Helical Rods (CATCHRs) which also includes DSL1, COG and GARP complexes, evolutionary related. They share structural features comprising alpha-helical bundles towards the C-terminal and coiled-coil regions at the N-terminal. This entry represents the N-terminal coiled-coil region (also referred to as CorEx motif) of Sec10 and its homologues from animals ECOC5 (Exocyst complex component 5), which participates in the central pair-wise interactions by forming an intact antiparallel 'zipper' which Sec15 subunit. Sec10-Sec15 CorEx motifs interact with Exo70-Exo84 dimers and form a four-helical bundle [[PMID:29335562]].

Name: Exocyst complex component Exo70 N-terminal
Description: The Exo70 protein is a subunit of the exocyst complex [[PMID:16359701],[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181],[PMID:10207081],[PMID:17717527]], first identified in yeast, which is evolutionarily conserved in eukaryotes. It mediates the tethering of post-Golgi secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane and promotes the assembly of the SNARE complex for membrane fusion. It also plays a role in cell polarisation, primary ciliogenesis, cytokinesis, pathogen invasion, tumourigenesis and metastasis [[PMID:29335562]]. It is a member of the the Complex Associated with Tethering Containing Helical Rods (CATCHRs) family which also includes Conserved Oligomeric Golgi complex (COG) and Golgi-Associated Retrograde Protein complex (GARP) and DSL1 complexes, all evolutionarily related and share structural features consisting of alpha-helical bundles at the C-terminus and coiled-coil region at the N-terminus [[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. Exo70 interacts with the Rho3 GTPase and phospholipids [[PMID:10207081],[PMID:17717527]]. This entry represents the N-terminal coiled-coil region which participates in the central pair-wise interactions by forming an antiparallel 'zipper' with Exo84 subunit. Exo70-Exo84 'zipper' interacts with Sec10-Sec15 counterpart to form a four-helical bundle [[PMID:29335562]].

Name: Conserved oligomeric Golgi complex subunit 3, C-terminal
Description: This entry includes the Conserved oligomeric Golgi complex subunit 3 (COG3, also known as Sec34) which is a component of the peripheral membrane COG complex, involved in intra-Golgi protein trafficking. [[PMID:11703943],[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. COG is a member of the complexes associated with tethering containing helical rods (CATCHR) family which also includes the exocyst, GARP and DSL1 complexes, which are evolutionarily related and share structural features consisting of alpha-helical bundles towards the C-terminal and an N-terminal coiled-coil region [[PMID:29335562],[PMID:34061181]]. This entry represents the alpha-helical bundles of COG3.

Name: Maintenance of Photosystem II under High light 2
Description: This is a family of chloroplast thylakoid lumenal proteins, including Maintenance of Photosystem II under High light 2 (also known as Thylakoid lumenal 16.5 kDa protein, [swissprot:O22773]) encoded by At4g02530 in Arabidopsis thaliana. MPH2 is required for the adaptation of this organism to photoinhibitory light and fluctuating light environments [[PMID:28874535]].

Name: Novel STAND NTPase 2
Description: This is an inactive STAND-NTPase superfamily domain found in a mobile two-gene bacterial conflict systems. nSTAND2 is fused to various effector molecules. The neighbouring gene is an active STAND domain. These STAND NTPases define a novel family close to CR-ATPases, the mitochondrial apoptotic ribosomal protein S29/Dap3 and the mitochondria-associated STAND NTPase RNA12 [[PMID:34061031]].

Name: Cellophane-induced 1 (cin1) repeat
Description: Venturia inaequalis is a hemi-biotrophic fungus that causes scab disease of apple. A recently-identified gene from this fungus, cin1 (cellophane-induced 1), is up-regulated over 1000-fold in planta and considerably on cellophane membranes, and encodes a cysteine-rich secreted protein of 523 residues with eight imperfect tandem repeats of ~60 amino acids [[PMID:22771296]].

Name: Tail fibre protein gp37 trimerization region
Description: This entry represents part of the phage tail fibre. In T-even phages, gp37 and gp38 are components of the tail fibre that are critical for phage-host interaction.

Name: Protein M, large region
Description: This domain is found in proteins from Mycoplasmaceae, including Uncharacterized protein MG281 from Mycoplasma genitalium, also known as protein M. This virulence protein consists of two domains. The larger one (this entry) binds the IgG light chain to block the binding of antibody to antigen. It includes a leucine-rich repeat (LRR)-like subdomain, which faces away from the antibody molecule [[PMID:24503852]].

Name: PAN2 N-terminal
Description: The poly(A)-specific nuclease (PAN) is a deadenylating enzyme involved in cytoplasmic mRNA turnover consisting of two subunits, PAN2 and PAN3 that form a conserved complex which acts in the initial trimming of poly(A) tails during deadenylation, followed by the processive activity of CCR4-NOT complex [[PMID:24880344]]. PAN2 has two independent structural units, the C-terminal catalytic unit (including [pfam:PF13423] and [pfam:PF00929]) and the N-terminal assembly unit, represented in this entry, which engages in a bipartite interaction with PAN3 dimers [[PMID:24880343]]. This assembly unit contains a WD40 propeller that contacts the PAN3 C-terminal domain and it is connected to an adaptable linker which reinforces the asymmetry of the PAN3 dimer preventing the recruitment of a second PAN2 molecule [[PMID:24880343]].

Name: SLS1 protein N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal helical domain found in the yeast SLS1 protein.  SLS1 is a fungal protein found bound to the mitochondrial inner-membrane [[PMID:8917313]]. It reacts physically with fungal Kar2p to promote translocation across the endoplasmic-reticulum membrane. This action appeared to be mediated via the promotion of the Sec63p-mediated activation of Kar2p's ATPase activity. This indicates that the Sls1p protein is a GrpE-like protein in the endoplasmic reticulum. In S.cerevisiae the SLS1 gene (ScSLS1) is not essential but is also involved in ERAD and folding [[PMID:11805046],[PMID:10958688]].

Name: Msx2-interacting protein, RAM7 domain
Description: This is the RBP-J binding domain (RAM7) found in the central region of  Msx2-interacting protein (MINT), a widely expressed transcription repressor that regulates key transcription effectors in several signalling pathways [[PMID:16583136]], including MSX2, RBP-J, and nuclear receptors. Structure predictions from AlphaFold suggest that this domain adopts a beta-solenoid structure which includes VNVLTGP/VNVLTTP repeats. RAM7 domain from MINT competes with the RAM domain of Notch for physical interaction with RBP-J [[PMID:20484411]]. Through this domain, MINT sequesters and prevents RBP-J DNA binding thus, precluding trans-activation directed by the RBP-J coadapter, Notch1 intracellular domain [[PMID:20484411]].

Name: Type III effector Xcv3220, C-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the C-terminal end of the type III effector Xcv3220 (XopL) from the plant pathogen Xanthomonas campestris and similar proteins from Xanthomonadales. XopL induces plant cell death, subverts plant immunity and shows E3 ubiquitin ligase activity, which is associated with this domain. This domain interacts with plant E2 ubiquitin conjugating enzymes and folds into a four-helix bundle, which can be subdivided into two uneven lobes almost perpendicular to each other giving the molecule an 'L' shape. A large cleft with a net negative charge is formed at the intersection of the two lobes [[PMID:23359647]].

Name: Pre-mRNA cleavage complex 2 protein Pcf11, RFEGP repeat
Description: This repeat is found in Pre-mRNA cleavage complex 2 protein Pcf11 from human ([swissprot:O94913]) and similar proteins from vertebrates. This repeat is about thirteen amino acids with a high content of proline and glycine residues, however, it varies in length among the sequences included in this entry. These repeats contain the highly conserved RFE/DG motif, conserved in PCF11 orthologues in vertebrates but not in Drosophila, C. elegans, or yeast [[PMID:30139799]]. It has been described that human PCF11 has 30 repeats of the RFE/DG motif (referred as FEGP in [[PMID:30139799]]) which are necessary for pre-mRNA cleavage complex II (CFII) activity [[PMID:30139799]]. PCF11 is a subunit and an essential component of CFII (the orthologue of cleavage and polyadenylation factor IA (CFIA) from yeast) which is involved in pre-mRNA 3' end processing and in transcription termination [[PMID:21993299]]. PCF11 is thought to be one of the scaffold proteins of the processing complex. PCF11-mediated termination is essential for vertebrate development [[PMID:30819644]].

Name: WKTW repeat
Description: This repeat is found in five copies at the C-terminal of Cerebellar degeneration-related antigen 1 (CDR1) from human. It has the conserved sequence motif WKTWI.

Name: LED repeat
Description: This entry represents a six residue tandem repeat found in Cerebellar degeneration-related antigen 1 (CDR1) from human [[PMID:3474619]]. It is rich in Glu and Asp and usually flanked on the N-terminal by hydrophobic residues (Leu/Phe) and on the C-terminal by an aliphatic residue followed by and Asp residue [[PMID:3474619]].

Name: UVSSA N-terminal domain
Description: This alpha solenoid repeat domain is found at the N-terminus of the UV-stimulated scaffold protein A.

Name: Testis-expressed protein 13, MVPLGDSxSHS repeat
Description: This entry represents a repeat found in TEX13 (swiss:A0A0J9YWL9) from human and similar proteins from mammals. These repeats have the conserved pattern MVPLGDSxSHS.

Name: FAM186A, PQQAQ repeat
Description: This entry represents a short repeat found in protein FAM186A from human and related sequences from mammals. These repeats form a long alpha-solenoid structure, in which each repeat forms a single alpha- helix, characterised by PQQAQ sequence.

Name: Nuclear pore complex-interacting protein B13-like, MII repeat
Description: This entry represents the MII repeat found in Nuclear pore complex-interacting protein family member B13 (NPB13), NPB12, NPB11, NPB3, NPB4 and NPB5. These repeats may arrange in a beta-solenoid structure. Members of this entry have the repeated sequence MIISRHLPSV.

Name: Small heat shock protein Tsp36, N-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the N-terminal end of Small heat shock protein Tsp36 from the parasitic flatworm Taenia saginata and similar proteins from worms. Tsp36 is organised into two alpha-crystallin domains, two repeat segments on the N-terminal side, a linker region, and a long N-terminal segment of about 80 residues (this entry). This domain consists of a series of helices with sharp turns between them marked by prolines [[PMID:16165157]].

Name: Pasteurella multocida toxin, C2 domain
Description: Pasteurella multocida toxin (PMT) inhibits osteoblastic differentiation in mammals and birds. Its N-terminal region binds to target cells, while its C-terminal region, with a Trojan horse-like shape, carries the intracellularly active moiety. The latter consists of three distinct domains: C1 ([pfam:PF11647]), C2 and C3. This entry represents the larger of these domains, the central region C2, which has 18 helices and nine beta-strands and is organised into two subdomains with typical alpha-beta structures characteristic of nucleotide-binding proteins [[PMID:17360394]]. This domain is also found in the Ras/Rap1-specific endopeptidase (RRSP), an effector domain from the multifunctional-autoprocessing repeats-in- toxin (MARTX) toxin from Vibrio vulnificus responsible for the disruption of host innate immune defenses during infection [[PMID:30279169],[PMID:30282804]].

Name: TAR DNA-binding protein 43, C-terminal
Description: TDP-43 is a member of the heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein (hnRNP) family, which is involved in RNA processing and in stress granule response. It is linked to the pathogenesis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD). It is the main component of intraneuronal inclusion bodies (IBs), a pathological hallmark of ALS. This entry represents the C-terminal Gly-rich region of TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43) [[PMID:22235134],[PMID:34388383],[PMID:35185458],[PMID:26735904]], which is an intrinsically disordered prion-like domain, capable of assemble into dynamic oligomers rich in beta-sheet structures, being responsible of TDP-43 aggregation [[PMID:34388383]].

Name: DWD hypersensitive to UV-B 1, N-terminal
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of DWD hypersensitive to UV-B 1 from Arabidopsis thaliana, which may act as a substrate receptor of a CUL4-RING E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase (CRL4) complex involved in the negative regulation of cellular responses to ultraviolet-B (UV-B) illumination, likely in coordination with RUP1 [[PMID:25193399],[PMID:28735869]]. It interacts with COP1 and probably prevents the formation of active UVR8-COP1 complex thus avoiding UVR8-COP1-mediated positive regulation of UV-B responses [[PMID:28735869]]. DHU1 consists of two distant domains, LRR included in this entry and WD40 ([pfam:PF00400]), which mediate protein-protein interactions.

Name: Anamorsin, N-terminal
Description: Anamorsin (also named CIAPIN1 for cytokine-induced anti-apoptosis inhibitor 1), is the human homologue of yeast Dre2, a conserved soluble eukaryotic Fe-S cluster protein, that functions in cytosolic Fe-S protein biogenesis [[PMID:21700214],[PMID:23596212],[PMID:22487307]]. It is found in both the cytoplasm and in the mitochondrial intermembrane space (IMS) [[PMID:18625724]]. Anamorsin is found to be up-regulated in hepatocellular cancer,  is considered to be a downstream effector of the receptor tyrosine kinase-Ras signalling pathway, and is essential in mouse definitive haematopoiesis [[PMID:18299278]]. In addition, it mediates the anti-apoptotic effects of various cytokines [[PMID:14970183]]. This entry represents the N-terminal domain, which has a S-adenosylmethionine methyltransferase-like fold.

Name: Huntingtin, bridge
Description: HTT is expressed in many tissues, with various expression levels and is involved in a number of cellular functions. However, its exact function is not yet clear. An an abnormal polyglutamine (polyQ) expansion in this protein causes Huntington's disease (HD) a progressive autosomal-dominant neurodegenerative disease characterised by an uncontrolled motor movement known as chorea, cognitive disorder and depression [[PMID:29466333]]. HTT consists of multiple HEAT repeats organised in N-terminal, bridge (represented in this entry) and C-terminal domains [[PMID:29466333]], which function as a scaffold that mediates interactions with binding partners. This domain connects the N- and C-HEAT domains.

Name: Huntingtin, N-terminal HEAT 1
Description: This entry represents the first region of HEAT repeats from Huntingtin (Htt), which is part of the N-terminal HEAT domain [[PMID:32668197]]. It is separated from the rest of the domain ([pfam:PF12372]) by an unstructured region. Htt is expressed in many tissues with various expression levels and is involved in a number of cellular functions. However, its exact function is not yet clear. An abnormal polyglutamine (polyQ) expansion between 1-90aa at the very N-terminal causes Huntington's disease (HD), a progressive autosomal-dominant neurodegenerative disease characterised by an uncontrolled motor movement known as chorea, cognitive disorder and depression [[PMID:29466333]]. Htt consists of multiple HEAT repeats organised in N-terminal, bridge and C-terminal domains [[PMID:29466333]], which function as a scaffold that mediated interactions with binding partners.

Name: Huntingtin, C-terminal HEAT
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Huntingtin (Htt), which consists of HEAT repeats. Htt is expressed in many tissues with various expression levels and is involved in a number of cellular functions. However, its exact function is not yet clear. An an abnormal polyglutamine (polyQ) expansion in this protein causes Huntington's disease (HD) a progressive autosomal-dominant neurodegenerative disease characterised by an uncontrolled motor movement known as chorea, cognitive disorder and depression [[PMID:29466333]]. HTT consists of multiple HEAT repeats organised in N-terminal ([pfam:PF20926] and [pfam:PF12372]), bridge ([pfam:PF20925]) and C-terminal domains [[PMID:29466333]], which function as a scaffold that mediated interactions with binding partners. It has been suggested that the polyQ expansion in Htt alters phosphorylation status along the protein which affects its structure and function. In particular, modifications of phosphorylation in this domain induce conformational changes that impair its movement and interaction with the N-HEAT domain [[PMID:32668197]].

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF6847)
Description: This family represents bacterial uncharacterised proteins such as Uncharacterized conserved protein Dip1984 from Corynebacterium diphtheriae, which shows an all-alpha structure. Some members of this family are annotated as septicolysin, such as [swissprot:H6NMB2], a cholesterol-dependent pore-forming cytolysin that acts to facilitate the infection of certain pathogenic gram-positive bacteria.

Name: Acanthaporin
Description: Acanthaporin is a pore-forming toxin from acanthamoebae, an opportunistic pathogen. This protein is cytotoxic for neuronal cells in humans and shows antimicrobial activity against diverse bacterial strains by permeabilising their membranes. It folds into a disulfide-bonded four-alpha-helix monomeric structure that dimerises at pH 8.0 due to the deprotonation of some histidine residues, which leads to a conformational change [[PMID:23143413]].

Name: ExoU toxin, C-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found in the toxin ExoU from Pseudomonas aeruginosa, an effector of the type III secretion system of the bacteria. ExoU is a patatin-like phospholipase that binds to the host membranes and induces cytotoxic effects leading to rapid necrotic cell death. This protein shows four domains: a putative chaperone binding domain. a patatin-like domain ([pfam:PF01734]), and two domains that form the membrane localization domain (MLD). This entry represents the C-terminal domain, which folds into a four-helical bundle with a conserved arginine exposed. This residue seems to be essential for localization [[PMID:23166655],[PMID:22496657],[PMID:25505182]].

Name: Nucleolar protein 11 C-terminal domain
Description: This alpha-solenoid repeat in found at the C-terminus of nucleolar protein 11.

Name: Suprabasin, GxHH repeat
Description: Suprabasin (SBSN) is involved in epidermal differentiation and may play a role in skin barrier formation. It has been proposed to be a component of the cornified envelope, an essential structure for skin protection [[PMID:34322491],[PMID:33477529]]. Its exact function is not yet clear; it has been associated with multiple diseases such as cancer and autoimmune disorders [[PMID:34322491],[PMID:33477529],[PMID:35222534]]. It is a secreted molecule that may induce cellular signalling (AKT, WNT/beta-catenin, and/or p38MAPK signalling) and processes (migration, proliferation, neovascularization apoptosis- and immune-resistance). It contains a repeated sequence GxHH, with no other similar sequence in the human proteome, which suggests an evolutionarily unique and conserved role [[PMID:33477529]]. It has also been described a FG (Phe-Gly) motif, which is common in scaffold proteins (keratin 1/2/10 and fillagrin-2 in addition to SBSN) [[PMID:34322491]].

Name: LOC400499 family
Description: This entry represents a human protein of unknown function. It has a remarkable high confidence structure prediction showing an extended single beta sheet composed of a single long meander. No obvious sequence repeat is present in the sequence. Some members of this family contain lipovitellin homologous regions additionally to the N-terminus of this family. Lipovitellin contains similar open beta structures which are used to bind to lipids suggesting that this family is also involved in lipid binding and storage.

Name: Spectrin-like repeat
Description: This entry represents a spectrin repeat found in the plectin protein [[PMID:17397861]].

Name: Regulator of MON1-CCZ1 complex, N-terminal
Description: This is the N-terminal beta-propeller of Regulator of MON1-CCZ1 complex (RMC1). RMC1 is a component of the the CCZ1-MON1 complex, which functions as a guanine exchange factor (GEF) for RAB7 during endocytic and autophagic flux to the lysosome [[PMID:29038162]]. This region may function analogously to the WIPI proteins to bind phosphoinositides at the membrane [[PMID:29038162]].

Name: WD repeat-containing protein 93
Description: This entry represents the beta-propeller of WD repeat-containing protein 93 (WDR93). Its function is unknown.

Name: WDR54
Description: WDR54 has a regulatory effect on ERK signaling pathway activity in response to EGF stimulation, when is cross-linked to form dimers and trimers. It colocalizes with the EGF receptor in a WDR54-specific vesicle where it sustains the internalisation and controls the degradation of the EGF receptor after EGF stimulation [[PMID:30458214]].

Name: PROPPIN
Description: This entry includes PROPPIN family members, which are beta-propellers that bind polyphosphoinositides, conserved from yeast to human. These are PI(3)P sensors that share a beta-propeller fold and the presence of an FRRG motif required for phosphoinositide binding and autophagy, playing a key role in autophagosome biogenesis. Four PROPPINs are known in human (WIPI1-4) and three in yeast (Atg18, Atg21 and Hvs2) [[PMID:23088497],[PMID:28561066],[PMID:22704557],[PMID:31271352],[PMID:30797857]]. EPG6 from C. elegans is an homologue of human WIPI4 [[PMID:30102152]].

Name: Regulator of microtubule dynamics protein 1-3
Description: This is the C-terminal domain of Regulator of microtubule dynamics proteins 1-3 (RMD1-3) which consists of alpha-helical repeats. These are microtubule-binding proteins. RMD3 is involved in cellular calcium homeostasis regulation, and it may participate in differentiation and apoptosis of keratinocytes [[PMID:22131369]]. RMD1 from C. elegans functions in chromosome segregation [[PMID:18070910]].

Name: BCAS3, WD40 repeat
Description: BCAS3 microtubule associated cell migration factor (BCAS3, also known as Rhudira in mice) is involved in angiogenesis and metastasis [[PMID:33499712],[PMID:22300583]]. It participates in the regulation of cell polarity and directional endothelial cell migration by mediating both the activation and recruitment of CDC42 and and the reorganisation of the actin cytoskeleton at the cell leading edge [[PMID:22300583]]. BCAS3 plays a regulatory role in autophagic activity; it forms a complex with PHAF1 (C16orf70) which associates with the preautophagosomal structure during both non-selective and selective autophagy [[PMID:33499712]]. This is the WD40 repeat domain of BCAS3, which directly binds phosphatidylinositol-3-phosphate [[PMID:33499712]]. This domain is also found in ATG18-like proteins from Arabidopsis.

Name: Coiled-coil domain-containing protein 138, C-terminal domain
Description: This is the C-terminal domain of coiled-coil domain-containing protein 138 (CCDC138), which adopts an alpha-solenoid structure. CCDC138 function is not yet clear. However, it has been localised to basal bodies, suggesting to play a role in centrosomal and ciliary biology [[PMID:28596423]].

Name: Centrosomal protein CEP104, N-terminal domain
Description: Centrosomal protein CEP104 localises to the distal end of centrioles that interacts through its N- and C-terminal region, respectively, with the distal end proteins Cep97 and CP110 [[PMID:28017521]]. It is required for ciliogenesis and for structural integrity at the ciliary tip, being involved in regulating microtubule length in cilia. This is a multidomain protein that consists of an N-terminal all-beta domain, a central all-alpha-helical HEAT repeat domain and a C-terminal array of four zinc fingers domain [[PMID:28017521]]. This is the N-terminal all-beta domain of CEP104, which is structurally similar to domains of intraflagellar transport protein 25 (IFT25), DNA repair protein XRCC1 ([pfam:PF01834]) and anaphase-promoting complex APC subunit 10 [[PMID:28017521]] and is implicated in Cep97 binding.

Name: Centrosomal protein CEP104, ZnF
Description: Centrosomal protein CEP104 localises to the distal end of centrioles that interacts through its N- and C-terminal region, respectively, with the distal end proteins Cep97 and CP110 [[PMID:28017521]]. It is required for ciliogenesis and for structural integrity at the ciliary tip, being involved in regulating microtubule length in cilia. This is a multidomain protein that consists of an N-terminal all-beta domain, a central all-alpha-helical HEAT repeat domain and a C-terminal array of four zinc finger domains [[PMID:28017521]], the latter represented in this entry. This ZnF domain interacts with kinase Nek1 which competes with CP110 [[PMID:28017521]].

Name: Centrosomal protein CEP104, TOG domain
Description: Centrosomal protein CEP104 localises to the distal end of centrioles that interacts through its N- and C-terminal region, respectively, with the distal end proteins Cep97 and CP110 [[PMID:28017521]]. It is required for ciliogenesis and for structural integrity at the ciliary tip, being involved in regulating microtubule length in cilia. This is a multidomain protein that consists of an N-terminal all-beta domain, a central all-alpha-helical TOG domain and a C-terminal array of four zinc fingers domain [[PMID:28017521]]. This entry represents the central tubulin-binding TOG domain, consisting of HEAT repeats [[PMID:28017521],[PMID:27402853]].

Name: XMAP215/Dis1/CLASP, TOG domain
Description: This is the tubulin-binding TOG domain of two related families of microtubules (MTs) regulatory proteins, XMAP215/Dis1 and Cytoplasmic Linker ASsociated Proteins CLASP [[PMID:21782439],[PMID:27156448],[PMID:33591274]]. XMAP215/Dis1 family includes Stu2 from S. cerevisiae, S. pombe Alp14 and Dis1, A. thaliana MOR1, C. elegans Zyg9, D. melanogaster MiniSpindles (MSPS) and human ch-TOG (also known as Cytoskeleton-associated protein 5), which have conserved roles in promoting the assembly of microtubules [[PMID:21782439]]. CLASP proteins promote MT rescue and suppress MT catastrophe events [[PMID:21782439],[PMID:27156448]].

Name: CIP2A, N-terminal
Description: Cancerous inhibitor of PP2A (CIP2A) functions via protein binding through interactions with many other proteins including MYC, polo like kinase (PLK1) and NIMA protein. t is an inhibitor of PP2A, a tumour suppressor. CIP2A binds to PP2A and inhibits its phosphatase functions resulting in tumourigenesis transformation of cells [[PMID:25015035],[PMID:28174209]]. This is the N-terminal HEAT repeats domain, which can be subdivided into tip, stem and C-dimerisation subdomains; the latter is responsible for CIP2A dimerisation [[PMID:28174209]].

Name: Integrator subunit 10
Description: Integrator complex subunit 10 is a component of the multiprotein complex Integrator, which is responsible for the endonucleolytic cleavage of small nuclear RNA (snRNA) transcripts within the nucleus [[PMID:22252320],[PMID:22250197]]. INT10 adopts an armadillo-type fold.

Name: Maestro, HEAT repeats
Description: This entry represents HEAT repeats from maestro heat-like repeat-containing proteins. Maestro (Male-specific Transcription in the developing Reproductive Organs (MRO)) is expressed in human granulosa cumulus cells and in testicular germ cells [[PMID:28406912]]. SPIF (also known as MRO2B) may play a role in the process of sperm capacitation [[PMID:27105888]].

Name: Cilia- and flagella-associated protein 69, ARM repeat
Description: Cilia- and flagella-associated protein 69 is required for sperm flagellum assembly/stability. Truncating mutations of this protein induce autosomal-recessive MMAF (multiple morphological abnormalities of the flagella) and primary male infertility [[PMID:29606301]]. In mice, it regulates the speed of activation and termination of the odour response and, thus, contributes to the robustness of olfactory transduction pathways [[PMID:28495971]]. This entry represents the ARM-repeats domain.

Name: LisH domain-containing protein ARMC9, ARM repeats
Description: ARMC9 is localised to the basal body of a cilium and is upregulated during ciliogenesis [[PMID:32453716],[PMID:28625504]]. It is required for appropriate acetylation and polyglutamylation of ciliary microtubules, and regulation of cilium length [[PMID:32453716]]. This entry represents the armadillo repeats found towards the C-terminal ARMC9 [[PMID:32453716]].

Name: EFR3, armadillo repeat
Description: EFR3 protein form a complex with Ypp1/TTC7 which is required to localise phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase (PI4K) to the plasma membrane. EFR3 acts as a regulator of the Stt4p phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase at contact sites between the endoplasmic reticulum and the plasma membrane [[PMID:24360784],[PMID:25380825],[PMID:25608530]]. EFR3 consists of armadillo repeats at its N-terminal, which act as a scaffold and a basic patch binds acidic phospholipids that anchors the complex to the plasma membrane [[PMID:24360784]].

Name: Biogenesis factor required for ATP synthase 1, C-terminal domain
Description: This domain is predominantly found at the C-terminal end of proteins from cyanobacteria and plants, including Biogenesis factor required for ATP synthase 1 (BFA1, [swissprot:F4J1U2]) from Arabidopsis thaliana. BFA1 promotes the assembly of the CF1 subcomplex of the chloroplast ATP synthase by specifically interacting with the gamma and beta subunits. This domain consists of ten beta-sheets and one alpha- helix forming a typical beta-barrel, which locates perpendicularly to [pfam:PF12204] [[PMID:30012777]].

Name: ZSWIM4-8, C-terminal
Description: This entry represents an alpha-solenoid structure found at the C-terminal of Zinc finger ZSWIM domain-containing proteins 4-8 (ZSWIM4-8). ZSWIM8 is a substrate recognition component of a SCF-like E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase complex that promotes target-directed microRNA degradation (TDMD), a process that mediates degradation of microRNAs (miRNAs) [[PMID:33184237],[PMID:33184234]]. ZSWIM4 has been associated with JAK2 inhibition resistance in breast cancer [[PMID:34102191]]. ZSWIM5 was reported to suppress the invasion and migration of lung cancer cells by regulating the expression of cyclins and MMPs and may constitute a target for the diagnosis and therapy of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) [[PMID:30233241]]. ZSWIM6 plays a key role in neuronal development and  mutations in this protein causes acromelic frontonasal dysostosis (AFND), an autosomal-dominant severe frontonasal and limb malformation syndrome [[PMID:29198722],[PMID:28433741]]. In ZSWIM6, this is termed the Sin3-like domain, in which a recurrent de novo missense variant causes AFND [[PMID:29198722]].

Name: Stereocilin
Description: Stereocilin is expressed in the sensory hair cells and is associated with the stereocilia, the stiff microvilli forming the structure for mechanoreception of sound stimulation [[PMID:31776257],[PMID:11687802],[PMID:21165971]]. It is essential for the formation of horizontal top connectors in hair cells, which leads to deafness when it is defective [[PMID:11687802],[PMID:21165971]]. This is a large secreted protein containing a large leucine-rich region [[PMID:31776257]].

Name: MvdD pre-ATP grasp domain
Description: The pair of ATP-grasp proteins MvdD and MvdC (microviridin D and C), as well as an acetyltransferase, produce microviridin K, an example of a RiPP (ribosomally synthesized and posttranslationally modified peptide). Microviridins are peptidase inhibitors. This domain represents the pre-ATP grap domain.

Name: LARP1 HEAT repeat region
Description: This entry represents the HEAT repeat region of the LARP1 protein [[PMID:26206669],[PMID:31676287]]. La-related protein 1 (LARP1) regulates the stability of many mRNAs. These include 5'TOPs, mTOR-kinase responsive mRNAs with pyrimidine-rich 5' UTRs, which encode ribosomal proteins and translation factors [[PMID:26206669]]. The repeats within this protein have been called DM15 repeats.

Name: EFR3 protein
Description: This entry represents the HEAT-like repeat region from the EFR3 protein [[PMID:24360784]]. Protein EFR3 homologue A (EFR3A) is a component of a phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase (PI4K) complex, composed of PI4KA, EFR3 (EFR3A or EFR3B), TTC7 (TTC7A or TTC7B) and FAM126 (FAM126A or FAM126B). This complex is required for the attachment of phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase (PI4K) to the plasma membrane. EFR3A is palmitoylated at its N terminus, anchoring the protein to the plasma membrane, and probably acts as the membrane-anchoring component in the complex. In yeast, the Efr3p protein is a main regulator of the Stt4p phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase at contact sites between the endoplasmic reticulum and the plasma membrane.

Name: Nucleoporin Nup188, N-terminal subdomain III
Description: Nup188 one of the many peptides that make up the nucleoporin complex (NPC), and is found across eukaryotes [[PMID:11029043],[PMID:16361228],[PMID:23795296]]. The Nup188 subcomplex (Nic96p-Nup188p-Nup192p-Pom152p) is one of the at least six that make up the NPC, and as such is symmetrically localised on both faces of the NPC at the nuclear end, being integrally bound to the C-terminus of Pom34p [[PMID:16361228]]. This protein folds into an extended stack of helices where the N-terminal forms a closed ring (subdivided into subdomains I-III in [[PMID:23795296]]) and the C-terminal forms a more regular, superhelical structure [[PMID:23795296]]. This entry represents the helical segment of subdomain III described in [[PMID:23795296]], which consists of an array of HEAT and ARM repeats with an inserted SH3-like domain [[PMID:23795296]].

Name: Nup188 SH3-like domain
Description: Nup188 is one of the many peptides that make up the nucleoporin complex (NPC), and is found across eukaryotes [[PMID:11029043],[PMID:23795296]]. The Nup188 subcomplex (Nic96p-Nup188p-Nup192p-Pom152p) is one of at least six that make up the NPC, and as such is symmetrically localised on both faces of the NPC at the nuclear end, being integrally bound to the C terminus of Pom34p [[PMID:16361228]]. This protein folds into an extended stack of helices where the N-terminal forms a closed ring (subdivided into subdomains I-III in [[PMID:23795296]]) and the C-terminal forms a more regular, superhelical structure [[PMID:23795296]]. This entry represents a beta-stranded SH3-like domain inserted in subdomain III from Nup188 N-terminal region, which is a circular permutation from canonical SH3 domain [[PMID:23795296]].

Name: Spectrin repeat
Description: This entry represents a spectrin repeat found in a variety of plectin and related proteins.

Name: YqgU-like 6-bladed beta propeller domain
Description: This entry represents a beta propeller domain found in the B. subtilis YqgU protein. The proteins in this family contain an N-terminal lipid attachment motif suggesting they are lipoproteins.

Name: STPRs (score and three amino acid peptide repeats)
Description: The silkgland-specific transcription factor FMBP-1 from the silkworm Bombyx mori contains a unique DNA-binding domain of four tandem STPRs, namely the score and three amino acid peptide repeats [[PMID:26939889]]. In the presence of target DNA, STPRs adopts a zig-zag structure of three or four tandem alpha-helices that run along the major groove of DNA [[PMID:26939889]].

Name: CDI toxin restriction endonuclease-like domain
Description: Contact-dependent growth inhibition (CDI) systems encode polymorphic toxin/immunity proteins that mediate competition between neighbouring bacterial cells [[PMID:23236156]]. Despite sharing little sequence identity, the toxin domains are structurally similar and have homology to endonucleases. The EC869 toxin is a Zn(2+)-dependent DNase capable of completely degrading the genomes of target cells [[PMID:23236156]].

Name: Ryanodine receptor junctional solenoid repeat
Description: This entry represents a region of the Ryanodine receptor composed of alpha helical ARM repeats [[PMID:33547325]]. This region has been termed the Junctional solenoid region (Jsol) [[PMID:33547325]].

Name: Nucleoporin Nup49 C-terminal helical region
Description: This entry represents a C-terminal helical region from the Nup49 protein [[PMID:26316600]]. Nup49 is one of three proteins that form the channel nucleoporin heterotrimer (CNT) [[PMID:26316600]].

Name: Flagellar hook-associated protein 1, D2-like domain
Description: This entry includes Flagellar hook-associated protein 1 (FlgK or HAP1), one of three HAPs that form the hook-filament junction and has structural similarity to flagellin. FlgK is required for normal flagellum assembly. Proteins in this entry have a multidomain fold comprising an elongated alpha-helical scaffold core, and depending on the bacterial species, a variable number of smaller domains [[PMID:25645451]]. This entry represents a beta-sandwich domain from FlgK proteins; Salmonella typhimurium FlgK ([swissprot:P0A1J5]) has one beta-sandwich domain, while FlgK from Burkholderia pseudomallei has two of them protruding from the alpha-helical core, referred to as D2 and D3 domains in [[PMID:25645451]], of which the first one, D2, is covered in this entry [[PMID:25645451]]. FlgK has cytotoxic effects suggesting an important role in pathogenesis and is highly immunogenic. Domains D2 and D3 from B. pseudomallei have been identified as epitopes with increased immunogenic potential for vaccine development.

Name: Flagellar hook-associated protein 1, D3 domain
Description: This entry includes Flagellar hook-associated protein 1 (FlgK or HAP1), one of three HAPs that form the hook-filament junction and has structural similarity to flagellin. FlgK has a multidomain fold comprising an elongated alpha-helical scaffold core, and depending on the bacterial species, a variable number of smaller domains [[PMID:25645451]]. FlgK from Burkholderia pseudomallei (the causing agent of melioidosis) has two beta-sandwich domains protruding from the large alpha-helical core, referred to as D2 and D3 domains in [[PMID:25645451]], which the second one (D3) is represented in this entry [[PMID:25645451]]. FlgK has cytotoxic effects suggesting an important role in pathogenesis and is highly immunogenic. Domains D2 and D3 have been identified as epitopes with increased immunogenic potential for vaccine development [[PMID:25645451]].

Name: ETF-QO, ubiquinone-binding
Description: Electron transfer flavoprotein-ubiquinone oxidoreductase (ETF-QO) is a a 4Fe4S flavoprotein located in the inner mitochondrial membrane and catalyses ubiquinone (UQ) reduction. It forms a single structural domain with three functional regions (that bind FAD, the 4Fe4S cluster, and UQ) which are closely packed and share structural elements [[PMID:17050691]]. This domain includes the residues involved in UQ binding [[PMID:17050691]]. This domain is also found in FixC proteins from bacteria, which share similarities with ETF-QO [[PMID:15386115]].

Name: Dumpy, DPY module
Description: This entry represents the DPY repeat module described in dumpy from Drosophila melanogaster, a gigantic extracellular matrix protein that contains epidermal growth factor (EGF) modules interspersed with DPY modules. The DPY module forms a compact fold consisting of a small N-terminal 3-10 helix packed against a double-stranded anti-parallel disulphide-stabilised beta-sheet motif capable of linking end-to-end with EGF modules to form a fibre [[PMID:10837220]].

Name: Fanconi-associated nuclease 1, SAP subdomain
Description: This domain is found in Fanconi-associated nuclease 1 from humans (Fan1) and similar proteins from chordates. Fan1 is involved in the processing of DNA interstrand crosslinks (ICLs) and therefore in the maintenance of genomic stability and prevention of tissue decline in multiple organs. FAN1 consists of an SAP-containing N-terminal domain, a middle tetratricopeptide repeat domain (TPR) and a C-terminal viral replication and repair nuclease domain (VRR_nuc domain, [pfam:PF08774]). This entry represents the SAP subdomain, part of the N-terminal domain. It consists of four alpha-helices [[PMID:25500724],[PMID:25430771],[PMID:25547363]].

Name: Fanconi-associated nuclease 1, TPR domain
Description: This is the TPR domain from Fanconi-associated nuclease 1 (FAN1), which mediates inter-domain interactions and is part of the dimerization interface [[PMID:25500724]]. FAN1 is a DNA structure-specific nuclease involved in processing DNA interstrand crosslinks. FAN1 maintains genomic stability and prevents tissue decline in multiple organs [[PMID:25500724],[PMID:25430771],[PMID:25547363]]. It mediates cancer drug resistance in several cancer subtypes.

Name: PgaA membrane beta barrel domain
Description: The partially de-N-acetylated poly-beta-1,6-N-acetyl-d-glucosamine (dPNAG) polymer serves as an intercellular biofilm adhesin that plays an essential role for the development and maintenance of integrity of biofilms of diverse bacterial species. Translocation of dPNAG across the bacterial outer membrane is mediated by a tetratricopeptide repeat-containing outer membrane protein, PgaA [[PMID:26957546]]. This entry represents the PgaA membrane beta barrel domain [[PMID:26957546]].

Name: Yme1-like, N-terminal
Description: This domain is found at the N-terminal end of a group of proteins predominantly found in fungi, plants and basal metazoans, including Mitochondrial inner membrane i-AAA protease supercomplex subunit YME1 from Saccharomyces cerevisiae. This domain adopts a tetratricopeptide repeat fold [[PMID:25576874]].

Name: PI4KB/PIK1, accessory (PIK) domain
Description: Phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase beta (PI4KB) is one of four mammalian PI4K enzymes that phosphorylate phosphatidylinositol to generate phosphatidylinositol 4-phosphate (PI4P). It localizes at the Golgi and is essential for Golgi formation and function. PI4KB consists of two domains, a right-handed helical solenoid at the N-terminal, also referred to as accessory (PIK) domain, and a C-terminal kinase domain [[PMID:24876499],[PMID:25897704],[PMID:26756197]]. This entry represents the N-terminal PIK domain from mammalian PI4KB and the yeast orthologue Pik1 [[PMID:24876499]], which may be involved in substrate presentation.

Name: Ubiquitin carboxyl-terminal hydrolase 38-like, N-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the N-terminal end of Ubiquitin carboxyl- terminal hydrolase 38 from humans and similar proteins found in vertebrates. This domain forms ARM repeats. It is often associated to [pfam:PF00443].

Name: Small ribosomal subunit protein uS5m, N-terminal
Description: This entry represents an extension found at the N-terminal of the mitochondrial 28S Small ribosomal subunit protein uS5m [[PMID:24799711],[PMID:25837512]]. This region lines a part of the mRNA entry path and connects the 28S subunit body to the head forming a latch across the mRNA channel entrance [[PMID:25837512]].

Name: Glycoside hydrolase 120, insertion domain
Description: This insertion domain is found in a group of bacterial glycoside hydrolases from the CAZy family 120, including Parallel beta-helix repeat protein from Thermoanaerobacterium saccharolyticum (also known as xylosidase). It is often found associated to [pfam:PF13229].

Name: HTH-type transcriptional regulator Rgg, C-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the C-terminal end of HTH-type transcriptional regulator Rgg from Streptococcus gordonii, and similar proteins predominantly found in Firmicutes. Rgg is a peptide pheromone receptor that directly binds pheromones transported to the cytosol. The large C-terminal repeat domain contains five HTH folds and a capping helix that form a right-handed superhelical structure, resembling a TPR domain superhelix [[PMID:16339309],[PMID:17038121],[PMID:25847993],[PMID:26714274],[PMID:29030429]]. This domain is found associated with the N-terminal [pfam:PF01381].

Name: Enhancer of mRNA-decapping protein 4, C-terminal
Description: Enhancer of mRNA-decapping protein 4 (EDC4, also known as Ge-1), is a regulator of mRNA decapping to function in the mRNA P-bodies within the cytoplasm. It has been shown to interact with mTORC1 [[PMID:25514416]], coenzyme A synthase [[PMID:22982864]] and MARF1 [[PMID:32510323]]. It is also involved in the regulation of immune system [[PMID:17513503]]. This is the C-terminal domain which is necessary and sufficient for P-body targeting and in plants, it also mediates DCP1 and DCP2 interaction, as well as self-association [[PMID:17513503],[PMID:17158604]]. It adopts an helical-repeat fold similar to that of ARM/HEAT repeat proteins.

Name: BIRC2/3-like, UBA domain
Description: Baculoviral IAP repeat-containing proteins, also known as c-IAPs, function as ubiquitin E3 ligases that mediate the ubiquitination of the substrates involved in apoptosis, nuclear factor-kappaB (NF-kappaB) signaling, and oncogenesis [[PMID:19916060],[PMID:22021857],[PMID:20651737]]. This entry    represents the UBA domain of BIRC2/3/4/8 from mammals and BIRC7 from Xenopus tropicalis [[PMID:19916060],[PMID:22021857],[PMID:20651737]].

Name: Apoptotic protease-activating factor 1-like, winged-helix domain
Description: This domain is found in Apoptotic protease-activating factor 1 from Mus musculus (APAF-1), responsible to ignite the downstream cascade of caspases in the mitochondrial pathway of apoptosis. This entry represents the winged-helix domain (WHD) [[PMID:15829969],[PMID:23521171],[PMID:26543158],[PMID:21827944],[PMID:27697150]].

Name: Mitochondrial dynamics protein MID51-like, C-terminal domain
