Additionally, it influences the RNA substrate content and the association of editing complexes with mitoribosomes. The family is part of the REH2-associated complex, interacting with various editing complexes and mitochondrial ribosomes.

Name: Mitochondrial distribution and morphology protein 12
Description: Mitochondrial distribution and morphology protein 12 (Mdm12) is a component of the ERMES/MDM complex (composed of MMM1, MDM10, MDM12 and MDM34), which serves as a molecular tether to connect the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. Mdm12 is required for the interaction of the ER-resident membrane protein Mmm1 and the outer mitochondrial membrane-resident beta-barrel protein Mdm10 [[PMID:17410204]]. The Mdm12-Mmm1 subcomplex functions in the biogenesis of all mitochondrial outer membrane beta-barrel proteins, and acts in a late step after the SAM (sorting and assembly machinery) complex. The Mdm10-Mdm12-Mmm1 subcomplex further acts in the Tom40 (translocase of outer membrane 40)-specific pathway after the action of the Mdm12-Mmm1 complex. Mdm12 is essential for establishing and maintaining the structure of mitochondria and maintenance of mtDNA nucleoids [[PMID:17410204],[PMID:19556461]]. MDM12 structure is similar to the so-called TULIPs (Tubular lipid binding proteins) domain fold, which is characteristic of a superfamily of lipid-binding proteins known as Aha1/BPI domain-like.

Name: Mdm34, N-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found N-terminal in the Mitochondrial distribution and morphology protein 34 and related proteins. Mdm34 is a component of the ERMES/MDM complex, which serves as a molecular tether to connect the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. MDM34 is required for the interaction of the ER-resident membrane protein MMM1 and the outer mitochondrial membrane-resident beta-barrel protein MDM10 [[PMID:11907266],[PMID:14981098],[PMID:19556461]].

Name: Tricorn protease N-terminal beta propeller domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal beta propeller domain of the tricorn proteases. The Tricorn protease family is involved in the degradation of oligopeptides, likely derived from the proteasome. Members of this family function by sequentially breaking down oligopeptides and channelling the resulting products to other proteases, such as F1, F2, and F3, which complete the terminal degradation step to yield free amino acids. The Tricorn protease family is characterized by its assembly into complex structures, such as hexameric toroids, which may further assemble into larger icosahedral supermolecules. This structural organization facilitates the efficient processing of substrates through distinct channels within the protease complex. The family belongs to the peptidase S41B family, indicating a shared evolutionary origin and functional characteristics among its members.

Name: Tricorn protease second beta propeller domain
Description: This entry represents the second beta propeller domain of the tricorn proteases. The Tricorn protease family is involved in the degradation of oligopeptides, likely derived from the proteasome. Members of this family function by sequentially breaking down oligopeptides and channelling the resulting products to other proteases, such as F1, F2, and F3, which complete the terminal degradation step to yield free amino acids. The Tricorn protease family is characterized by its assembly into complex structures, such as hexameric toroids, which may further assemble into larger icosahedral supermolecules. This structural organization facilitates the efficient processing of substrates through distinct channels within the protease complex. The family belongs to the peptidase S41B family, indicating a shared evolutionary origin and functional characteristics among its members.

Name: LETM1-like, C-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the C-terminal end of human Mitochondrial proton/calcium exchanger protein (LETM1) and similar animal proteins. LETM1 plays an important role in maintenance of mitochondrial morphology and in mediating either calcium or potassium/proton antiport [[PMID:18628306],[PMID:19797662],[PMID:24898248],[PMID:24344246],[PMID:29123128],[PMID:32139798],[PMID:36321428]].

Name: Epg5-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in Ectopic P granules protein 5 homolog from Drosophila melanogaster (Epg5) and similar proteins mainly from animals. Epg5 plays a role in late steps of autophagy [[PMID:26917586]].

Name: YLPM1-like, spectrin repeat
Description: This entry represents a spectrin repeat found in human YLP motif-containing protein 1 (YLPM1) and similar animal sequences. YLPM1 plays a role in the reduction of telomerase activity during differentiation of embryonic stem cells by binding to the core promoter of TERT and controlling its down-regulation.

Name: PLL-like beta propeller
Description: This domain, previously known as DUF8189, is found in the L-fucose-binding lectins PHL and PLLs(1-5) from various Photorhabdus species and their homologues. This domain forms a seven-bladed beta-propeller [[PMID:27758853],[PMID:28806750],[PMID:40459235]]. Each blade makes a W-motif composed of four antiparallel beta-strands (beta1-beta4) connected by beta-turns and loops. The C and N terminus are located on the same side of the torus and create a cap of the inner cavity of the torus. With exception of PLL3, which is a monomer, all other domains assemble into oligomeric structures, either homo-dimeric or homo-tetrameric, connected with an inter-subunit disulfide bridge [[PMID:27758853],[PMID:28806750],[PMID:40459235]]. Besides L-fucose or fucosylated saccharides, some of these domains bind to unusual O-methylated disaccharide 3,6-O-Me2-d-Glc-beta1-4(2,3-O-Me2)-l-Rha-alpha (MGMR) [[PMID:40459235]].

Name: Photosystem II assembly protein Psb35
Description: This entry represents Psb35 protein from cyanobacterium Synechocystis sp. PCC 6803 and related proteins. Psb35 is a component of the CP47 antenna module of Photosystem II [[PMID:33258963]]. Psb35 stabilises the CP47 assembly module and associated high-light inducible proteins during the biogenesis of Photosystem II. It is likely involved in the life cycle of cyanobacterial Chl-binding proteins, especially CP47 [[PMID:33258963]].

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF8209)
Description: This entry represents uncharacterised proteins found in gamma- and beta-proteobacteria. A member of this family, STM2901, is a putative cytoplasmic protein from many but not all forms of SPI-1 (Salmonella Pathogenicity Island 1).

Name: Family of unknown function (DUF8210)
Description: This entry represents uncharacterised cyanobacterial proteins that are probably related to Psb35 protein and are restricted to species that have photosystem II.

Name: Mitochondrial ribosome receptor Mrx15
Description: This entry represents Mrx15 (YNR040W), a mitochondrial inner membrane protein that functions as a ribosome receptor in fungi. Mrx15 contains two transmembrane segments and interacts with the large subunit (LSU) of the mitoribosome through its positively charged C-terminal domain, which faces the mitochondrial matrix [[PMID:30336542],[PMID:30091672]]. Together with the peripheral membrane protein Mba1, Mrx15 tethers the mitoribosome to the inner membrane to facilitate cotranslational insertion of mitochondrially encoded proteins. Mrx15 contacts nascent polypeptide chains as they emerge from the ribosomal exit tunnel and ensures proper alignment with the membrane insertase Oxa1. Unlike Mba1, which dissociates from the ribosome to escort Cox2 for downstream assembly, Mrx15 remains stably bound to the mitoribosome throughout translation. Combined deletion of MRX15 and MBA1 causes respiratory deficiency and cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV) defects due to impaired Cox2 biogenesis. Deletion of MRX15 alone confers increased tolerance to proteotoxic stress, suggesting a role in protein quality control. As Mrx15 homologues are restricted to fungi, it may functionally substitute for the permanently bound mL45 ribosomal protein found in mammalian mitoribosomes [[PMID:30336542],[PMID:30091672]].

Name: C triple X like EGF domain
Description: This Cysteine rich repeat was originally found in [pfam:PF02363] but was removed to make this new family. This domain is related to the EGF domain and largely found in insect proteins..

Name: Nucleolar complex protein 4-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found at the N-terminal end of yeast Nucleolar complex protein 4 (NOC4), which is involved in nucleolar processing of pre-18S ribosomal RNA and ribosome assembly. It plays a role in the nuclear export of 40S pre-ribosomal subunit to the cytoplasm [[PMID:12446671],[PMID:15590835],[PMID:16159874]].

Name: DOP1B-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in human DOP1B and similar sequences. DOP1B may play a role in regulating membrane trafficking of cargo proteins. Together with ATP9A and MON2, it regulates SNX3 retromer-mediated endosomal sorting of WLS away from lysosomal degradation [[PMID:30213940]].

Name: RPO41-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found at the N-terminal end of DNA-directed RNA polymerase, mitochondrial from Saccharomyces cerevisiae (RPO41) and similar sequences. RPO41 catalyses the transcription of DNA into RNA using the four ribonucleoside triphosphates as substrates.

Name: Exportin-6, C-terminal TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found at the C-terminal end of human Exportin-6 (XPO6), which mediates the nuclear export of actin and profilin-actin complexes in somatic cells [[PMID:14592989]].

Name: Stalled ribosome sensor GCN1, HEAT repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in human Stalled ribosome sensor GCN1 and similar proteins. GCN1 is a ribosome collision sensor that plays a key role in the RNF14-RNF25 translation quality control pathway, which occurs when a ribosome has stalled during translation, promoting ubiquitination and degradation of translation factors on stalled ribosomes [[PMID:32610081],[PMID:36638793]]. The structure of this protein is predicted to be entirely formed by HEAT repeats.

Name: Beige protein homolog 1, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in yeast Beige protein homolog 1 (BPH1), which may be involved in protein sorting and cell wall formation [[PMID:15296494]].

Name: Cullin-9-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in       human Cullin-9 (CUL9) and similar proteins. CUL9 id a core component of a Cul9-RING ubiquitin-protein ligase complex composed of CUL9 and RBX1 mediates ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of BIRC5 and is required to maintain microtubule dynamics and genome integrity [[PMID:24793696],[PMID:38605244]].

Name: ATPase synthesis protein 25, C-terminal TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found at the C-terminal end of ATPase synthesis protein 25, mitochondrial from Aspergillus fumigatus (Atp25), which is thought to be a mitochondrial mRNA stabilisation factor.

Name: Myb-binding protein 1A-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found towards the C-terminal end of mouse Myb-binding protein 1A (Mybbp1a) and similar proteins. Mybbp1a may activate or repress transcription via interactions with sequence specific DNA-binding proteins [[PMID:9447996],[PMID:11956195],[PMID:14744933]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8241)
Description: This domain is found in uncharacterised plant proteins. It contains three repeats with topology similar to the C-terminal domain of defensin. Each of these repeats has two predicted disulfide bonds that are conserved and occupying the same spatial position as the disulfide bonds in defensin. This domain is frequently found in combination with [pfam:PF23106] and [pfam:PF13920].

Name: Integrator complex subunit 8, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in       human Integrator complex subunit 8 (INTS8) and similar sequences. INTS8 is a component of the integrator complex, a multiprotein complex that terminates RNA polymerase II transcription in the promoter-proximal region of genes [[PMID:34004147],[PMID:37080207],[PMID:33243860],[PMID:38570683],[PMID:28542170]].

Name: UNC79, C-terminal HEAT repeats
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal region of Protein unc-79 homolog from Homo sapiens and related proteins. In the Uncoordinated protein 79 (UNC79) from C.elegans, it spans nearly the entire length. This domain is composed of alpha-helical HEAT repeats. The Protein unc-79 homolog is an auxiliary subunit of the NALCN sodium channel complex which is a voltage-gated ion channel responsible for the resting Na+ permeability that controls neuronal excitability [[PMID:32620897]]. This complex is activated by neuropeptides substance P, neurotensin. In addition, the channel is inhibited by extracellular Ca2+ through the Ca2+-sensing receptor [[PMID:19092807]]. The human the human NALCN channelosome has been structurally characterised [[PMID:34929720]]. NALCN, UNC79 and UNC80 are essential, and mutations in human NALCN and UNC80 cause severe developmental and neurological disease. In the human NALCN channelosome structure UNC79 and UNC80 form an intertwined anti-parallel superhelical assembly, which docks intracellularly onto the NALCN-FAM155A pore-forming subcomplex (PDB:7SX3) [[PMID:34929720]].

Name: PRD1-like, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in       Protein PUTATIVE RECOMBINATION INITIATION DEFECT 1 from Arabidopsis thaliana (PRD1) and similar sequences. PRD1 is involved in DNA cleavage that forms the double-strand breaks (DSB) that initiate meiotic recombination [[PMID:17762870],[PMID:19763177]].

Name: Integrator complex subunit 8, TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in human Integrator complex subunit 8 (INTS8) and similar sequences. INTS8 is a component of the integrator complex, a multiprotein complex that terminates RNA polymerase II transcription in the promoter-proximal region of genes [[PMID:34004147],[PMID:37080207],[PMID:33243860],[PMID:38570683],[PMID:28542170]].

Name: SPM domain
Description: This self-processing module (SPM) domain is found in repeat-in-toxin (RTX) proteins. This domain is involved in an unique, calcium-dependent posttranslational modification known as 'clip-and-link' activity. It involves a rearrangement of the polypeptide backbone through a highly specific Ca2+-dependent autocatalytic cleavage of an Asp-Pro peptide bond and a nonspecific covalent linkage of the released carboxyl of the Asp residue to amino groups of adjacent Lys residues. The cleavage of the Asp-Pro peptide bond is catalyzed by the Ca2+-dependent structural transition of the SPM domain [[PMID:32184239]]. SPM domain adopt an unique fold which is stabilised by binding four calcium ions [[PMID:32184239]].

Name: MIP2 alpha-solenoid domain
Description: This entry represents an all-alpha domain found in MAG2-interacting protein 2 (MIP2) proteins found in plants. MIP2 is essential for the proper maturation of seed storage proteins in plants. It forms a complex on the endoplasmic reticulum with MAG2, ZW10/MIP1, and MIP3. This complex is likely involved in the efficient transport of seed storage proteins, ensuring their correct processing and delivery within the cell. The coordinated action of these proteins is crucial for the development and storage of nutrients in seeds, which are vital for plant reproduction and growth. This domain contains a similar fold to that of Sec39 (PF:PF08314) which is also present in yeast and mammals.

Name: MIP2 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain found in MAG2-interacting protein 2 (MIP2) proteins found in plants. MIP2 is essential for the proper maturation of seed storage proteins in plants. It forms a complex on the endoplasmic reticulum with MAG2, ZW10/MIP1, and MIP3. This complex is likely involved in the efficient transport of seed storage proteins, ensuring their correct processing and delivery within the cell. The coordinated action of these proteins is crucial for the development and storage of nutrients in seeds, which are vital for plant reproduction and growth [[PMID:24118572]]. This domain contains a similar fold to that of Sec39 (PF:PF08314) which is also present in yeast and mammals.

Name: SGR6, HEAT repeats
Description: This is a region of HEAT repeats found in Protein SHOOT GRAVITROPISM 6 from Arabidopsis thaliana (SGR6) and similar proteins. SGR6 is involved in inflorescence stems gravitropism, by modulating vacuolar membrane dynamics in gravity-sensing cells during the amyloplast sedimentation process [[PMID:9210330],[PMID:24486761]].

Name: RBM43, KH-domain type I
Description: This entry represents the type I KH domain in RNA-binding protein 43 (RBM43) mostly found in mammals. RBM43 is induced by inflammatory cytokines and suppresses mitochondrial biogenesis in a PGC1alpha-dependent manner [[PMID:39965564]]. This domain is often found associated with [pfam:PF23085].

Name: Condensin-2 complex subunit D3-like. HEAT repeats
Description: This region of HEAT repeats is found in human Condensin-2 complex subunit D3 (NCAPD3) and similar proteins. NCAPD3 is a regulatory subunit of the condensin-2 complex, a complex which establishes mitotic chromosome architecture and is involved in physical rigidity of the chromatid axis [[PMID:27385818]].

Name: Condensin-2 complex subunit D3-like. HEAT repeats
Description: This region of HEAT repeats is found in human Condensin-2 complex subunit D3 (NCAPD3) and similar proteins. NCAPD3 is a regulatory subunit of the condensin-2 complex, a complex which establishes mitotic chromosome architecture and is involved in physical rigidity of the chromatid axis [[PMID:14532007]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8253)
Description: This domain is found in uncharacterised proteins mainly from Microbacteriaceae. It contains repetitive QC sequence motif frequently found in the context of NQCN motif. This domain is predicted to fold into an unique beta-sandwich formed of these consecutive motifs.

Name: Phage-encoded cationic antimicrobial peptide for lysis
Description: This entry represents the disruptin family, a class of phage lysis proteins required for outer membrane disruption during bacteriophage lysis of Gram-negative bacterial hosts [[PMID:34339297]]. Disruptins are small cationic antimicrobial peptides that function as an alternative to spanins for outer membrane disruption. The prototype member is phiKT gp28, a 56-amino-acid protein with a net charge of +7 and predicted amphipathic helical structure. Unlike spanins, which form periplasm-spanning complexes connecting the inner and outer membranes, disruptins function through their similarity to cationic antimicrobial peptides (CAMPs). The protein localises to both inner and outer membranes and is thought to disrupt the outer membrane by competing for divalent cation binding sites in the lipopolysaccharide layer, thereby weakening the membrane's tensile strength. This makes the outer membrane susceptible to rupture after peptidoglycan degradation by the endolysin. Disruptins are found in approximately 15% of phages that infect Gram-negative bacteria and lack identifiable spanin genes. Synthetic gp28 exhibits antimicrobial activity comparable to the human cathelicidin LL-37 in standard bactericidal assays [[PMID:34339297]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8283)
Description: This domain is found in uncharacterised proteins mainly from Alveolata lineage. It is predicted to fold into an alpha-solenoid with a structural similarity to TAL effector repeats known to bind to double stranded DNA.

Name: Capsule depolymerase
Description: This entry represents a capsule depolymerase domain found in bacteriophage tailspike proteins that specifically degrade the capsular polysaccharides of Klebsiella pneumoniae. These enzymes are essential for phage infection as they enable access to the bacterial cell surface by degrading the protective capsular layer. The domain adopts a right-handed parallel beta-helix fold with the catalytic site located between adjacent chains in the trimeric structure [[PMID:32386574]]. The catalytic mechanism involves four key carboxylate residues (Glu170, Asp229, Glu239, and Asp241) that function through a retaining glycoside hydrolase mechanism. Different members of this family show specificity for different Klebsiella capsular serotypes, including K3, K21, and K47 types [[PMID:32117192],[PMID:30405575]]. These depolymerases have potential therapeutic applications as antivirulence agents, as they can sensitise encapsulated bacteria to complement-mediated killing and enhance phagocytosis by immune cells [[PMID:30405575]]. The domain may be accompanied by carbohydrate binding modules and lectin-like domains that assist in substrate recognition and binding [[PMID:32386574]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8323)
Description: This entry represents a domain of unknown function in a small group of proteins mostly found in animals , particularly nematodes. This domain contains a Spectrin fold with a conserved three-helix bundle structure that is typically arranged in tandem repeats. These repeats confer mechanical flexibility and structural resilience, allowing the proteins to maintain cell shape and organisation. Spectrin-repeat proteins form elongated scaffolds beneath the plasma membrane and contribute to cytoskeletal integrity and membrane stability [[PMID:27698030]]. This domain can be found associated with [pfam:PF00307].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8324)
Description: This entry represents a domain of unknown function in uncharacterised proteins mostly from plants. This domain contains a Spectring fold with a conserved three-helix bundle structure that is typically arranged in tandem repeats. These repeats confer mechanical flexibility and structural resilience, allowing the proteins to maintain cell shape and organisation. Spectrin-repeat proteins form elongated scaffolds beneath the plasma membrane and contribute to cytoskeletal integrity and membrane stability.

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8326)
Description: This entry represents a domain of unknown function in putative Ig-domain-containing proteins mainly from animals. This domain contains a Spectrin fold with a conserved three-helix bundle structure that is typically arranged in tandem repeats. These repeats confer mechanical flexibility and structural resilience, allowing the proteins to maintain cell shape and organisation. Spectrin-repeat proteins form elongated scaffolds beneath the plasma membrane and contribute to cytoskeletal integrity and membrane stability [[PMID:27698030]].

Name: Desmoplakin-like, Spectrin domain
Description: This entry represents a spectrin repeat in Plakin and Cytolinker proteins which play crucial roles in linking cytoskeletal components such as actin, microtubules, and intermediate filaments. These proteins are involved in various cellular processes including stabilisation of microtubules, regulation of focal adhesions, and cell migration. They are essential for maintaining cell structure and integrity, particularly in epithelial cells where they anchor keratin filaments to hemidesmosomes. Proteins also participate in signaling pathways such as Wnt and PKB/AKT, and are important for processes like wound healing, neurite outgrowth, and axonal transport. This entry includes proteins like microtubule-actin cross-linking factors, desmoplakin, envoplakin, and periplakin [[PMID:9412476]]. This domain is associated with [pfam:PF00681].

Name: TamB, N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal region of Translocation and assembly module subunit TamB Escherichia coli and related bacterial proteins. TamB is an integral inner membrane protein that forms part of the translocation and assembly module or TAM, which facilitates the insertion and assembly of specific beta-barrel proteins into the outer membrane [[PMID:22466966],[PMID:39174534],[PMID:29129383]]. TAM is also responsible for the efficient secretion of the adhesin Ag43 in E.coli K-12 [[PMID:22466966]]. This long region is predicted to show a set of tandem beta-sheets. It is also found twice in Uncharacterized protein BU087 from Buchnera aphidicola and related sequences.

Name: F-box-like Leucine-rich domain
Description: This entry represents a Leucine-rich domain in putative F-box proteins and other uncharacterised proteins mainly from animals. Each Leucine Rich Repeat is composed of a beta-alpha unit. These units form elongated non-globular structures. Leucine Rich Repeats are often flanked by cysteine rich domains.

Name: TMOD/LMOD Leucine-rich domain
Description: This entry represents the Leucine-rich repeats domain in Tropomodulin and Leiomodin mainly from animals. Tropomodulin blocks the elongation and depolymerisation of the actin filaments at the pointed end. The Tmod/TM complex contributes to the formation of the short actin protofilament, which in turn defines the geometry of the membrane skeleton. It may play an important role in regulating the organisation of actin filaments by preferentially binding to a specific tropomyosin isoform at its N-terminal. Leiomodin mediates nucleation of actin filaments and thereby promotes actin polymerisation [[PMID:28292896],[PMID:25774500],[PMID:18403713]]. Each Leucine Rich Repeat is composed of a beta-alpha unit. These units form elongated non-globular structures. Leucine Rich Repeats are often flanked by cysteine rich domains.

Name: PRAME C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the leucine-rich domain at the C-terminal in PRAME and similar proteins including LCR14 and OOG3 mainly from animals. PRAME proteins are involved in various cellular processes, primarily through its role as a substrate-recognition component of Cul2-RING E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase complexes. These complexes mediate the ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of target proteins, including truncated selenoproteins. PRAME proteins also function as transcriptional repressors, particularly inhibiting retinoic acid signaling, which affects cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis. Additionally, they are involved in regulating pluripotency in embryonic stem cells and play roles in immune signaling pathways by modulating NF-kappa-B signaling [[PMID:26138980],[PMID:23565261],[PMID:28604677]].

Name: PRAME N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the leucine-rich domain at the N-terminal in PRAME and similar proteins including LCR14 and OOG3 mainly from animals. PRAME proteins are involved in various cellular processes, primarily through its role as a substrate-recognition component of Cul2-RING E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase complexes. These complexes mediate the ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of target proteins, including truncated selenoproteins. PRAME proteins also function as transcriptional repressors, particularly inhibiting retinoic acid signaling, which affects cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis. Additionally, they are involved in regulating pluripotency in embryonic stem cells and play roles in immune signaling pathways by modulating NF-kappa-B signaling [[PMID:26138980],[PMID:23565261],[PMID:28604677]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8346)
Description: This entry represents a Leucine Rich domain in uncharacterised proteins mainly from animals. Each Leucine Rich Repeat is composed of a beta-alpha unit. These units form elongated non-globular structures. Leucine Rich Repeats are often flanked by cysteine rich domains.

Name: Conjugation-associated 9-bladed beta-propeller domain
Description: This entry represents a 9-bladed beta-propeller domain found in proteins involved in bacterial DNA conjugation. The prototype member, D806_5234 from Mycolicibacterium smegmatis strain MKD8, is a 481 amino acid membrane protein essential for DNA conjugation in recipient strains [[PMID:18554329]]. The protein contains an N-terminal transmembrane helix (residues 18-38) that anchors it to the inner membrane, followed by this large extracytoplasmic beta-propeller domain. Beta-propeller domains typically function as protein-protein or protein-DNA interaction platforms. In this conjugation system, the 9-bladed architecture likely provides multiple binding surfaces for interactions with other conjugation machinery components or with the incoming DNA. This protein is part of a recipient strain-specific genomic insert (RSR-II) that distinguishes recipient strains like MKD8 from donor strains like mc(2)155 in the unidirectional M. smegmatis conjugation system [[PMID:18554329]]. Disruption of this protein abolishes DNA conjugation ability in recipient strains without affecting ESX-1 secretion, suggesting a specific role in DNA uptake or processing during conjugation.

Name: Functional amyloid protein FapB/FapC family
Description: This entry represents the FapB/FapC family of functional amyloid proteins found in Pseudomonas species. These proteins are components of the Fap (functional amyloid in Pseudomonas) system that forms extracellular amyloid fibrils. FapC is the major structural component of the amyloid fibrils, while FapB is a minor component that likely nucleates fibril formation [[PMID:23504942]]. Both proteins contain an N-terminal signal peptide for secretion and multiple imperfect repeats: FapB contains three repeats of approximately 35 residues each, while FapC contains three repeats of approximately 34 residues connected by linker regions. FapC also contains a C-terminal Cys-X-X-Cys motif. The Fap amyloid fibrils are highly stable, resistant to boiling in 2% SDS and requiring greater than 90% formic acid to dissolve. Overexpression of the fap operon (fapA-fapF) causes cell aggregation and increased biofilm formation, though deletion of the entire locus shows no visible growth phenotype under laboratory conditions [[PMID:23504942]]. The functional amyloids contribute to biofilm stability and may play a role in cell adhesion during surface colonisation.

Name: GtfB middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle domain in GtfB proteins found in Streptococcus pneumoniae. GtfB is a 45 amino acid protein required for polymorphic O-glycosylation of serine-rich repeat proteins. GtfB stabilises the glycosylation activity of GtfA and forms a heterotetramer complex with GtfA [[PMID:24936067],[PMID:28246170]]. The protein contains a glycosyltransferase 1 domain at residues 55-171 which is required for stable complex formation with GtfA [[PMID:24936067]]. GtfB is a peripheral membrane protein localised to the cell membrane and is part of the accessory SecA2/SecY2 protein translocation system [[PMID:24936067],[PMID:28246170]].

Name: CRISPR-associated protein MJ1674, second domain
Description: This entry represents the second domain of the CRISPR-associated protein MJ1674 found in Archaea. The full-length protein is 634 amino acids with a molecular weight of approximately 71.5 kDa and contains four domains. MJ1674 is part of the CRISPR (clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat) adaptive immune system that provides protection against mobile genetic elements. The protein is encoded in a type III-A CRISPR locus and is involved in antiviral defence. MJ1674 is found in Methanocaldococcus jannaschii and related archaeal species. The specific function of this second domain remains to be determined.

Name: [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type complex Eha, membrane protein EhaK
Description: [NiFe] hydrogenases function in H2 metabolism in a variety of microorganisms, enabling them to use H2 as a source of reducing equivalent under aerobic and anaerobic conditions [NiFe] hydrogenases consist of two subunits, hydrogenase large and hydrogenase small. The large subunit contains the binuclear [NiFe] active site, while the small subunit binds at least one [4Fe-4S] cluster [[PMID:15119826]]. Energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases (or [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type) form a distinct group within the [NiFe] hydrogenase family [[PMID:15168611],[PMID:16645307],[PMID:22872868]]. Members of this subgroup include: Hydrogenase 3 and 4 (Hyc and Hyf) from Escherichia coli, CO-induced hydrogenase (Coo) from Rhodospirillum rubrum, Mbh hydrogenase from Pyrococcus furiosus, Eha and Ehb hydrogenases from Methanothermobacter species, Ech hydrogenase from Methanosarcina barkeri, Energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases are membrane-bound enzymes with a six-subunit core: the large and small hydrogenase subunits, plus two hydrophilic proteins and two integral membrane proteins. Their large and small subunits show little sequence similarity to other [NiFe] hydrogenases, except for key conserved residues coordinating the active site and [FeS] cluster. However, they show considerable sequence similarity to the six-subunit, energy-conserving NADH:quinone oxidoreductases (complex I), which are present in cytoplasmic membranes of many bacteria and in inner mitochondrial membranes. However, the reactions they catalyse differ significantly from complex I. Energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases function as ion pumps. Eha and Ehb hydrogenases contain extra subunits in addition to those shared by other energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases (or [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type). Eha contains a 6[4Fe-4S] polyferredoxin, a 10[4F-4S] polyferredoxin, ten other predicted integral membrane proteins (EhaA, EhaB, EhaC, EhaD, EhaE, EhaF, EhaG, EhaI, EhaK, EhaL) and four hydrophilic subunits (EhaM, EhaR, EhS, EhT) [[PMID:10491142],[PMID:22872868]]. The ten predicted integral membrane proteins are absent from Ech, Coo, Hyc and Hyf complexes, which may have simpler membrane components than Eha. Eha and Ehb catalyse the reduction of low-potential redox carriers (e.g. ferredoxins or polyferredoxins), which then might function as electron donors to oxidoreductases. Based on sequence similarity and genome context analysis, other organisms such as Methanopyrus kandleri, Methanocaldococcus jannaschii, and Methanothermobacter marburgensis also encode Eha-like [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type complexes and have very similarehaoperon structure. This entry represents small membrane proteins that are predicted to be the EhaK transmembrane subunits of multi-subunit membrane-bound [NiFe]-hydrogenase Eha complexes.

Name: TP_0126
Description: This entry represents TP_0126 proteins found in Treponema and related spirochaetes. The proteins adopt a beta-barrel fold structure and belong to the OmpA-like superfamily. TP_0126 is found in Treponema pallidum, the causative agent of syphilis, and shows similarity to TP_0733. The proteins are classified in the OmpA-OmpF porin family and contain predicted transmembrane helices. The function of this protein remains to be determined.

Name: Type III secretion system regulatory protein HrpD6
Description: This entry represents HrpD6, a small regulatory protein involved in controlling the type III secretion system (T3SS) in Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzicola. HrpD6 regulates several genes of the hrp-hrc-hpa cluster, upregulating expression of hpa2, hpa1 and hpaB, and partially controlling hrcC and hrcT expression [[PMID:21615204]]. The protein controls secretion of T3SS TAL effectors such as AvrXa27 and regulates expression of HrpX-regulated protein (Xrp) genes [[PMID:21615204],[PMID:24675748]]. HrpD6 is positively regulated by HrpX and is expressed under control of the hrpD operon promoter [[PMID:21615204]]. The protein is 80 amino acids in length and belongs to the HrpD6-like family. HrpD6 is essential for proper T3SS function and bacterial pathogenicity [[PMID:21615204],[PMID:16957248]].

Name: HrcQa N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of HrcQa proteins found in Pseudomonas and related gammaproteobacteria. HrcQa is a component of the type III secretion system required for effector protein delivery and pathogenicity in plants [[PMID:10781092]]. The protein is localised to the cell inner membrane as a peripheral membrane protein and probably participates in the formation of a C-ring-like assembly along with HrcQb. While the full-length protein has demonstrated roles in type III secretion, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: GreA-associated C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents a TPR-like domain in GreA-associated domains (GRAD) proteins. This domain is found primarily in spirochaetes, particularly Treponema species. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: SsaM
Description: This entry represents the SsaM domain. SsaM is a secretion system apparatus protein that functions as a component of the type III secretion apparatus in Salmonella pathogenicity island 2 [[PMID:9140973]]. SsaM expression is induced when grown in an acidic environment and is activated by the response regulator SsrB [[PMID:17630976]]. This domain belongs to the type III secretory system chaperone clan, which comprises small acidic proteins involved in the translocation of pathogenic proteins into host cells. The proteins are found predominantly in Gammaproteobacteria, particularly Enterobacteriaceae. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: TP_0324 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of TP_0324 proteins. This domain contains a structure with multiple repeating modules comprising five beta-sheets followed by a loop, and forms a long hydrophobic groove. The domain is found predominantly in Spirochaetota, with representatives in Treponema and Spirochaeta genera within Spirochaetales order. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: BigA fourth domain
Description: This entry represents the fourth domain of BigA autotransporter proteins found in Salmonella and related Enterobacteriaceae. BigA is a large surface-exposed virulence protein of approximately 1953 amino acids that contains an autotransporter domain and multiple repeats. This fourth domain adopts a pectate lyase-like fold, a parallel beta-helix structure. BigA proteins are found across 37 bacterial species, predominantly in Pseudomonadota. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: BigA third pectate lyase-like domain
Description: This entry represents the third domain of BigA proteins. The domain adopts a pectate lyase-like fold structure. BigA proteins are putative surface-exposed virulence proteins found in bacteria, predominantly in Pseudomonadota including Gammaproteobacteria and Betaproteobacteria. The proteins contain an autotransporter domain. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: TP_0855 C-terminal
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of TP_0855 proteins found primarily in Treponema and related spirochaetes. This domain contains tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) motifs and includes a coiled-coil region at residues 1076-1126. TP_0855 is a large protein of approximately 1127 amino acids that is processed from a precursor containing an N-terminal signal peptide. The protein is found across 28 bacterial species, predominantly in the Spirochaetota phylum. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: YgjJ
Description: This entry represents YgjJ proteins found in Enterobacteriaceae, particularly in Escherichia and Shigella. YgjJ is a protein of approximately 356 amino acids that adopts a porin fold structure. The protein is processed from a precursor containing an N-terminal signal peptide. The protein is found across 28 bacterial species, predominantly in Gammaproteobacteria within the Enterobacterales order. The function of this protein remains to be determined.

Name: Type II toxin-antitoxin system toxin Rv0299
Description: This entry represents the toxin component of a type II toxin-antitoxin system found predominantly in actinobacteria. The prototype protein Rv0299 from Mycobacterium tuberculosis inhibits colony formation when expressed in M. smegmatis [[PMID:20011113]]. The toxic effect is neutralised by coexpression with the cognate antitoxin Rv0298. This domain is related to MazF/PemK-like toxins. Members of this family are approximately 100 amino acids in length.

Name: YycP, beta-propeller domain
Description: This entry represents the beta-propeller domain found in YycP, an uncharacterised protein from Bacillus subtilis. YycP is a 387 amino acid precursor protein with a signal peptide. This domain is found predominantly in Bacillati, particularly within the Bacillales order including Bacillaceae, Paenibacillaceae and Caryophanaceae families. The function of this domain remains to be determined [[PMID:9205843]].

Name: AlgK tetratricopeptide repeat domain
Description: This entry represents the tetratricopeptide repeat domain found in AlgK, an alginate biosynthesis protein involved in the polymerisation of mannuronate to alginate [[PMID:9043140],[PMID:9457868]]. AlgK is a lipoprotein localised to the periplasmic side of the cell outer membrane and interacts with AlgX in a multiprotein complex. This domain adopts a TPR-like helical structure and belongs to the Sel1 superfamily. This domain is found predominantly in Pseudomonadota, particularly within Pseudomonas species. While AlgK is involved in alginate biosynthesis, the specific function of this TPR domain remains to be determined.

Name: LAM55 pectate lyase-like domain
Description: This entry represents the second domain in LAM55 (Exo-beta-1,3-glucanase). LAM55 specifically hydrolyses laminarin and laminarioligosaccharides, producing glucose and laminaribiose as end products. This domain contains a right-handed beta helix, similar to that first found in pectate lyase [[PMID:25752603]].

Name: APC1 fourth domain
Description: This entry represents the fourth domain found in anaphase-promoting complex subunit 1 proteins. This domain is part of the multi-domain architecture of APC1, which is a component of the anaphase-promoting complex/cyclosome (APC/C). The APC/C is a cell cycle-regulated E3 ubiquitin ligase that controls progression through mitosis and the G1 phase of the cell cycle [[PMID:11134076],[PMID:11832245]]. The full-length APC1 protein mediates ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of target proteins and plays roles in metaphase to anaphase transition, embryonic development, and neurotransmitter regulation. This domain is found across eukaryotes including metazoans, fungi and plants. The specific function of this fourth domain within APC1 remains to be determined [[PMID:11134076],[PMID:11832245],[PMID:11861581],[PMID:15556870]]. This domain contains a TPR-like fold.

Name: HIR3 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in HIR3 proteins. This domain is present in proteins such as HIR3 from Candida albicans, a histone transcription regulator involved in nucleosome assembly pathways required for heterochromatin integrity and chromosome segregation. While the full-length HIR3 protein functions in chromatin organisation and transcription regulation, the specific function of this N-terminal TPR domain remains to be determined.

Name: VPS35L
Description: This entry represents VPS35L proteins. These proteins are present in zebrafish, a component of the retriever complex essential for retromer-independent retrieval and recycling of numerous cargos such as integrins. The full-length VPS35L protein drives the retrieval and recycling of cargo proteins by coupling to SNX17 and is involved in endocytic recycling processes [[PMID:25355947],[PMID:28892079],[PMID:31712251]].

Name: CNOT10 tetratricopeptide repeat domain
Description: This entry represents an uncharacterised domain in CNOT10, a component of the CCR4-NOT complex. CNOT10 is one of the major cellular mRNA deadenylases linked to bulk mRNA degradation, miRNA-mediated repression, and transcriptional regulation [[PMID:23221646]]. CNOT10 and CNOT11 form a subcomplex that docks to the CNOT1 scaffold [[PMID:23303381]]. The protein contains multiple TPR repeats ([pfam:PF00515]) that likely mediate protein-protein interactions within the complex. The specific function of these TPR domains remains to be determined.

Name: HIR3 C-terminal helical domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal helical domain of histone transcription regulator 3 (HIR3) and related proteins. HIR3 is a component of the HIR complex that functions in transcriptional repression of histone genes [[PMID:9001207]]. This C-terminal domain spans approximately 250 residues and consists of multiple alpha-helices arranged in a compact fold. The domain is distinct from the TPR-like repeats found in the N-terminal and middle regions of the protein. The C-terminal region contains a disordered segment at positions 1597-1630 that may provide flexibility for protein interactions. HIR3 functions together with HIR1, HIR2 and HPC2 to form the HIR complex, which interacts with the histone chaperone ASF1 and the chromatin protein RTT106 [[PMID:16303565],[PMID:19683497]]. The complex is required for nucleosome assembly and periodic repression of histone genes during the cell cycle. This domain is conserved in HIR3 homologues found in fungi and vertebrates. The specific molecular functions of this C-terminal domain within the HIR complex remain to be determined.

Name: Armadillo repeat-containing protein 6 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of armadillo repeat-containing protein 6 (ARMC6), found in metazoans. The full-length ARMC6 proteins contain multiple armadillo repeats in their C-terminal region and have been implicated in anatomical structure morphogenesis and haematopoietic progenitor cell differentiation. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:24055424]].

Name: [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type complex Eha, membrane protein EhaI
Description: [NiFe] hydrogenases function in H2 metabolism in a variety of microorganisms, enabling them to use H2 as a source of reducing equivalent under aerobic and anaerobic conditions [NiFe] hydrogenases consist of two subunits, hydrogenase large and hydrogenase small. The large subunit contains the binuclear [NiFe] active site, while the small subunit binds at least one [4Fe-4S] cluster [[PMID:15119826]]. Energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases (or [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type) form a distinct group within the [NiFe] hydrogenase family [[PMID:15168611],[PMID:16645307],[PMID:22872868]]. Members of this subgroup include: Hydrogenase 3 and 4 (Hyc and Hyf) from Escherichia coli, CO-induced hydrogenase (Coo) from Rhodospirillum rubrum, Mbh hydrogenase from Pyrococcus furiosus, Eha and Ehb hydrogenases from Methanothermobacter species, Ech hydrogenase from Methanosarcina barkeri, Energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases are membrane-bound enzymes with a six-subunit core: the large and small hydrogenase subunits, plus two hydrophilic proteins and two integral membrane proteins. Their large and small subunits show little sequence similarity to other [NiFe] hydrogenases, except for key conserved residues coordinating the active site and [FeS] cluster. However, they show considerable sequence similarity to the six-subunit, energy-conserving NADH:quinone oxidoreductases (complex I), which are present in cytoplasmic membranes of many bacteria and in inner mitochondrial membranes. However, the reactions they catalyse differ significantly from complex I. Energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases function as ion pumps. Eha and Ehb hydrogenases contain extra subunits in addition to those shared by other energy-converting [NiFe] hydrogenases (or [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type). Eha contains a 6[4Fe-4S] polyferredoxin, a 10[4F-4S] polyferredoxin, ten other predicted integral membrane proteins (EhaA, EhaB, EhaC, EhaD, EhaE, EhaF, EhaG, EhaI, EhaK, EhaL) and four hydrophilic subunits (EhaM, EhaR, EhS, EhT) [[PMID:10491142],[PMID:22872868]]. The ten predicted integral membrane proteins are absent from Ech, Coo, Hyc and Hyf complexes, which may have simpler membrane components than Eha. Eha and Ehb catalyse the reduction of low-potential redox carriers (e.g. ferredoxins or polyferredoxins), which then might function as electron donors to oxidoreductases. Based on sequence similarity and genome context analysis, other organisms such as Methanopyrus kandleri, Methanocaldococcus jannaschii, and Methanothermobacter marburgensis also encode Eha-like [NiFe]-hydrogenase-3-type complexes and have very similarehaoperon structure. This entry represents small membrane proteins that are predicted to be the EhaI transmembrane subunits of multi-subunit membrane-bound [NiFe]-hydrogenase Eha complexes.

Name: Encapsidation protein CP3
Description: Staphylococcus aureus pathogenicity islands (SaPIs) are highly mobile phage-related pathogenicity islands that typically carry genes for one or more superantigens. They are the primary cause of superantigen-induced diseases, especially toxic shock syndrome. SaPIs are induced to excise and replicate by the presence of a temperate phage. The SaPI then induces the phage to produce special small capsids into which it is encapsidated, resulting in exteremly high transfer frequences [[PMID:18223072]]. This entry represents the SaPI CP3 protein, which is involved in the morphogenesis of the characteristic small SaPI capsids [[PMID:17581119]].

Name: CLU5d C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of pathogenicity cluster 5 protein d (CLU5d) found in plant pathogenic fungi. CLU5d is a secreted protein required for appressorial penetration of intact host epidermal cells and for pathogenicity [[PMID:31599055]]. The protein is among the most highly expressed genes in appressoria and biotrophic hyphae in planta. CLU5d contains N-linked glycosylation sites and disordered regions. This C-terminal domain follows the central region of the protein and is found in CLU5d homologues across Colletotrichum species and related plant pathogens. The specific function of this C-terminal domain in appressorial penetration remains to be determined.

Name: YCF81 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of YCF81 (ycf81) proteins found in chloroplasts. YCF81 is an uncharacterised protein first identified in the ancestral chloroplast genome of Mesostigma viride, a green alga representing an early branch of green plant evolution [[PMID:10688199]]. The protein contains a C-terminal TRAM domain and a PIN domain, suggesting potential roles in RNA processing or nucleic acid metabolism. This N-terminal domain precedes these characterised domains and is predicted to contain multiple transmembrane regions based on sequence analysis. The domain is found in YCF81 homologues in chloroplast genomes across green plants. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: FAM240
Description: This entry represents FAM240 small uncharacterised proteins found in vertebrates. These proteins are typically 78-95 amino acids in length. This entry includes FAM240A, FAM240B, and FAM240C proteins found in humans and related species. FAM240C shows tissue-enriched expression in the tongue according to the Human Protein Atlas. The proteins contain a C-terminal disordered region with basic and acidic residue composition bias. This entry also includes Leucine-rich repeat-containing protein 2. The function of these proteins is unknown.

Name: ANKRD40 ubiquitin-like domain
Description: This entry represents an uncharacterised domain found in ANKRD40 proteins (ankyrin repeat domain-containing protein 40). This domain contains an ubiquitin-like fold. ANKRD40 proteins are approximately 360-370 amino acids in length and show tissue-enhanced expression in brain and other tissues. The proteins contain multiple disordered regions with compositional bias towards proline and acidic residues. While full-length ANKRD40 proteins have been identified through proteomics studies and show various protein-protein interactions, the specific function of this ubiquitin-like domain remains to be determined. The domain is found across vertebrate species including mammals and birds. The ubiquitin-like fold suggests potential roles in protein modification or signalling pathways.

Name: UCP007778, beta-propeller domain
Description: This is a beta-propeller domain found in the uncharacterised conserved protein YGR117C from S.cerevisiae and similar sequences. They have a LisH motif at the N-terminal.

Name: Increased recombination centres protein 19
Description: Irc19 is involved in sporulation and maintenance of the mitochondrial DNA [[PMID:12432101],[PMID:11921089],[PMID:19751518]].

Name: TNFRSF19 second TNFR Cys domain
Description: This entry represents the second TNFR-Cys domain found in tumor necrosis factor receptor superfamily member 19 (TNFRSF19) and related proteins. This domain is located between the N-terminal extracellular TNFR-Cys repeat region and the large C-terminal cytoplasmic disordered region. TNFRSF19 is a single-pass type I membrane protein that can mediate activation of JNK and NF-kappa-B pathways and promote caspase- independent cell death [[PMID:10809768]]. The protein interacts with TRAF proteins and is highly expressed in prostate tissue. While the full-length protein functions are characterised, the specific role of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: SERAC1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of SERAC1 (Serine active site-containing protein 1), which forms a small helical bundle. SERAC1 is found in eukaryotes and facilitates serine transport from cytosol to mitochondria by stabilising sideroflexin-1 [[PMID:22683713],[PMID:35235340]]. The full-length protein plays important roles in phosphatidylglycerol remodelling and mitochondrial DNA maintenance [[PMID:22683713],[PMID:35235340]]. SERAC1 localises to the endoplasmic reticulum and endoplasmic reticulum-mitochondria interface. The specific function of this N-terminal helical domain remains to be determined.

Name: Mediator subunit 33 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal cullin-like repeats domain of Mediator complex subunit 33 proteins (MED33). These proteins are components of the Mediator complex, a coactivator involved in the regulated transcription of nearly all RNA polymerase II-dependent genes [[PMID:17560376]]. The Mediator complex functions as a bridge to convey information from gene-specific regulatory proteins to the basal RNA polymerase II transcription machinery. MED33A and MED33B are involved in the repression of phenylpropanoid biosynthesis and may compete for common binding partners or for occupancy in Mediator. The proteins are localised to the nucleus. The specific function of this C-terminal cullin-like repeats domain remains to be determined.

Name: RNA editing complex structural subunit MP81, C2H2 zinc finger
Description: This entry includes structural subunit MP81 ([swissprot:Q586L9], also known as KREPA1) from the RNA editing complex found in Trypanosoma and Leishmania. The editing process is performed by the editosome, a multi-protein complex that catalyses the insertion and deletion of uridylates (U) that occurs during RNA editing. Uridylate insertion/deletion editing, which appears to be unique to these organisms, processes most mitochondrial pre-mRNAs to produce mature mRNAs. Editosomes consist of several components and in Trypanosoma these include: REL1/MP52 and REL2/Mp48 RNA ligases; MP18, MP42, MP63 and MP81 that contain zinc fingers (represented in this entry) and an OB fold domain at the C-terminal [[PMID:12649499]].

Name: Mitochondrial translation factor Atp22
Description: This entry represents the mitochondrial translation factor Atp22, which is specific for subunit 6 of the mitochondrial ATPase [[PMID:12646576],[PMID:17110482]]. Atp22 is a component of the mitochondrial inner membrane required for the expression of the CF(0) component of the ATPase [[PMID:12646576]].

Name: Mediator subunit 33 middle TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents the TPR-like domain of Mediator complex subunit 33 (MED33) proteins. These proteins are components of the Mediator complex, a coactivator involved in the regulated transcription of nearly all RNA polymerase II-dependent genes [[PMID:22167189]]. The Mediator complex functions as a bridge to convey information from gene-specific regulatory proteins to the basal RNA polymerase II transcription machinery. MED33A and MED33B are involved in the repression of phenylpropanoid biosynthesis and may compete for common binding partners or for occupancy in Mediator. The proteins are found in plants and are localised to the nucleus. The specific function of this TPR-like domain remains to be determined.

Name: Bacteriocin, heterocycloanthracin/sonorensin family
Description: Numerous bacteria encode systems for producing bacteriocins by extensive modification of ribosomally produced precursors. Members of the TOMM class (thiazole/oxazole-modified microcins) are recognizable by association with cyclodehydratase (and often dehydrogenase) maturation proteins. This family consists of a special subclass, the heterocycloanthracin family, that share a homologous leader peptide region and then a repeat region with Cys as every third residue. In Bacillus anthracis and Bacillus cereus, the RiPP (ribosomally translated and post-translationally modified natural product) precursor is encoded far from its maturase genes, and every strain has the system. In other species (e.g. B. licheniformis, B. sorenensis), precursor and maturase genes are close together. Sonorensin, from B. sonorensis MT93, was shown to have broad spectrum antimicrobial activity, affecting Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [[PMID:19383135]].

Name: Dynein axonemal intermediate chain 3 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of dynein axonemal intermediate chain 3 (DNAI3), also known as WD repeat-containing protein 63. While the full-length DNAI3 protein is a component of the multisubunit axonemal dynein complex involved in cilia function and acts as a negative regulator of cell migration through inhibition of Arp2/3 complex-mediated actin polymerisation, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:29285825],[PMID:25498833],[PMID:30060180]].

Name: CRISPR-associated protein Csx19
Description: Members of this protein family are found exclusively in CRISPR-containing organisms, in operon contexts with RAMP (repeat-associated mystery protein) proteins also linked to CRISPR. The Csx19 family previously was known as the TIGR03984 family CRISPR-associated protein [[PMID:28698278]].

Name: Colanic acid biosynthesis WcaM
Description: This protein of uncharacterised function is the final gene in the conserved colanic acid biosynthesis cluster observed in Enterobacteraceae [[PMID:8759852]].

Name: Putative exported peptide YydF-like
Description: This entry describes a rare family of small proteins, about 50 residues in length, that includes YydF from Bacillus subtilis and SAG 2028 from Streptococcus agalactiae 2603V/R. Mutational analysis and genomic context show that members of this family likely are modified by a (variably present) radical SAM enzyme, are exported by an ABC transporter, and may have antimicrobial or signalling properties (or both). The member from Bacillus subtilis induces the LiaRS two-component system [[PMID:17921301]].

Name: PEX8 central TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents a TPR-like domain found in PEX8 and related peroxisomal membrane proteins in fungi. PEX8 proteins are essential components of the machinery required for the import of peroxisomal matrix proteins [[PMID:7738036]]. PEX8 binding to the N-terminal region of the PEX5 cargo receptor induces conformational changes in the TPR domains, decreasing their binding affinity to cargo and facilitating the release of PTS1 proteins within the peroxisome [[PMID:23902771]]. The specific function of this TPR-like domain remains to be determined.

Name: Mediator subunit 33 second TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents the second TPR-like domain found in Mediator subunit 33 proteins in plants. MED33 proteins are components of the Mediator complex, a coactivator involved in regulated transcription of RNA polymerase II-dependent genes [[PMID:17560376],[PMID:22167189]]. The full-length proteins function as a bridge between gene-specific regulatory proteins and the basal RNA polymerase II transcription machinery, and are involved in repression of phenylpropanoid biosynthesis [[PMID:22167189]]. The specific function of this fourth TPR-like domain remains to be determined.

Name: Adenylate cyclase activation protein git1 third TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents the third domain of adenylate cyclase activation protein git1. Git1 is involved in the glucose/cAMP signalling pathway and is required for maintaining basal cAMP levels and for producing a glucose-triggered cAMP response [[PMID:16489217]]. The full-length protein interacts with adenylate cyclase (cyr1) and localises to the cytoplasm, nucleus, and spindle pole body. Git1 contains Munc13 homology domains which are characteristic of proteins involved in vesicle priming and membrane trafficking. This domain is found in fungi. The specific function of this third domain remains to be determined. This domain contains TPR-like fold.

Name: Hydroxyacyl-thioester dehydratase type 2
Description: Htd2 is a mitochondrial 3-hydroxyacyl-thioester dehydratase involved in fatty acid biosynthesis. It is required for respiratory growth and for normal mitochondrial morphology [[PMID:15387819]].

Name: Modification target Cys-rich repeat
Description: This entry represents a cysteine-rich repeat found in a number of bacterial putative radical-SAM modified natural product precursors [[PMID:21478363]].

Name: ACGX-repeat peptide
Description: Members of this family average 75 amino acids in length and average six instances of the motif ACGX, where X is A, S, or T. Members are proposed target sequences for modification by adjacent radical SAM/SPASM domain proteins (family TIGR04340). Cys residues adjacent to Gly residues are common as proposed sites for modification by radical SAM enzymes.

Name: NuzM, N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of the fungal-specific NuzM subunit (also known as NUO2) of mitochondrial NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase (Complex I). This domain comprises a short helix with loops at the N-terminal region of the protein. The NuzM protein is a fungal-specific subunit that functions in electron transfer from NADH to the respiratory chain, with ubiquinone as the immediate electron acceptor [[PMID:25801605],[PMID:26087349]]. In Candida albicans, NUO2 plays roles in cell wall integrity, osmotic and oxidative resistance, yeast to hypha transition, and virulence [[PMID:25801605]]. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: NuzM C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the fungal-specific NuzM subunit (also known as NUO2) of mitochondrial NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase (Complex I). This domain comprises a short helices with loops at the C-terminal region of the protein. The NuzM protein is a fungal-specific subunit that functions in electron transfer from NADH to the respiratory chain, with ubiquinone as the immediate electron acceptor [[PMID:25801605],[PMID:2137337]]. In Candida albicans, NUO2 plays roles in cell wall integrity, osmotic and oxidative resistance, yeast to hypha transition, and virulence [[PMID:25801605]]. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Putative ATP-grasp target RiPP, Rhodococcus
Description: Members of this small family are putative RiPP (Ribosomally translated, Post-translationally modified Peptides) precursors, modified by RimK-like ATP-grasp proteins. Members are encoded near both an ATP-grasp protein and C39 peptidase domain-containing transporter. Members are short polypeptides that contain the GG motif expected for cleavage on export.

Name: NUP42 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of nucleoporin NUP42 found in vertebrates. NUP42 is a component of the nuclear pore complex required for the export of mRNAs containing poly(A) tails from the nucleus into the cytoplasm. The protein localises to the nuclear pore complex at the nuclear membrane. NUP42 contains an N-terminal C3H1-type zinc finger domain and multiple FG repeats that serve as interaction sites for karyopherins. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:10358091]].

Name: ATP synthase subunit C lysine N-methyltransferase N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of ATP synthase subunit C lysine N-methyltransferases (ATPSCKMT/FAM173B). The full-length protein is a mitochondrial protein-lysine N-methyltransferase that trimethylates ATP synthase subunit C, specifically ATP5MC1 and ATP5MC2 [[PMID:30530489]]. This N-terminal region contains a transmembrane helix and includes a mitochondrial targeting sequence required for mitochondrial localisation [[PMID:30530489]]. The domain is found in eukaryotic proteins and is essential for proper mitochondrial membrane association. While the C-terminal region contains the S-adenosyl-L-methionine-dependent methyltransferase activity, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Diuretic hormone class 2-like
Description: DH31 is a diuretic hormone that increases cAMP production and fluid secretion in Malpighian tubules of several insect species. DH31 is similar to the calcitonin family [[PMID:10841553],[PMID:11316500]]. In Drosophila, it has also been linked to a neuropeptide relay mechanism for circadian control of sleep [[PMID:25455031]], and peristalsis in the junction region of the larval midgut [[PMID:20698983]]. Drosophila neuropeptide DH31 is a ligand for orphan GPCR named CG17415 [[PMID:15781884]]. This entry also includes venom protein 55.1 and U-limacoditoxin(8)-Dv66 (also known as Vulnericin) [[PMID:33893140]], which belong to the diuretic hormone class 2 family. U-scoloptoxin(21)-Sm1/2a are uncharacterised proteins related to DH31.

Name: Mitochondrial import receptor subunit TOM5 homologue
Description: Protein import into the mitochondria involves several translocase complexes: the preprotein translocase of the outer mitochondrial membrane (TOM complex) and the two preprotein translocases of the inner mitochondrial membrane (called TIM complexes). TOM5 is a component of the TOM complex. This entry represents TOM5 from the plant complex, which consist at least of six subunits [[PMID:9642296],[PMID:11161051]].

Name: Succinate dehydrogenase subunit 7, mitochondrial
Description: The succinate dehydrogenase complex (complex II) is a highly conserved protein complex composed of the SDH1-SDH4 subunits in bacteria and in the mitochondria of animals and fungi, with up to four additional subunits in complex II of plants, termed SDH5-SDH8. SDH6 and SDH7 contribute to anchoring succinate dehydrogenase to the inner mitochondrial membrane [[PMID:28039307]]. This entry represents SDH7.

Name: Protein GDF5OS, mitochondrial
Description: The function of mitochondrial protein GDF5OS, also known as GDF5 antisense RNA 1 (GDF5-AS1), is not known. Orthologues are known only from mammals.

Name: Sperm mitochondrial-associated cysteine-rich protein
Description: This entry includes sperm mitochondrial cysteine-rich protein (SMCP) from human, which enhances sperm motility [[PMID:16325371]]. Its absence has been associated with infertility.

Name: NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase 17.8kDa helical domain
Description: This entry represents the helical domain found in the 17.8 kDa subunit of NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase (Complex I). This domain contains a single transmembrane helix that anchors the protein in the mitochondrial inner membrane. While the complete 17.8 kDa subunit is involved in electron transfer from NADH to ubiquinone as part of the respiratory chain [[PMID:8343129]], the specific function of this helical domain is membrane insertion and stability within the Complex I assembly. The domain is found in the 17.8 kDa subunit across fungal species, where it facilitates the protein's integration into the mitochondrial inner membrane as a single-pass transmembrane protein. Complex I comprises approximately 40 different subunits working together in respiratory electron transport.

Name: CON7 transcription factor helical domain
Description: This entry represents a helical domain found in the CON7 transcription factor from the rice blast fungus Pyricularia oryzae. CON7 is a C2H2 zinc finger transcription factor that plays a central role in appressorium formation and pathogenicity [[PMID:17378924]]. The protein is required for expression of genes involved in membrane metabolism, ergosterol biosynthesis, and pathogenic development. This helical domain corresponds to a predicted coiled-coil region and is found in fungi across multiple taxonomic orders including Hypocreales and Eurotiales. The specific function of this helical domain remains to be determined.

Name: Effector TSP1
Description: This entry represents an antiparallel beta-barrel domain found in TSP1 effector proteins and related small secreted cysteine-rich proteins. The domain is found in fungi, particularly within the Hypocreales. The structure consists of alternating stacked layers of tryptophan and arginine residues from respective strands, stabilised by multiple disulphide bonds across the domain. TSP1 functions as a secreted effector that stimulates salicylic acid signalling in plant roots [[PMID:34547313]]. The protein forms homodimers and is induced during fungal growth on plant roots [[PMID:34547313]]. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Bat coronavirus HKU5 Non-structural protein 3c
Description: This entry represents the accessory protein 4b, ORF4b (also called NS3c protein) of Pipistrellus bat coronavirus HKU5 and related proteins from bat coronaviruses. ORF4b/NS3c plays a role in the inhibition of host innate immunity by inhibiting the interaction between host IkappaB kinase epsilon (IKBKE or IKKE) and mitochondrial antiviral-signalling protein (MAVS). In turn, this inhibition prevents the production of host interferon beta. Additionally, it may also interfere with host antiviral response within the nucleus. ORF4b/NS3c proteins in this subgroup are similar to the MERS-CoV ORF4b (also known as MERS-CoV 4b) which has been shown to interfere with the NF-kappaB-dependent innate immune response during infection, as well as antagonizing the early antiviral alpha/beta interferon (IFN-alpha/beta) response, which may significantly contribute to MERS-CoV pathogenesis [[PMID:24443473],[PMID:26631542],[PMID:29370303],[PMID:16840328]].

Name: SARS-CoV-2 Orf10 protein
Description: This entry represents the Orf10 protein of Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), also known as 2019 novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV). SARS-CoV-2 causes the disease called coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). Orf10 appears to have no homologous proteins in SARS-CoV and other coronaviruses and is uniquely expressed in SARS-CoV-2 [[PMID:34845370],[PMID:32015508],[PMID:32098422]]. Recently, it was reported that Orf10 suppresses the IFN-I signalling pathway through the interaction with mitochondrial antiviral signalling protein (MAVS), which is degraded via Orf10-induced autophagy pathway. Orf10 interacts with NIX and LC3B and induces mitophagy [[PMID:34845370]].

Name: Protein LURE 1.1-1.6, defensin-like domain
Description: This entry represents the defensin-like (DEFL) domain of AtLURE1.1-1.6 and similar sequences found in Arabidopsis. This domain adopts a typical structure characterised by cysteine-stabilized alpha/beta scaffold [[PMID:29109411]]. LURE1.1-1.6 from Arabidopsis thaliana are pollen tube attractants guiding pollen tubes to the ovular micropyle [[PMID:29109411],[PMID:25153857],[PMID:15955924]]. AtLURE1.2 attracts specifically pollen tubes from A. thaliana, but not those from A. lyrata [[PMID:23271953]]. It triggers endocytosis of MDIS1 in the pollen tube tip [[PMID:26863186]].

Name: Metabolite traffic protein EboA
Description: This entry represents a group of proteins specific to cyanobacteria that carry the EboA (eustigmatophyte/bacterial operon A) domain. Members of this family appear required for transport of certain secondary metabolite precursors to the periplasm, including (but not limited) to precursors of scytonemin. More than half members of this group belong to scytonemin producers [[PMID:27906133],[PMID:30482833]].

Name: Plastid division protein N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of plastid division proteins PDV1 and PDV2 found in plants. These proteins are components of the plastid division machinery that accomplishes binary fission by simultaneous constriction of the FtsZ ring and the ARC5/DRP5B ring [[PMID:16998069],[PMID:32005784]]. The N-terminal region is located in the cytoplasm and contains disordered regions and coiled-coil structures. PDV proteins mediate recruitment and regulation of ARC5/DRP5B at the plastid division site and relay positional information between the stroma and outer surface via interactions with cytoplasmic and membrane-bound division machinery components [[PMID:28248291]]. While the full-length PDV proteins have established roles in chloroplast division, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: VPA1267-like
Description: This entry represents the uncharacterised protein VPA1267 [swissprot:Q87GP8] from Vibrio parahaemolyticus and related proteins found in proteobacteria. Members of this family occur in mobile elements, as for VPA1267 found in pathogenicity island 6 of Vibrio parahaemolyticus RIMD2210633 [[PMID:16672049]].

Name: STT4, N-terminal TPR repeats
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found at the N-terminal end of yeast Phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase STT4, which acts in the first committed step in the production of the second messenger inositol 1,4,5,-trisphosphate.

Name: Peptide resistance ABC transporter activity modulator VraH
Description: This entry represents VraH protein mainly found in Staphylococcus. VraH is a component of VraDEH system that confers a high-level resistance against daptomycin and gallidermin and enhances pathogenicity [[PMID:26856834]].

Name: Centrosomal protein of 89 kDa
Description: This entry represents CEP89 (centrosomal protein of 89 kDa) proteins found in metazoans. These are coiled-coil proteins of approximately 700-800 amino acids that localise to multiple cellular compartments including centrosomes, centrioles and mitochondria [[PMID:14654843],[PMID:23348840]]. CEP89 is required for ciliogenesis and localises to the distal appendage region of the centriole, which anchors the mother centriole to the plasma membrane [[PMID:23348840]]. The protein also plays a role in mitochondrial metabolism where it may modulate complex IV activity [[PMID:23575228]]. CEP89 contains extensive coiled-coil regions and multiple disordered regions. Homozygous deletions of CEP89 have been associated with complex IV deficiency and developmental abnormalities [[PMID:23575228]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8374)
Description: This is a region of tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats found in       a group of uncharacterised proteins from proteobacteria. Its function is unknown.

Name: Multiple cyclophane triceptide AmcA, group 3a
Description: This entry represents a family of proteins from actinomycetes that belong to one of several classes of peptide natural product synthesized on the ribosome and then post-translationally modified (RiPP), where the modification begins with the actinobacterial multiple cyclophane-forming radical SAM peptide maturase AmcB [[PMID:36454686]].

Name: Avirulence protein
Description: This entry represents Avirulence protein AvrXv3, a type III secretion system effector from Xanthomonas euvesicatoria [swissprot:Q9F9J3], and similar proteins found mainly in gammaproteobacteria. The expression of this protein is controlled by the hypersensitivity and pathogenicity (hrp) regulatory system. AvrXv3 possess transcription activation activity in yeast, and the domain located at the C terminus of this protein is responsible for this activity [[PMID:10975648]].

Name: Type III secretion system effector XopP-like
Description: This entry represents the Type III secretion system effector XopP and related proteins found in beta- and gammaproteobacteria. XopP is a highly conserved core-effector protein produced by X. campestris, the causal agent of black rot disease of crucifers such as Brassica spp., radish, and turnip. This protein is essential for virulence and inhibits the function of the host-plant exocyst complex by direct targeting of Exo70B, a subunit of the exocyst complex, which plays a significant role in plant immunity [[PMID:35640532]]. XopP interferes with exocyst-dependent exocytosis and efficiently inhibits the host's pathogen-associated molecular pattern (PAMP)-triggered immunity [[PMID:35640532],[PMID:25388636]]

Name: SAAL1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of SAAL1 (Synoviocyte proliferation-associated in collagen-induced arthritis protein 1). SAAL1 is a nuclear protein of approximately 459-474 amino acids found in vertebrates that plays a role in promoting cell proliferation in response to pro-inflammatory stimuli [[PMID:22127701]]. The full-length protein promotes the proliferation of synovial fibroblasts and is expressed in testis, ovary, and synovial tissue of knee joints. The protein contains an ARM-type fold and belongs to the SAAL1. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Dot/Icm T4SS effector AnkD/LegA15
Description: This entry represents a group of proteins from Legionellales, including the ankyrin repeat-containing protein LegA15 (AnkD, [swissprot:Q5ZSR1] from Legionella pneumophila, which shows structural similarity to another effector, LegA3/AnkH [swissprot:Q5ZT65]. This protein contains a cysteine protease-like (CPL) domain with similarity to ubiquitin-like hydrolases [[PMID:34866609]].

Name: Plant pathogen avirulence protein
Description: This family of proteins includes members that are involved in the interaction between plant pathogens and their host plants. The proteins in this family are typically secreted by bacterial pathogens and can function as avirulence factors. These factors are recognized by the plant immune system, leading to the activation of plant defense responses. The recognition of avirulence proteins by specific resistance proteins in the plant triggers a hypersensitive response, which is a form of programmed cell death in plants that restricts the spread of the pathogen. The detailed mechanisms of action and the specific targets of these avirulence proteins within the host plants may vary among family members.

Name: DOCK6-11, TPR domain
