Description: This is a tetratricopeptide (TPR)-like repeats domain found in human Dedicator of cytokinesis protein 6-11 (DOCK6-11) and related proteins. They are guanine nucleotide-exchange factors (GEF) that activate proteins by exchanging bound GDP for free GTP [[PMID:17196961],[PMID:37342957],[PMID:25851601],[PMID:15710388]]. DOCK7 activates Rac1 and Rac3 Rho small GTPases and unlike other counterparts, it does not have a GEF activity for CDC42. It is required for STMN1 Ser-15 phosphorylation during axon formation and consequently for neuronal polarisation [[PMID:16968698]]. As part of the DISP complex, DOCK7 may regulate the association of septins with actin and thereby regulate the actin cytoskeleton [[PMID:22494997]]. DOCK10 is essential for dendritic spine morphogenesis in Purkinje cells and in hippocampal neurons, via a CDC42-mediated pathway. It sustains B-cell lymphopoiesis in secondary lymphoid tissues and regulates FCER2/CD23 expression. [[PMID:25729399],[PMID:27502165]].

Name: Large ribosomal subunit protein uL10m C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal helical domain of mitochondrial ribosomal protein L11 (MRPL11/uL10m). This domain is found in fungi and contains disordered regions at the C-terminal. MRPL11 is a component of the mitochondrial large ribosomal subunit (54S) and is part of the mitoribosome machinery responsible for synthesis of mitochondrial genome-encoded proteins [[PMID:33056988]]. The protein belongs to the universal ribosomal protein uL10 family and has been structurally characterised in the Neurospora crassa mitoribosome [[PMID:33056988]]. While the full-length MRPL11 protein has established roles in mitochondrial translation, the specific function of this C-terminal helical domain remains to be determined.

Name: Like early starvation, chloroplastic
Description: This entry represents a beta-sheet domain found within LESV (Like Early Starvation) chloroplastic proteins in plants. LESV proteins are alpha-glucan binding proteins that bind preferentially to highly ordered alpha-glucans such as starch and crystalline maltodextrins [[PMID:27207856],[PMID:34367195]]. The full-length proteins are involved in the organisation of starch granule matrix and promote gravitropic responses by facilitating starch granule formation [[PMID:27207856],[PMID:34367195]]. These proteins are found across Viridiplantae, ranging from 426-578 amino acids in length. The proteins localise to chloroplast stroma and bind to starch granules. The specific function of this beta-sheet domain remains to be determined.

Name: PT dipeptide repeat lipoprotein
Description: This entry represents a group of uncharacterised proteins specific to Acinetobacter. Members of this group show an N-terminal lipoprotein signal peptide, followed by a variable length stretch of Pro-Thr dipeptide repeats or a related Pro-rich region and the non-repetitive domain.

Name: Sactipeptide RiPP StsA
Description: Members of this small family are encoded next to the radical SAM enzyme StsB, and are average about 40 amino acids in length. Maturation involves the introduction of three sulfur-to-alpha carbon thioether (sactionine) crosslinks, from the Cys residues in the motif CxCxC to glycines in a nearby motif GxGxG that runs antiparallel in a hairpin structure [[PMID:37363077]].

Name: AIM11 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the transmembrane domain at the N-terminal found in AIM11 proteins. AIM11 (Altered inheritance of mitochondria protein 11) is a multi-pass membrane protein involved in mitochondrial inheritance. This domain encompasses both transmembrane helical regions of the protein and the intervening sequence. The protein is found in fungi, particularly within the Saccharomycetes. AIM11 proteins localise to cellular membranes and play a role in the proper inheritance of mitochondria during cell division. The specific function of this transmembrane domain remains to be determined [[PMID:17494770]].

Name: INA17 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of INA17 proteins. INA17 is a component of the inner membrane assembly complex that promotes the biogenesis of mitochondrial F1F0-ATP synthase [[PMID:24942160]]. The protein facilitates assembly of the peripheral stalk and promotes assembly of the catalytic F1-domain with the membrane-embedded F0-domain. This C-terminal domain encompasses the transmembrane region and part of the mitochondrial intermembrane space-facing region. INA17 is found in fungi and localises to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a single-pass membrane protein. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: INA17 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of INA17 proteins. INA17 is a component of the inner membrane assembly complex that promotes the biogenesis of mitochondrial F1F0-ATP synthase [[PMID:24942160]]. The protein facilitates assembly of the peripheral stalk and promotes assembly of the catalytic F1-domain with the membrane-embedded F0-domain. This N-terminal domain encompasses the transmembrane region and part of the mitochondrial intermembrane space-facing region. INA17 is found in fungi and localises to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a single-pass membrane protein. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Secretion system apparatus protein SsaO
Description: This entry represents the homologues of Salmonella pathogenicity island 2 protein SsaO which is a member of a type III secretion system involved in the survival and replication of Salmonella in a host cell [[PMID:9140973]].

Name: Type III secretion system chaperone SseA
Description: This entry represents the type III secretion system chaperone SseA and related proteins. SseA functions as a type III secretion system (T3SS) chaperone, which is required for SseB and SseD accumulation and secretion [[PMID:12724372],[PMID:12603739]]. It may have a direct role in secretion of SseB and SseD, or may facilitate their correct folding, for efficient secretion and function. It is required for survival and replication within epithelial cells and macrophages. SseA is required for translocation of Salmonella pathogenicity island-2 effectors into host cells [[PMID:12787732]].

Name: Secretion system apparatus protein SsaP
Description: This entry represents the Secretion system apparatus protein SsaP and related proteins. SsaP is a component of the type III secretion system from Salmonella pathogenicity island 2 (SPI2), involved in the survival and replication of Salmonella in a host cell [[PMID:9140973]]. SsaP along with ssaL and ssaM do not have significant similarity to products of other type III secretion systems, and might be important for the specific function of the SPI2 type III secretion system.

Name: Biotrophy-associated secreted protein 2
Description: This entry represents the biotrophy-associated secreted protein 2 (BAS2) proteins. These are small secreted effector proteins of approximately 100 amino acids found in plant pathogenic fungi, particularly in the rice blast fungus Pyricularia oryzae. BAS2 is secreted into biotrophic interfacial complexes (BICs) and is involved in biotrophic colonisation of plant cells [[PMID:19357089]]. The protein contains an N-terminal signal sequence and is up-regulated in invasive hyphae during plant infection. BAS2 functions as an effector protein that facilitates the establishment of biotrophic relationships between the fungal pathogen and its plant host.

Name: Mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase kinase 1, TOG domain
Description: This entry represents the TOG domain in Mitogen-activated protein kinase kinase kinase 1 (MAP3K1) is a key signaling enzyme in the MAP kinase pathway, functioning at the top tier of the kinase cascade that regulates cellular responses to various external stimuli [[PMID:9808624]]. It activates the ERK and JNK kinase pathways by phosphorylation of MAP2K1 and MAP2K4. The TOG domain in MAP3K1 binds to tubulin heterodimers [[PMID:32817551]].

Name: Repeat of unknown function (DUF8380)
Description: This LRR domain of unknown function is found in uncharacterised proteins predominantly from metazoan.

Name: Class I lanthipeptide leader domain
Description: This entry represents a homology domain found regularly as a leader peptide, ending with a GlyGly dipeptide (a leader peptide cleavage site), of putative lanthipeptides (probably lantibiotics in most cases) encoded in the vicinity of a class I lanthionine synthase, or LanC. The designation L1a reflects model construction for class I lantibiotics (L1), first in a possible series (L1a), from the lanthipeptide RiPP collection [[PMID:32493223]]. Because the leader peptide may reflect constraints on cleavage and export, it may be expected that some members of this group undergo modifications other than lanthionine bridge formation, or no special modifications at all. Proteins in this entry are mainly found in bacteroidetes.

Name: Fanconi anemia group B protein N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal beta propeller domain of the Fanconi anemia group B protein (FANCB). FANCB is a DNA repair protein required for FANCD2 ubiquitination [[PMID:15502827]]. The protein belongs to the multisubunit FA complex composed of FANCA, FANCB, FANCC, FANCE, FANCF, FANCG, FANCL and FANCM [[PMID:15502827],[PMID:16116422]]. FANCB localises to the nucleus and is involved in the Fanconi anemia pathway. Mutations in FANCB cause Fanconi anemia complementation group B, an X-linked disorder affecting bone marrow elements and associated with chromosomal instability. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Leucine-rich repeat-containing protein 43 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of leucine-rich repeat-containing protein 43 (LRRC43). LRRC43 is found in mammals and contains multiple leucine-rich repeats and a leucine-rich repeat C-terminal domain (LRRCT). The protein is 656-667 amino acids in length and shows alternative splicing. The protein is expressed in testis and other tissues. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:21677750]].

Name: Fanconi anemia group G protein N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal TPR domain of Fanconi anemia group G protein (FANCG). FANCG is a DNA repair protein that operates in interstrand DNA cross-link repair and maintenance of normal chromosome stability [[PMID:9256465],[PMID:9806548]]. The protein belongs to the multisubunit FA complex composed of FANCA, FANCB, FANCC, FANCE, FANCF, FANCG, FANCL and FANCM. FANCG contains four tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domains that mediate protein-protein interactions. The protein localises to the nucleus and is highly expressed in testis and thymus. Mutations in FANCG cause Fanconi anemia complementation group G. The specific function of this N-terminal TPR domain remains to be determined.

Name: Leucine-rich repeat-containing protein 43 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal C2 domain of leucine-rich repeat-containing protein 43 (LRRC43). LRRC43 is found in mammals and contains multiple leucine-rich repeats and a leucine-rich repeat C-terminal domain (LRRCT). The protein is 656-667 amino acids in length and shows alternative splicing. The protein is expressed in testis and other tissues. This C-terminal region contains a C2 fold structure. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:21677750]].

Name: Integrator complex assembly factor BRAT1
Description: This entry represents BRAT1 proteins, also known as BRCA1-associated ATM activator 1. BRAT1 is a component of a multiprotein complex required for the assembly of the RNA endonuclease module of the integrator complex. The protein associates with INTS9 and INTS11 in the cytoplasm and blocks the active site of INTS11 to inhibit endonuclease activity before formation of the full integrator complex. Following dissociation of WDR73, BRAT1 facilitates nuclear import of the INTS9-INTS11 heterodimer. BRAT1 is also involved in DNA damage response, activating kinases ATM, SMC1A and PRKDC by modulating their phosphorylation status following ionising radiation stress. The protein contains HEAT repeats and a C-terminal BRAT1-like motif that mediates zinc coordination and INTS11 inhibition.

Name: Heat shock factor 2-binding protein ARM-like domain
Description: This entry represents the ARM-like domain of HSF2BP proteins, also known as heat shock factor 2-binding protein or MEILB2. The complete HSF2BP protein is a meiotic recombination factor involved in double-strand break repair [[PMID:31242413]]. HSF2BP modulates the localisation of recombinases DMC1 and RAD51 to meiotic double-strand break sites through interaction with BRCA2. The protein is essential for spermatogenesis and male fertility. This domain adopts an ARM-like fold and mediates interaction with BRCA2. The domain is testis-specific and localises to chromosomes during meiosis. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: MDM34 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of MDM34 (Mitochondrial distribution and morphology protein 34), found in fungi. This domain is located between the N-terminal SMP-LTD domain and a large disordered region at the C-terminal. MDM34 is a component of the ERMES complex, which serves as a molecular tether connecting the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. The full-length protein is required for interaction between ER-resident mmm1 and outer mitochondrial membrane mdm10, and functions in nonvesicular lipid trafficking between organelles [[PMID:21876677]]. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: DNAJC15 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of DNAJC15 (DnaJ homolog subfamily C member 15), found in metazoa. This domain precedes a transmembrane region and the C-terminal J-domain. DNAJC15 is a mitochondrial inner membrane protein that acts as a negative regulator of the mitochondrial respiratory chain and prevents mitochondrial hyperpolarisation [[PMID:23530063]]. The protein functions as an import component of the TIM23 translocase complex and stimulates the ATPase activity of HSPA9 [[PMID:23263864]]. The protein is highly expressed in heart, liver and kidney tissues. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase 21.3 kDa subunit C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the NADH-ubiquinone oxidoreductase 21.3 kDa subunit (NUJM), found in fungi. This domain follows three transmembrane regions in the protein sequence. NUJM is a subunit of mitochondrial respiratory complex I, which catalyses the transfer of electrons from NADH to ubiquinone whilst translocating protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane [[PMID:1825789]]. Complex I is composed of approximately 40 different subunits. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined. This domain seems not to be present in NUJM from related fungi species.

Name: Cullin-associated NEDD8-dissociated protein 1 N-terminal part
Description: This entry represents the TPR (tetratricopeptide repeat) domain found in CandA-N (Cullin-associated NEDD8-dissociated protein 1 N-terminal part). CandA-N is an assembly factor of SCF E3 ubiquitin ligase complexes that promotes exchange of F-box subunits. In most species, CandA-N is known as CANDA1 and it exists as a single protein, but in Aspergillus nidulans it is split into two separate proteins: candA-N (this entry) and candA-C (Q5BAH2), corresponding to the N-terminal and C-terminal portions respectively [[PMID:21119001]]. The full-length CAND1 protein interacts with unneddylated cullins. The specific function of this TPR domain remains to be determined.

Name: Gamma carbonic anhydrase 1, mitochondrial C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of gamma-class carbonic anhydrases (GAMMACA1) found primarily in plants. The full-length proteins function as homotrimers and are involved in the catabolism of bicarbonate but do not mediate the reversible hydration of carbon dioxide [[PMID:19808034]]. They require zinc as cofactors and are located in mitochondria, specifically as components of the respiratory chain complex I [[PMID:15604675],[PMID:16407270]]. The proteins mediate complex I assembly and respiration [[PMID:15935378]]. In Arabidopsis, GAMMACA2 is required for male fertility during anther development and dehiscence [[PMID:15935378]]. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Nucleoporin NUP37 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal beta-propeller domain of nucleoporin NUP37, a component of the nuclear pore complex (NPC). The domain contains multiple WD repeats that form a beta-propeller structure. NUP37 is part of the 55-60 MDa NPC, which constitutes the exclusive means of nucleocytoplasmic transport [[PMID:21784248]]. The NPC allows passive diffusion of small molecules and active transport of macromolecules such as proteins, RNAs and ribosomal subunits across the nuclear envelope. NUP37 interacts with other nucleoporins including NUP120. The specific function of this N-terminal beta-propeller domain remains to be determined.

Name: Mitochondrial ribosomal protein mS27
Description: This entry represents mitochondrial ribosomal protein mS27, a component of the small ribosomal subunit (37S) of mitochondrial ribosomes (mitoribosomes) in filamentous fungi such as Neurospora crassa. The mitoribosome is a dedicated translation machinery responsible for synthesis of mitochondrial genome-encoded proteins, including essential transmembrane subunits of the mitochondrial respiratory chain [[PMID:33056988]]. The 37S small subunit contains a 16S ribosomal RNA and 32 different proteins. Mitoribosomes are attached to the mitochondrial inner membrane and translation products are cotranslationally integrated into the membrane. This protein belongs to the mitochondrion-specific ribosomal protein mS27 family.

Name: Nucleoporin NUP53 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of nucleoporin NUP53, a component of the nuclear pore complex (NPC) found in eukaryotes. NUP53 functions as both a structural component of the NPC and as a docking partner for nuclear transport factors [[PMID:21784248]]. The protein is part of a tetrameric module comprising NUP192-NUP170-NIC96-NUP53 or NUP188- NUP170-NIC96-NUP53. NUP53 contains FG repeats that serve as interaction sites for karyopherins and form an affinity gradient guiding transport proteins through the NPC. The protein shows symmetric distribution at both cytoplasmic and nucleoplasmic sides of the nuclear membrane. While the full-length NUP53 protein is involved in nucleocytoplasmic transport, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: PGC-1 and ERR-induced regulator in muscle protein 1
Description: This entry represents the PGC-1 and ERR-induced regulator in muscle protein 1 (PERM1) family. This protein is found in mammals and is highly expressed in skeletal muscle and heart. PERM1 regulates the expression of selective PPARGC1A/B and ESRRA/B/G target genes with roles in glucose and lipid metabolism, energy transfer, contractile function, muscle mitochondrial biogenesis and oxidative capacity [[PMID:23836911]]. The protein localises to both cytoplasm and nucleus, showing nuclear localisation in the presence of PPARGC1A. PERM1 is induced by PPARGC1A, PPARGC1B, ESRRA, ESRRB and ESRRG, and its muscle-specific expression is increased by endurance exercise.

Name: Coiled-coil domain-containing protein 172
Description: This entry represents the coiled-coil domain-containing protein 172 (CCDC172) family, also known as TEKT2-binding protein 1. This protein is found in vertebrates and contains multiple coiled-coil domains. CCDC172 localises to the cytoplasm and cilia, particularly in the sperm midpiece where it is concentrated at the mitochondrial sheath of flagella and colocalises with TEKT2 at the perinuclear region [[PMID:24394471]]. The protein is predominantly expressed in testis and spermatozoa, with expression increasing after 3 weeks of postnatal development. CCDC172 may interact with TEKT2, suggesting a role in sperm structure and function.

Name: ANTKMT N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of adenine nucleotide translocase lysine N-methyltransferase (ANTKMT). This domain includes an N-terminal sequence (NTS) and a transmembrane region that is responsible for mitochondrial localisation. ANTKMT is a mitochondrial protein-lysine N-methyltransferase that trimethylates adenine nucleotide translocases ANT2/SLC25A5 and ANT3/SLC25A6, thereby regulating mitochondrial respiration. The enzyme contains a methyltransferase domain that uses S-adenosyl-L-methionine as a cofactor to perform lysine trimethylation [[PMID:31213526]]. The proteins are localised to the mitochondrial membrane and are found in vertebrates including humans and mice. The specific function of this N-terminal domain is for mitochondrial targeting and membrane localisation.

Name: MAP-homologous protein 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of MAP-homologous protein 1 (MHP1). This domain is found at the C-terminal region of MHP1, which is an essential protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae required for microtubule function. MHP1 is essential for the formation and stabilisation of microtubules, binds to microtubules in vitro, and localises to cytoplasmic microtubules and mitotic spindles. The protein contains leucine-rich repeat regions and extensive disordered regions, with a Tau/MAP repeat-like region at the C-terminal end. MHP1 undergoes extensive phosphorylation and ubiquitination modifications [[PMID:8947554]]. The protein belongs to the GIP3/HER1 family and is found in fungi. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Biotrophy-associated secreted protein 4
Description: This entry represents the biotrophy-associated secreted protein 4 (BAS4) found in plant pathogenic fungi. The prototype protein from Pyricularia oryzae (rice blast fungus) is a 102-amino acid secreted effector protein with a predicted signal peptide for secretion. BAS4 is involved in biotrophic colonisation of plant cells and plays a role in the transition from biotrophic to necrotrophic infection phases [[PMID:19357089],[PMID:31049006]]. The protein elicits rice basic defence responses during early infection and promotes cell death during late compatible interactions [[PMID:19357089]]. BAS4 localises to the extra-invasive hyphal membrane matrix and is highly upregulated in invasive hyphae [[PMID:19357089],[PMID:31049006]]. The protein undergoes N-glycosylation and is found across various fungal taxa including Sordariomycetes. The protein represents an important class of fungal effectors that manipulate host plant defences during pathogenesis.

Name: SIL1/FES1/HPBP1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of nucleotide exchange factor proteins, including SIL1, FES1 and HPBP1. SIL1 functions as a nucleotide exchange factor for the ER lumenal chaperone HSPA5 and is required for protein translocation and folding in the endoplasmic reticulum [[PMID:12356756],[PMID:11101517]]. The C-terminal region contains a degron motif (-Lys-Xaa-Xaa-Arg) that is recognised by CRL2(FEM1A) and CRL2(FEM1C) E3 ubiquitin ligase complexes, leading to protein degradation. FES1 is involved in protein translation, propagation of [PSI+] prions, and polyamine tolerance. It is required for fully efficient Hsp70-mediated folding of proteins [[PMID:12417338],[PMID:15082786],[PMID:16374585],[PMID:17132105]]. HPBP1, a SIL1 paralogue and FES1 orthologue, inhibits HSPA1A chaperone activity by changing the conformation of the ATP-binding domain of HSPA1A and interfering with ATP binding [[PMID:12651857]]. This domain adopts a TPR-like fold that in HSPBP1 is required to bring about the mentioned conformational change [[PMID:12651857]].

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8399)
Description: This domain is found in uncharacterised proteins mainly from fungi. It shares similarity with known ARM domains. It is frequently found next to an RRM domain.

Name: Platelet glycoprotein Ib beta chain C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal region of platelet glycoprotein Ib beta chain. This is a transmembrane helix located at the C-terminal of the leucine-rich repeat region [[PMID:3353370],[PMID:21908432]]. Platelet glycoprotein Ib beta is a component of the GP1b-IX-V complex that participates in platelet adhesion by binding to von Willebrand factor [[PMID:21908432]]. Mutations in this protein can lead to Bernard-Soulier syndrome, a bleeding disorder affecting platelet function.

Name: Protein PET54 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain found in protein PET54 from Saccharomyces cerevisiae and related fungi. PET54 is a mitochondrial translational activator protein that functions in RNA processing and translation regulation [[PMID:2555177]]. The protein is localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a peripheral membrane protein. PET54 is involved in the excision of intron aI5-beta from pre-mRNA for cytochrome c oxidase I and plays a role in promoting the translation of COX3. The protein contains an RNA recognition motif and binds to mRNA 5'-UTR regions and pre-mRNA intronic sequences. The function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Mitochondrial fission factor interactor C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of mitochondrial fission factor interactor (MFI), also known as C11orf65. This C-terminal domain is part of a protein that acts as an inhibitor of mitochondrial fission. While the full-length MFI protein interacts with mitochondrial fission factor (MFF) and prevents dynamin-1-like protein (DNM1L) recruitment to mitochondria, promoting a more fused mitochondrial network [[PMID:30059978]], the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined. The full-length protein is predominantly localised to the cytosol with a minor fraction at the outer mitochondrial membrane and is enriched in pancreatic beta cells and testis.

Name: Outer envelope pore protein 24
Description: This entry represents a family of outer envelope pore proteins found in plant chloroplasts and plastids. These proteins are approximately 213-224 amino acids in length and form high-conductance voltage-dependent solute channels with slight selectivity for cations. They transport triosephosphates, dicarboxylic acids, ATP, inorganic phosphate, sugars, and amino acids across the chloroplast outer membrane. The proteins form homooligomeric pores and belong to the plastid outer envelope porin family (TC 1.B.28.1). They contain 12 transmembrane beta strands and are present in both chloroplasts and etioplasts, including non-green root plastids. This family is found throughout land plants.

Name: Exportin-4
Description: This entry represents Exportin-4 and related nuclear transport receptors. Exportin-4 mediates the nuclear export of proteins by binding cooperatively to cargo proteins and to Ran-GTP in the nucleus. The protein contains armadillo repeat-like structures that form the characteristic solenoid fold typical of nuclear transport receptors. This TPR-like structure likely contributes to the protein-protein interactions required for cargo recognition and transport complex assembly.

Name: Pectate-lyase like beta helix domain
Description: This entry represents a family of beta helix domains primarily found in Pseudomonadati species of bacteria. Proteins usually contain at least two copies of this domain, but some have as many as five copies. These proteins often contain an N-terminal lipoprotein attachment motif suggesting they are anchored to the bacterial cell membrane and may have a role in adherence or processing of carbohydrate substrates.

Name: PAB middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle domain of the PAB (protein IN CHLOROPLAST ATPASE BIOGENESIS). PAB is a chloroplast stromal protein that functions as an assembly chaperone involved in the assembly of the F1 ATP synthase in chloroplast thylakoid membranes. The protein functions downstream of the CPN60 chaperones to promote assembly of the catalytically active core of the chloroplast ATP synthase and assists the assembly of the ATP synthase gamma subunit into the active F1 core. PAB interacts with ATPC1 and is critical for the biogenesis of the chloroplast ATP synthase. This middle domain is found in land plants, particularly in eudicotyledons and monocotyledons, where it may contribute to the structural organisation of the PAB protein. The specific function of this middle domain within the overall PAB protein architecture remains to be determined.

Name: PAB C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the PAB (protein IN CHLOROPLAST ATPASE BIOGENESIS). PAB is a chloroplast stromal protein that functions as an assembly chaperone involved in the assembly of the F1 ATP synthase in chloroplast thylakoid membranes. The protein functions downstream of the CPN60 chaperones to promote assembly of the catalytically active core of the chloroplast ATP synthase and assists the assembly of the ATP synthase gamma subunit into the active F1 core. PAB interacts with ATPC1 and is critical for the biogenesis of the chloroplast ATP synthase. This domain is found in land plants, particularly in eudicotyledons and monocotyledons, where it may contribute to the structural organisation of the PAB protein. The specific function of this domain within the overall PAB protein architecture remains to be determined [[PMID:25775508]].ED A0A4P1RHR1.1/114-142;

Name: Rv1828-like, C-terminal domain
Description: This is the C-terminal domain of Uncharacterized HTH-type transcriptional regulator Rv1828 from Mycobacterium tuberculosis and related proteins. Rv1828 is a transcriptional regulator that binds to its own promoter and thus may play a role in the regulation of the cotranscribed genes Rv1827 and Rv1828. It can also bind several promoter regions of genes that are essential, including ftsZ. It binds to the imperfect everted repeat sequence CTCAA through its N-terminal winged-HTH motif ([pfam:PF13411]) [[PMID:30306715]]. This domain corresponds to the effector binding domain and mediates dimer formation of Rv1828 [[PMID:30306715]].

Name: Subtelomeric hrmA-associated cluster protein
Description: This entry represents a family of proteins found in the subtelomeric hrmA-associated cluster (HAC) in Aspergillus fumigatus. These proteins are part of a gene cluster that alters hyphal surface properties, including reduced total chitin and increased beta-glucan exposure [[PMID:31548684]]. The proteins contribute to perturbation of inter-hyphal interactions within developing biofilms, resulting in loss of vertically aligned polarised growing filaments [[PMID:31548684]]. This leads to formation of a hypoxia-typic morphotype (H-MORPH) with altered biofilm architecture that increases hypoxia fitness and enhances pathogenicity in invasive aspergillosis [[PMID:31548684]]. Expression of genes in this cluster is regulated by the hypoxia responsive morphology factor A (hrmA) [[PMID:31548684]]. The specific molecular function of individual proteins in this family remains to be determined.

Name: GIGANTEA protein C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of GIGANTEA proteins found in plants. The full-length GIGANTEA protein is involved in regulation of circadian rhythm and photoperiodic flowering [[PMID:10469647],[PMID:10920210]]. GIGANTEA belongs to a distinct protein family and is localised to both the nucleus and cytoplasm [[PMID:10920210]]. The protein stabilises photoreceptors ADO1/ZTL and ADO3, and interacts with multiple transcription factors and regulatory proteins via its N-terminal region [[PMID:17704763]]. GIGANTEA shows circadian expression patterns with peak levels occurring 8-10 hours into the light period [[PMID:17704763]]. A crystal structure is available for a large portion of the Arabidopsis protein (PDB:7WA4) showing predominantly helical architecture [[PMID:10469647]]. While the full-length protein has well-characterised roles in circadian clock regulation and flowering control, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Mitochondrial nucleoid-associated protein 1 zinc finger domain
Description: This entry represents a small zinc finger domain found at the N-terminal region of mitochondrial nucleoid-associated protein 1 (MTNAP1) in vertebrates. The full-length MTNAP1 protein is a critical regulator of mitochondrial DNA abundance that binds double-stranded DNA throughout the mitochondrial genome without sequence specificity [[PMID:37676315]]. MTNAP1 controls mitochondrial DNA copy number by promoting its replication and plays important roles in mitochondrial metabolism and cell proliferation [[PMID:37676315]]. The protein is localised to mitochondrial nucleoids in the matrix and is retained in nucleoids under mitochondrial DNA replication stress [[PMID:37676315],[PMID:37401363]]. MTNAP1 is a single-pass type I membrane protein anchored in the mitochondrial inner membrane [[PMID:37401363]]. While the full-length protein has established functions in mitochondrial DNA regulation, the specific role of this N-terminal zinc finger domain remains to be determined.

Name: SusR N-terminal TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal periplasmic domain of the SusR protein. This region of SusR is involved in sensing maltose levels and then transduces this signal to the C-terminal DNA-binding domain to promote transcript of the Sus operon.

Name: Required for respiratory growth protein 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the required for respiratory growth protein 1 (RRG1). RRG1 is essential for respiratory growth and required for mitochondrial protein synthesis and vacuolar acidification [[PMID:16168084],[PMID:19751518]]. The protein is localised to the mitochondrion and belongs to the RRG1 family. RRG1 is found in fungi including Saccharomyces cerevisiae and related yeasts. The full-length protein is N-glycosylated, which is important for correct localisation. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Genetic interactor of prohibitins 3 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of genetic interactor of prohibitins 3 (GEP3). GEP3 is a mitochondrial protein that may be involved in mitochondrial lipid metabolism. The protein belongs to the TRAFAC class YlqF/YawG GTPase family, GEP3 subfamily. The full-length protein contains a CP-type G domain responsible for GTP binding and hydrolysis. GEP3 is found in fungi and contains a mitochondrial transit peptide for targeting to the mitochondrion. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Maltose excess protein 1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of maltose excess protein 1 (MEX1). MEX1 is a maltose transporter essential for the conversion of starch to sucrose in leaves at night, via the export of maltose from the chloroplast [[PMID:14704427]]. The protein is localised to the chloroplast inner membrane and contains multiple transmembrane helices. MEX1 is also required for root cap cell formation [[PMID:10536027]]. The protein is found in plants and contains a chloroplast transit peptide for targeting to the plastid. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: YGL041W-A C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of YGL041W-A and orthologous proteins found in fungi. YGL041W-A is an uncharacterised mitochondrial protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae. The protein contains a predicted mitochondrial transit peptide at the N-terminal and this domain is located at the C-terminal after a disordered region. The domain is found predominantly in fungal species, particularly within the Dikarya. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: SKIP35 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of SKIP35 (SKP1- interacting partner 35) and related proteins found in plants. SKIP35 is an ankyrin repeat protein that interacts with SKP1A/ASK1, a component of SCF ubiquitin E3 ligase complexes. The protein contains multiple ankyrin repeats in its C-terminal region, while this N-terminal domain precedes the ankyrin repeat region. The domain is found predominantly in land plants (Embryophyta). The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:12795696]].

Name: C3H7.08c C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of C3H7.08c and orthologous proteins found in fungi. C3H7.08c is an uncharacterised single-pass membrane protein in Schizosaccharomyces pombe. The protein contains a transmembrane helix and a disordered region, with this domain located at the C-terminal. The protein has been associated with mitochondrial proton-transporting ATP synthase complex assembly based on GO annotations. The domain is found predominantly in fungal species, particularly within the Ascomycota. The function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Plastid-specific ribosomal protein 5 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of large ribosomal subunit protein cL37. The domain spans approximately 54 residues and is located after a disordered region in the protein sequence. Protein cL37 is a component of the chloroplast large ribosomal subunit (50S) and exists in multiple forms (alpha, beta, gamma) due to different transit peptide cleavage sites [[PMID:10874046]]. The chloroplast ribosome is responsible for translating chloroplast genome-encoded proteins including components of the photosynthetic apparatus [[PMID:28007896]]. While the full-length cL37 protein functions in plastid translation, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Senescence-associated protein OSA15 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents a coiled-coil domain found in senescence- associated proteins of the ATA15/OSA15 family from plants. The domain spans approximately 89 residues and is located in the N-terminal region of these chloroplast-localised proteins. OSA15 proteins are up-regulated during leaf senescence and are involved in modulating redox homeostasis in response to age and stress factors [[PMID:12650614],[PMID:21940719]]. The proteins are induced by abscisic acid, salicylate, methyl jasmonate and ethylene, and their function is dependent on ethylene signalling pathways. While the full-length OSA15 proteins regulate leaf senescence and oxidative stress responses, the specific function of this coiled-coil domain remains to be determined.

Name: Meiosis inhibitor protein 1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of meiosis inhibitor protein 1 (MEI1), a vertebrate-specific protein required for normal meiotic chromosome synapsis. This domain is absent in some alternative splice isoforms. MEI1 is expressed predominantly in testis and is involved in the formation of meiotic double-strand breaks in spermatocytes [[PMID:14668445]]. Variants in MEI1 are associated with azoospermia and hydatidiform moles, causing defects in meiotic progression [[PMID:16683055]]. While the full-length MEI1 protein functions in meiotic chromosome synapsis and double-strand break formation, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Protein CPR-5 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of protein CPR-5 (constitutive expression of pathogenesis-related genes 5), a negative regulator of plant programmed cell death and effector-triggered immunity. The domain spans approximately 72 residues and follows the transmembrane regions of the protein. CPR5 is a multi-pass membrane protein localised to the nucleus membrane that controls senescence, chlorotic lesions, and pathogen defence responses [[PMID:11846876]]. The protein interacts with SIM and SMR1 proteins and plays a role in cell cycle regulation and transcriptional processes [[PMID:25455564]]. While the full-length CPR5 protein regulates programmed cell death and immunity, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Outer envelope pore protein 37
Description: This entry represents the OEP37 (Outer Envelope Pore protein 37) which is chloroplast outer membrane porins found in plants. OEP37 is a 343 amino acid voltage-dependent peptide-sensitive high conductance rectifying cation channel. OEP37 functions as an ion channel with strong affinity for TIC32 that is imported into the chloroplast [[PMID:16624824]]. The protein forms an hourglass-shaped multimeric complex and exhibits pH-dependent conductance that decreases with decreasing pH values [[PMID:16624824]]. OEP37 contains a chloroplast transit peptide (residues 1-73) and 12 transmembrane beta strands that span the chloroplast outer membrane. The protein is ubiquitously expressed at low levels, with highest accumulation in cotyledons, seedlings and embryos [[PMID:16624824]].

Name: SKIP35 C-terminal ankyrin repeat domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal ankyrin repeat domain found in SKIP35 (SKP1-interacting partner 35) proteins from plants. This domain contains six ankyrin repeats (residues 292-478 in Arabidopsis thaliana SKIP35) that are located at the C-terminal region of the 611 amino acid protein. SKIP35 interacts with SKP1A/ASK1, a component of SCF ubiquitin E3 ligase complexes [[PMID:12795696]]. While the full-length SKIP35 protein is involved in protein-protein interactions within the ubiquitin-proteasome system, the specific function of this C-terminal ankyrin repeat domain remains to be determined.

Name: STR4 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents an N-terminal helix-turn-helix domain found in STR4 (Sulfurtransferase 4) proteins from plants. STR4 is also known as TROL (Thylakoid Rhodanese-Like) and is a 466 amino acid chloroplast protein that contains a rhodanese domain (residues 144-250), two transmembrane helices, and transit peptides for chloroplast and thylakoid targeting. STR4/TROL is required for anchoring ferredoxin- NADP reductase to thylakoid membranes and sustaining efficient linear electron flow in photosystem II [[PMID:19682289]]. The protein is a component of high molecular weight thylakoid complexes and is localised to the non-appressed regions of the thylakoid membrane [[PMID:19682289]]. While the full- length STR4 protein functions in photosynthetic electron transport, the specific function of this N-terminal helix-turn-helix domain remains to be determined.

Name: SUV2 middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle TPR domain of SUV2 (SENSITIVE TO UV 2) proteins found in plants. SUV2 is a putative plant ATRIP (ATR interacting protein) orthologue involved in DNA damage response and cell cycle checkpoint control [[PMID:19619159],[PMID:19619158],[PMID:28556304]]. The full-length SUV2 protein is required for tolerance to DNA-damaging agents such as UV-B irradiation, gamma-radiation, and hydroxyurea, and is involved in cell-cycle G2/M arrest in response to DNA damage [[PMID:19619159],[PMID:19619158]]. The full-length protein contains a coiled-coil domain and a C-terminal phosphatase tensin-type domain, and forms multimers through the coiled-coil region [[PMID:19619159]]. The specific function of this middle domain remains to be determined.

Name: Halfway C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Halfway proteins found in Drosophila species. The full-length Halfway proteins are involved in the ecdysone-induced developmental cascade and have a role in the indirect control of late ecdysone genes [[PMID:15189568]]. Experimental evidence shows that mutations in these proteins specifically interrupt transmission of regulatory signals from early to late ecdysone inducible genes. This C-terminal domain is found downstream of leucine-rich repeat regions and LRRNT/LRRCT domains. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Y3IP1/CEST C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Y3IP1/CEST proteins found in plants. This domain contains a single transmembrane helix that anchors these proteins to the chloroplast thylakoid membrane. The full-length Y3IP1/CEST proteins are involved in photosystem I assembly [[PMID:20807881]] and stress tolerance responses [[PMID:20876334]]. Y3IP1 cooperates with the plastid-encoded Ycf3 protein in assembling stable photosystem I units, whilst CEST enhances tolerance to multiple environmental stresses and reduces photooxidative damage. The specific function of this C-terminal transmembrane domain remains to be determined.

Name: Dynein axonemal intermediate chain 3 WD40 domain
Description: This entry represents the WD40 beta-propeller domain found in dynein axonemal intermediate chain 3 (DNAI3). This domain contains multiple WD40 repeats that form a beta-propeller structure. The domain is found in the C-terminal region of DNAI3 proteins from vertebrates. DNAI3 is part of the multisubunit axonemal dynein complex and may be involved in cilia function regulation. The protein acts as a negative regulator of cell migration through inhibition of Arp2/3 complex-mediated actin polymerisation. While the full-length DNAI3 protein has established functions in dynein complex formation and actin regulation, the specific function of this WD40 domain remains to be determined.

Name: PRDA1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the PRDA1 protein family found in plants. PRDA1 is a chloroplast nucleoid protein essential for early chloroplast development and regulation of plastid gene expression. The full-length protein localises to chloroplast nucleoids and interacts with FSD2 and MRL7 proteins. PRDA1 is required for maintaining the redox state around chloroplast nucleoids and may positively regulate plastid-encoded RNA polymerase activity [[PMID:24132784]]. This C-terminal region contains predicted disordered sequences rich in basic and acidic residues. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: NUP214 coiled-coil domain
Description: This entry represents a coiled-coil domain found within the NUP214 nucleoporin in plants. NUP214 is a component of the nuclear pore complex essential for embryogenesis and seed viability. The complete protein contains extensive FG repeat regions that mediate nuclear transport by interacting with transport factors. NUP214 is required for normal embryo development, regulating cell divisions and apical-basal axis formation [[PMID:21189294],[PMID:22898497]]. This coiled-coil domain is located within the central region of the large 1819-amino acid protein and likely contributes to protein-protein interactions within the nuclear pore complex. The specific function of this coiled-coil domain remains to be determined.

Name: SUV2 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal TPR domain of the SUV2 protein found in plants. The complete SUV2 protein is required for tolerance to DNA damage and functions in cell cycle G2/M arrest responses. SUV2 is involved in aluminium-dependent gene regulation and root growth inhibition through ATR-dependent pathways [[PMID:19619159],[PMID:19619158]]. While the complete protein has phosphoprotein phosphatase activity, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Plastid transcriptionally active protein 12
Description: This entry represents the plastid transcriptionally active protein 12 (PTAC12) family, found in plants. These proteins are approximately 500-600 amino acids in length and contain chloroplast transit peptides at their N-termini. PTAC12 is a component of the transcriptionally active chromosome (TAC) complex and the plastid-encoded RNA polymerase (PEP) complex in chloroplasts [[PMID:24246379],[PMID:25599833]]. The protein is required for the activity of plastid-encoded RNA polymerase and full expression of genes transcribed by PEP [[PMID:24246379],[PMID:25599833]]. PTAC12 binds single-stranded DNA and RNA but not double-stranded DNA [[PMID:25599833]]. The protein localises to both chloroplast stroma and the nucleus [[PMID:25599833]]. In Arabidopsis, PTAC12 is also known as HEMERA and plays additional roles in photomorphogenesis by mediating phytochrome localisation and promoting degradation of phytochrome- interacting factors [[PMID:24246379]]. Loss of PTAC12 results in pale yellow-green leaf phenotypes and defects in plastid gene expression [[PMID:24246379],[PMID:25599833]].

Name: Mitochondrial uncharacterised protein UMP2
Description: This entry represents the mitochondrial uncharacterised protein UMP2 family, found in plants. These proteins are approximately 113 amino acids in length and contain N-terminal mitochondrial transit peptides that are cleaved after residue 14. The mature proteins localise to mitochondria and have been identified in mitochondrial proteome studies. UMP2 proteins contain a disordered region and a C-terminal helical region. The proteins have been detected in respiratory chain complex I, suggesting a potential role in mitochondrial respiration. Three-dimensional structures have been determined by cryo-electron microscopy, revealing the helical C-terminal domain structure. The family is distributed across plant species within the Viridiplantae. The specific function of these proteins remains to be determined.

Name: PTAC6 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of PTAC6 (PLASTID TRANSCRIPTIONALLY ACTIVE protein 6), found in land plants. This protein contains a chloroplast transit peptide (residues 1-59) that directs the protein to chloroplasts [[PMID:16326926],[PMID:33001465]]. The N-terminal region includes a disordered region and low complexity sequences. PTAC6 is an essential component of the plastid-encoded RNA polymerase complex involved in plastid gene expression and chloroplast biogenesis. The protein exhibits dual localisation between nucleus and chloroplast, linking photomorphogenesis with chloroplast development [[PMID:33001465]]. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: PTAC6 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of PTAC6 (PLASTID TRANSCRIPTIONALLY ACTIVE protein 6), found in land plants. This protein contains a chloroplast transit peptide (residues 1-59) that directs the protein to chloroplasts [[PMID:16326926],[PMID:33001465]]. PTAC6 is an essential component of the plastid-encoded RNA polymerase complex involved in plastid gene expression and chloroplast biogenesis. The protein exhibits dual localisation between nucleus and chloroplast, linking photomorphogenesis with chloroplast development [[PMID:33001465]]. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: PAC middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle domain of PAC (Protein PALE CRESS) and related proteins found in plants. PAC is a chloroplast-localised protein required for differentiation of chloroplasts from proplastids or etioplasts and is involved in chloroplast mRNA processing and maturation [[PMID:7919990],[PMID:9648738]]. The protein contains RNA binding activity and is essential for proper leaf development and chloroplast organisation. This domain is found in the central region of PAC proteins. The specific function of this middle domain remains to be determined.

Name: PAC N-terminal coiled-coil domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal coiled-coil domain found in PAC (Protein PALE CRESS) and related proteins from plants. PAC is a chloroplast-localised protein required for differentiation of chloroplasts from proplastids or etioplasts and is involved in chloroplast mRNA processing and maturation [[PMID:7919990],[PMID:9648738]]. The protein contains RNA binding activity and is essential for proper leaf development and chloroplast organisation. This coiled-coil domain is located in the N-terminal region following the chloroplast transit peptide. The specific function of this N-terminal coiled-coil domain remains to be determined.

Name: STR4A N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of STR4A (sulfurtransferase 4A) and related proteins found in plants. STR4A is a rhodanese-like domain-containing protein localised to the chloroplast, where it is associated with membranes as a single-pass membrane protein [[PMID:10819329],[PMID:17408957]]. The protein contains a rhodanese domain in its C-terminal region and belongs to the sulfurtransferase family. STR4A is part of the chloroplast thylakoid membrane system and may be involved in sulfur metabolism. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: LasA, N-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the N-terminal end of Protease LasA from Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which is involved in proteolysis and elastolysis (degradation of the host protein elastin). LasA has staphylolytic activity (degrades pentaglycine cross-links in cell wall peptidoglycan) [[PMID:8932318],[PMID:2110137],[PMID:1597429],[PMID:11179971]] . Its degradation is likely to contribute to the pathogenicity of the organism. This domain, which is usually found associated with [pfam:PF01551], is predicted to adopt a mostly alpha helical structure.

Name: CTF3 middle TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the middle tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in the CTF3 (CENP-I) protein. CTF3 is a component of the constitutive centromere-associated network (CCAN), which forms the inner kinetochore platform for outer kinetochore assembly [[PMID:22561346]]. The protein forms a heterotrimeric complex with MCM16 and MCM22. The full-length CTF3 protein is required for establishing bipolar spindle-microtubule attachments and proper chromosome segregation. CTF3 contains an N-terminal HEAT repeat domain and this middle TPR domain region. This family is found in fungi, particularly within the Saccharomycetaceae.

Name: MCD1 chloroplast division domain
Description: This entry represents the MCD1 (MULTIPLE CHLOROPLAST DIVISION SITE 1) domain, a plant-specific protein domain found in the chloroplast inner membrane. MCD1 is required for chloroplast division and determines the site of division in concert with MIND1 [[PMID:19135368],[PMID:29967285]]. The protein localises to a ring structure at the chloroplast constriction site. The domain contains a single transmembrane helix (approximately residues 117-139) with the N-terminal region in the chloroplast intermembrane space and the C-terminal region in the stroma [[PMID:19135368],[PMID:29967285]]. MCD1 interacts with MIND1 in the stroma and associates with FtsZ filaments via the membrane-tethering protein ARC6 to regulate FtsZ ring positioning [[PMID:29967285]]. The C-terminal region contains a disordered segment (residues 315-349). This domain is found in land plants across eudicotyledons and monocotyledons.

Name: SCO2 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of SCO2 (SNOWY COTYLEDON 2), a protein disulphide-isomerase found in the chloroplast thylakoid membrane of plants. This C-terminal region is designated as CR-type-like and contains two CXXCXGXG motifs, which are similar to CR-type zinc-fingers. The protein binds zinc and catalyses the rearrangement of disulphide bonds in proteins [[PMID:17921316]]. SCO2 is involved in chloroplast development in cotyledons and interacts with LHCB1 proteins during thylakoid biogenesis [[PMID:22040291]]. The protein is related to the DnaJ family but lacks the J domain. This domain is found in land plants across eudicotyledons and monocotyledons.

Name: Gloeobacter/Verrucomicrobia repeat
Description: This entry represents a repeat found originally in the genus Gloeobacter and in Verrucomicrobia, and named Gloeo_Verruco for that reason. However, the species distribution is broader. Multiple members of the entry have C-terminal sorting signals for recognition and processing by either a type IX secretion system (T9SS) or an exosortase, showing that this repeat has the choice-of-anchor character of a number of putative surface domains. According to structure predictions, these repeats may adopt a beta-propeller structure.

Name: PnsB4 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of PnsB4 (photosynthetic NDH subunit of subcomplex B 4), also known as NDF6. PnsB4 is a nucleus-encoded subunit of the chloroplast NAD(P)H dehydrogenase-like (NDH) complex found in terrestrial plants [[PMID:18535009],[PMID:21785130]]. The full-length protein is a single-pass thylakoid membrane protein that is a component of NDH subcomplex B, along with PnsB1, PnsB2, PnsB3 and PnsB5 [[PMID:18535009],[PMID:21785130]]. This N-terminal domain precedes the transmembrane helix. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: PnsB4 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of PnsB4 (photosynthetic NDH subunit of subcomplex B 4), also known as NDF6. PnsB4 is a nucleus-encoded subunit of the chloroplast NAD(P)H dehydrogenase-like (NDH) complex found in terrestrial plants [[PMID:18535009],[PMID:21785130]]. The full-length protein is a single-pass thylakoid membrane protein that is a component of NDH subcomplex B, along with PnsB1, PnsB2, PnsB3 and PnsB5 [[PMID:18535009],[PMID:21785130]]. This C-terminal domain follows the transmembrane helix. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: CRR41 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal helical bundle of CRR41 (chlororespiratory reduction 41), a nucleus-encoded biogenesis factor for the chloroplast NAD(P)H dehydrogenase-like (NDH) complex found in plants [[PMID:22274627]]. CRR41 is localised to the chloroplast stroma and is required for both formation and activity of the NDH complex [[PMID:22274627]]. The full-length protein functions in assembly or stabilisation of the NDH complex, probably involved together with NdhO and NdhH in the formation of an NDH subcomplex A assembly intermediate [[PMID:22274627]]. This C-terminal region contains a predicted coiled-coil motif. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: BSD2 N-terminal zinc-binding domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal zinc-binding domain of BSD2 (bundle sheath defective 2). BSD2 is a chloroplast chaperone required for RuBisCo biogenesis and translational regulation of the RuBisCo large subunit (RbcL) [[PMID:25124725]]. The full-length protein stabilises an end-state assembly intermediate of eight RbcL subunits until the small subunits become available to produce a complete stable RuBisCo complex containing eight small and eight large subunits. BSD2 associates with chloroplastic polysomes and is localised to the chloroplast stroma [[PMID:25124725]]. This N-terminal domain contains two zinc-binding sites coordinated by conserved cysteine residues, which are essential for chaperone activity [[PMID:25124725]]. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: DMAC2 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal helix-turn-helix domain of DMAC2 (distal membrane-arm assembly complex protein 2), also known as ATP synthase subunit s-like protein. DMAC2 is a mitochondrial protein required for the assembly of the NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase complex (complex I) [[PMID:27626371]]. The full-length protein is involved in the assembly of the distal region of complex I and interacts with incompletely assembled complex I [[PMID:27626371]]. DMAC2 belongs to the ATP synthase subunit s family and contains a leucine-rich repeat-like (LRR) domain superfamily fold. This N-terminal helix-turn-helix domain precedes the LRR-like region. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: PTAC10 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal helix-turn-helix domain of PTAC10 (plastid transcriptionally active 10), also known as PEP-associated protein 3 (PAP3). PTAC10 is a component of the transcriptionally active chromosome (TAC) complex in chloroplasts [[PMID:21949211]]. The full-length protein interacts with PTAC7 [[PMID:23082802]] and contains an S1 RNA-binding domain. PTAC10 is found in plants and is localised to the chloroplast. This N-terminal helix-turn- helix domain precedes the S1 motif. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: PTAC10 helical bundle domain
Description: This entry represents helical bundle domain of PTAC10 (plastid transcriptionally active 10), also known as PEP-associated protein 3 (PAP3). PTAC10 is a component of the transcriptionally active chromosome (TAC) complex in chloroplasts [[PMID:21949211]]. The full-length protein interacts with PTAC7 [[PMID:23082802]] and contains an S1 RNA-binding domain. PTAC10 is found in plants and is localised to the chloroplast. This domain precedes the S1 motif. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Colossin-D second immunoglobulin-like domain
Description: This entry represents the third immunoglobulin-like domain of Colossin-D. Colossin-D is a secreted protein found in Dictyostelium discoideum that belongs to the serine-aspartate repeat-containing protein (SDr) family. The protein contains multiple N-linked glycosylation sites. This third Ig-like domain is one of the two Ig-like fold domains present in the full-length protein. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8445)
Description: This entry represents a domain found in uncharacterised plant sequences which may adopt a structure similar to spectrin repeats.

Name: Topoisomerase C-terminal domain, Bacteroidota
Description: This entry represents a C-terminal domain-like domain found in topoisomerases and gyrases in Bacteroidota. The domain adopts a four-bladed beta-propeller structure. These proteins usually contain two copies of this domain. The domain is found primarily in Flavobacteriaceae, Chitinophagaceae, Sphingobacteriaceae and Bacteroidales families within the Bacteroidota phylum. C-terminal domains of topoisomerases are involved in DNA binding and substrate recognition. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Protein involved in starch initiation 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of protein involved in starch initiation 1 (PII1), also known as myosin-resembling chloroplast protein (MRC). PII1 is required for the initiation of starch granule biosynthesis in leaf chloroplasts and determines starch granule number and size. The protein interacts with PTST2 and SS4, interactions that are essential for starch granule initiation. PII1 contains multiple coiled-coil regions and localises to distinct puncta within chloroplasts. While PII1 is involved in starch biosynthesis regulation, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:30055112]].

Name: PII1 coiled-coil
Description: This entry represents a coiled-coil found in protein involved in starch initiation 1 (PII1), also known as myosin-resembling chloroplast protein (MRC). PII1 contains multiple coiled-coil regions, PII1 is required for the initiation of starch granule biosynthesis in leaf chloroplasts and interacts with PTST2 and SS4 proteins. The protein localises to distinct puncta within chloroplasts and is found in the soluble protein fraction of leaves. While PII1 is involved in starch granule initiation and determining granule number and size, the specific function of this coiled-coil domain remains to be determined [[PMID:29866647]].

Name: Ubiquinol-cytochrome c reductase subunit 11
Description: This entry represents the UQCR11/QCR10 family of small subunits of the ubiquinol-cytochrome c oxidoreductase complex (Complex III). This complex is part of the mitochondrial electron transport chain and catalyses electron transfer from ubiquinol to cytochrome c, linking this redox reaction to proton translocation across the mitochondrial inner membrane. The family consists of small transmembrane proteins of approximately 57-62 amino acids with a single transmembrane helix. UQCR11 has a role in Complex III assembly and stability of the Rieske iron-sulphur protein. The protein is localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane with domains in both the matrix and intermembrane space. The family is found in land plants [[PMID:12970493]].

Name: VPS18, alpha-solenoid domain, first half
Description: This entry represents the first half of the alpha-solenoid domain of VPS18 proteins. VPS18 proteins are involved in vesicle-mediated protein trafficking to lysosomal compartments, playing a crucial role in endocytic membrane transport and autophagic pathways. They act as a core component of the HOPS and CORVET endosomal tethering complexes, facilitating the conversion of Rab5 to Rab7 endosomes and mediating tethering and docking events during SNARE-mediated membrane fusion. The HOPS complex is recruited to Rab7 on the late endosomal membrane, regulating late endocytic, phagocytic, and autophagic traffic towards lysosomes. The CORVET complex functions as a Rab5 effector, mediating early endosome fusion [[PMID:12006978],[PMID:16000385],[PMID:29463724],[PMID:9065698],[PMID:27253064],[PMID:36098503],[PMID:38898033]]. These proteins are essential for processes such as vacuolar biogenesis, lysosome biogenesis, and the degradation of apoptotic cells. They also play roles in embryogenesis, root development, and dendrite development in various organisms [[PMID:10549280],[PMID:22160599]]. These proteins are essential for HOPS complex formation but also also show E3 ligase activity on their own [[PMID:36098503]].

Name: VPS18, alpha-solenoid domain, second half
Description: This entry represents the second half of the alpha-solenoid domain of VPS18 proteins. VPS18 proteins are involved in vesicle-mediated protein trafficking to lysosomal compartments, playing a crucial role in endocytic membrane transport and autophagic pathways. They act as a core component of the HOPS and CORVET endosomal tethering complexes, facilitating the conversion of Rab5 to Rab7 endosomes and mediating tethering and docking events during SNARE-mediated membrane fusion. The HOPS complex is recruited to Rab7 on the late endosomal membrane, regulating late endocytic, phagocytic, and autophagic traffic towards lysosomes. The CORVET complex functions as a Rab5 effector, mediating early endosome fusion [[PMID:12006978],[PMID:16000385],[PMID:29463724],[PMID:9065698],[PMID:24554766],[PMID:27253064],[PMID:28063257],[PMID:36098503],[PMID:38898033]]. These proteins are essential for processes such as vacuolar biogenesis, lysosome biogenesis, and the degradation of apoptotic cells. They also play roles in embryogenesis, root development, and dendrite development in various organisms. These proteins are essential for HOPS complex formation, but they also show E3 ligase activity on their own [[PMID:36098503]].

Name: RMD9 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal TPR domain of RMD9 family proteins. RMD9 proteins are mitochondrial RNA-binding proteins that bind the 3'-UTR of mitochondrial mRNAs and are involved in the processing or stability of mitochondrial mRNAs [[PMID:15229592]]. These proteins are found in yeasts including Saccharomyces cerevisiae, Candida glabrata and Kluyveromyces lactis. The full-length proteins are approximately 600-750 amino acids in length and localise to the mitochondrial inner membrane. The function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: RMD9 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal mitochondrial transit peptide TPR domain found in RMD9 proteins. RMD9 is an RNA-binding protein that localises to the mitochondrial inner membrane and binds the 3'-UTR of mitochondrial mRNAs. The transit peptide directs the protein to the mitochondrial matrix where it is cleaved off during import. The peptide varies in length from 12 residues in Eremothecium gossypii to 108 residues in Debaryomyces hansenii. RMD9 proteins are found throughout Ascomycota fungi, particularly in Saccharomycotina and some Pezizomycotina. The mature protein following transit peptide cleavage is involved in mitochondrial mRNA processing and stability. RMD9 proteins function as monomers and phosphorylation promotes RNA binding activity.

Name: NADH dehydrogenase [ubiquinone] 1 alpha subcomplex subunit 3
Description: This entry represents NADH dehydrogenase [ubiquinone] 1 alpha subcomplex subunit 3 (NDUFA3), an accessory subunit of mitochondrial respiratory chain Complex I [[PMID:36622099],[PMID:36952377]]. NDUFA3 is believed not to be directly involved in catalysis but is required for complex assembly and stability [[PMID:36622099],[PMID:36952377]]. Complex I functions in the transfer of electrons from NADH to ubiquinone in the respiratory chain. The protein contains a single transmembrane helix and is localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane [[PMID:36622099],[PMID:36952377]]. In Drosophila, this protein is also known as purriato and is required for maintenance of muscle integrity and cell proliferation in wing imaginal discs, possibly through interaction with the chaperone-assisted selective autophagy (CASA) pathway [[PMID:36968202]]. The protein shows weak sequence similarity to mammalian NDUFA3 but occupies the corresponding structural position in cryo-EM structures of Drosophila Complex I [[PMID:36622099],[PMID:36952377]]. This entry includes C.elegans uncharacterised protein K12H4.

Name: MDM34 helical domain
Description: This entry represents a helical domain found in Mdm34 (mitochondrial distribution and morphology protein 34). Mdm34 is a component of the ERMES (endoplasmic reticulum-mitochondria encounter structure) complex, which serves as a molecular tether connecting the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. The ERMES complex is composed of Mmm1, Mdm10, Mdm12 and Mdm34, and is involved in the control of mitochondrial shape and protein biogenesis, and functions in nonvesicular lipid trafficking between the ER and mitochondria. Mdm34 is localised to the mitochondrion outer membrane and contains an N-terminal SMP-LTD domain that can bind glycerophospholipids. This helical domain is found C-terminal to the SMP-LTD domain. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Actin-binding protein F N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of actin-binding protein F found in Dictyostelium discoideum. The full-length protein interacts with actin and is localised to both the nucleus and cytoskeleton, particularly at the centrosome. Actin-binding protein F is a large protein that plays a role in cytoskeletal organisation and cellular structure. The protein contains multiple coiled coil regions and disordered segments. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined, though it may contribute to the protein's actin-binding activity or subcellular localisation properties.

Name: Photosynthetic NDH subunit of subcomplex B 5 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain in PNSB5 found in land plants. PNSB5 is a component of the chloroplast NAD(P)H dehydrogenase (NDH) complex, specifically part of subcomplex B along with PnsB1, PnsB2, PnsB3 and PnsB4. The NDH complex shuttles electrons from NAD(P)H to plastoquinone via FMN and iron-sulphur centres in the photosynthetic electron transport chain. The protein is localised to the chloroplast membrane and contains two predicted transmembrane helices. PNSB5 is encoded in the nucleus and imported into the chloroplast via an N-terminal transit peptide [[PMID:19903870]].

Name: RMD9 middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle domain found in members of RMD9 proteins. RMD9 proteins are mitochondrial RNA-binding proteins that bind the 3'-UTR of mitochondrial mRNAs and are involved in the processing or stability of mitochondrial mRNAs. The proteins are localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane as peripheral membrane proteins on the matrix side. This domain is found in fungal species including Saccharomyces cerevisiae, Candida species, and other yeasts. The function of this domain remains to be determined [[PMID:23167605]].

Name: PnsB5 helical domain
Description: This entry represents the helical bundle of PnsB5 (photosynthetic NDH subunit of subcomplex B 5), also known as NDH18. PnsB5 is a nucleus-encoded subunit of the chloroplast NAD(P)H dehydrogenase-like (NDH) complex found in plants [[PMID:19903870],[PMID:21785130]]. The full-length protein is a multi-pass membrane protein localised to the chloroplast membrane and is a component of NDH subcomplex B, along with PnsB1, PnsB2, PnsB3 and PnsB4 [[PMID:19903870],[PMID:21785130]]. This helical bundle contains two transmembrane helices. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: PT7 toxin protein
Description: This entry represents the PT7 toxin protein from Bacillus cereus and related proteins. PIM7 is the cognate immunity protein of the polymorphic toxin PT7 which acts as a DNase. PT7 and PIM7 form a complex, in which PIM7 hinder the catalytic pocket [[PMID:39438720]]. The PT binding site in the PIM7 is negatively charged mimicking the negatively charged phosphate backbone of a DNA molecule. PT7 folds into an alpha/beta structure consisting of a four-stranded antiparallel beta-sheet packed with an alpha-helix [[PMID:39438720]].

Name: Toxin protein PT1
Description: This entry represents the toxin protein PT1 from E. marmotae and related proteins. PIM1 is the cognate immunity protein of the polymorphic toxin PT1 which acts as a DNase. PT1 and PIM1 form a complex, in which PIM1 hinder the PT1 catalytic pocket [[PMID:39438720]]. PT1 folds into an alpha-helical structure composed of three stranded beta-sheet and helices packed on one side of it.

Name: PT3 toxin domain
Description: This domain is found in Ralstonia solanacearum where it was shown to possess antibacterial and antifungal activity [[PMID:39438720]]. This domain is closely related to RhsP2 C-terminal toxin domain from Pseudomonas aeruginosa which is an ADP-ribosyltransferase and ADP-ribosylates 2'-hydroxyl groups of double-stranded RNA [[PMID:36070765]].

Name: PT8 toxin domain
Description: This domain is found in the toxin protein PT8 from Leptospira interrogans and related proteins. PT8 was shown to possess antibacterial and antifungal activity [[PMID:39438720]]. It is predicted to fold into a five-stranded beta-meander and contains two highly conserved aspartates and histidine residues. PIM8 is its immunity protein.

Name: Small ribosomal subunit protein mS46 middle domain
Description: This entry represents a domain found in mitochondrial ribosomal protein mS46 in fungi. The full-length mS46 protein is a component of the mitochondrial small ribosomal subunit (37S) of the 74S mitoribosome [[PMID:33056988]]. The mitoribosome is a dedicated translation machinery responsible for the synthesis of mitochondrial genome-encoded proteins, including essential transmembrane subunits of the mitochondrial respiratory chain. The mitoribosomes are attached to the mitochondrial inner membrane and translation products are cotranslationally integrated into the membrane [[PMID:33056988]]. The specific function of this domain within the mS46 protein remains to be determined.

Name: U10-hexatoxin-Hi1a
Description: This entry represents a family of small venom peptides found in spiders, exemplified by U10-hexatoxin-Hi1a from Hadronyche infensa. The family is found across orb-weaver spiders (Araneoidea) including members of the Nephilidae and Araneidae families. These peptides are approximately 100-130 amino acids in length and are secreted from venom glands. The proteins contain multiple disulfide bonds and function as probable ion channel inhibitors [[PMID:32398368]]. The family appears to have evolved through duplication and diversification of an ancient peptide-encoding gene.

Name: Scoloptoxin-like N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents an N-terminal domain found in arthropod proteins, including carboxypeptidase inhibitors and other uncharacterised proteins. The domain is approximately 50-60 amino acids in length and is found in spiders and related arachnids. It shares structural similarity with scoloptoxin proteins from centipedes, which are small disulfide-rich venom peptides [[PMID:24847043]]. In multi-domain proteins, this domain is often found in combination with other domains such as VldE. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Gem-associated protein 4 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of Gemin4 proteins found in insects. Gemin4 is a component of the survival motor neuron (SMN) complex that catalyses the assembly of small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), which are essential building blocks of the spliceosome. In Drosophila melanogaster, there are three almost identical paralogs (Gem4a, Gem4b and Gem4c) resulting from genomic triplication with some redundant function. These proteins are required for neuromuscular function and organismal viability. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:30563832]].

Name: Gem-associated protein 4 second TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the second TPR domain of Gemin4 proteins found in insects. Gemin4 is a component of the survival motor neuron (SMN) complex that catalyses the assembly of small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), which are essential building blocks of the spliceosome. In Drosophila melanogaster, there are three almost identical paralogs (Gem4a, Gem4b and Gem4c) resulting from genomic triplication with some redundant function. These proteins are required for neuromuscular function and organismal viability. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined [[PMID:30563832]].

Name: Gem-associated protein 4 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Gemin4 proteins found in insects. Gemin4 is a component of the survival motor neuron (SMN) complex that catalyses the assembly of small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), which are essential building blocks of the spliceosome. In Drosophila melanogaster, there are three almost identical paralogs (Gem4a, Gem4b and Gem4c) resulting from genomic triplication with some redundant function. These proteins are required for neuromuscular function and organismal viability. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:30563832]].

Name: Small cysteine and glycine repeat-containing domain
Description: This entry represents the small cysteine and glycine repeat-containing domain found in keratin-associated proteins of the type 28 family.
