The domain is characterised by tandem cysteine-glycine (CG) dipeptide repeats, typically ranging from 10 to 19 repeats per protein. These proteins are components of the hair cortex interfilamentous matrix and are essential for formation of rigid hair shafts through extensive disulphide bond cross-linking with hair keratins [[PMID:18721477]]. It is found in mammals and shows considerable variation in repeat number between members. Several members exist as pseudogenes in some human populations due to nonsense mutations.

Name: Gem-associated protein 4 TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the first tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in gem-associated protein 4 (Gem4). This domain is located at approximately residues 582-789 in Gem4 proteins and is positioned between the N-terminal Gem4_N domain and the C-terminal Gem4_C domain. Gem4 proteins are components of the survival motor neuron (SMN) complex that catalyses the assembly of small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs), which are essential building blocks of the spliceosome [[PMID:30563832]]. The domain is found in the three almost identical Drosophila paralogs Gem4a/Glos, Gem4b and Gem4c that result from genomic triplication and have redundant function. TPR domains typically mediate protein-protein interactions. The specific function of this TPR domain within the Gem4 protein architecture remains to be determined.

Name: Succinate dehydrogenase subunit 8, mitochondrial
Description: This entry represents succinate dehydrogenase subunit 8 (SDH8), a plant-specific component of mitochondrial respiratory complex II. Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) in plants contains eight subunits: the four classical SDH subunits (SDH1, SDH2, SDH3 and SDH4) plus four additional plant-specific subunits including SDH8 [[PMID:15604729]]. The classical subunits comprise a flavoprotein, an iron-sulphur protein, and a cytochrome b composed of large and small subunits. SDH8 is a small peripheral membrane protein localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane and is involved in the tricarboxylic acid cycle [[PMID:15604729]]. This family is found exclusively in plants and represents one of the additional subunits that distinguish plant complex II from those found in bacteria and other eukaryotes. The protein is approximately 46-58 amino acids in length and has been experimentally characterised in Arabidopsis thaliana.

Name: HEAT repeat domain
Description: This domain is found in uncharacterised proteins mainly from gram negative bacteria. It has similarity to known heat repeat domains.

Name: Toxin SpoIISA, transmembrane domain
Description: SpoIISA is a toxin which causes lysis of vegetatively growing cells. It forms part of a type II toxin-antitoxin system, where the SpoIISB protein, [pfam:PF14185], acts as an antitoxin. It is a transmembrane protein, with a cytoplasmic domain accounting for approximately two-thirds of the protein. The structure of the cytoplasmic domain resembles that of the GAF domains, Pfam: PF01590. SpoIISB binds to the cytoplasmic domain of SpoIISA with high affinity [[PMID:21147767]]. This entry represents the transmembrane domain [[PMID:21147767]].

Name: Venom protein 214-like helical bundle domain
Description: This entry represents a helical bundle domain found predominantly in arthropod proteins. The domain is found in various uncharacterised proteins across Hexapoda (insects) and Chelicerata (arachnids), including species from Diptera, Lepidoptera, and Araneae. In the scorpion Lychas mucronatus, this domain is present in venom protein 214, a secreted protein expressed in venom glands that contains three disulfide bonds. The domain spans approximately 95-100 amino acids and adopts a helical bundle structure. Members of this domain are found across diverse arthropod lineages, suggesting an ancient origin within this phylum. The specific function of this helical bundle domain remains to be determined.q

Name: MGR3 middle domain
Description: This entry represents a TPR-like domain found in the middle region of MGR3 and MGR3-like proteins. MGR3 is a component of the mitochondrial inner membrane i-AAA protease supercomplex that degrades misfolded mitochondrial proteins. In Saccharomyces cerevisiae MGR3, this domain is located approximately between residues 191-341 and is positioned between TPR repeats. The protein functions as an adapter together with MGR1, targeting substrates to the i-AAA protease YME1 for degradation. MGR3 is a single-pass membrane protein with its N-terminal region in the mitochondrial matrix and C-terminal region in the intermembrane space. This protein is required for growth of cells lacking the mitochondrial genome and is involved in protein quality control for misfolded proteins. This middle domain is conserved across fungal MGR3 orthologues, particularly in Saccharomycotina species. The specific function of this helical domain remains to be determined [[PMID:18843051]].

Name: Icarapin C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of icarapin-like proteins. Icarapin-like proteins are secreted glycoproteins found in honeybee venom and are known allergens (Api m 10). This C-terminal domain is found in arthropods, primarily in hymenopteran insects. The domain contains predicted N-linked glycosylation sites. While icarapin-like proteins are venom components that cause allergic reactions in humans, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Secreted virulence factor MC69
Description: This entry represents the MC69 virulence factor family found in plant pathogenic fungi. These small secreted proteins are approximately 35-40 amino acids in length and are essential for appressorial penetration of intact host epidermal cells and pathogenicity [[PMID:22589729],[PMID:31599055]]. The proteins contain conserved cysteine residues that form intramolecular or intermolecular disulfide bonds. Members include MC69 from Pyricularia oryzae and CLU5a from Colletotrichum graminicola. The family is found primarily in sordariomycete fungi, particularly species in the orders Hypocreales, Sordariales and Glomerellales that are plant pathogens.

Name: Small ribosomal subunit protein uS15 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of uS15 (universal ribosomal protein S15), found in both cytoplasmic and mitochondrial forms. The domain spans approximately 56 residues and contains the RNA-binding region characteristic of the S15/NS1 superfamily. uS15 is a component of the small ribosomal subunit and functions as a structural constituent of the ribosome during protein synthesis. The mitochondrial form (uS15m) is imported into mitochondria and incorporates into the mitochondrial ribosome small subunit. This N-terminal domain is essential for RNA binding and ribosome assembly. The domain is conserved across eukaryotes and found in nematodes and other metazoans [[PMID:21177967],[PMID:15990870],[PMID:9851916]].

Name: Mitoshell middle domain
Description: This entry represents a middle domain in mitoshell proteins involved in male meiotic processes in insects. Mitoshell proteins are required for male meiotic cytokinesis through involvement in regulation of mitochondrial aggregation and fusion, astral spindle assembly and contractile ring formation [[PMID:20491580]]. The specific function of this domain within the full-length protein remains to be determined.

Name: Mitoshell first helical domain
Description: This entry represents the first helical domain in mitoshell proteins involved in male meiotic processes in insects. Mitoshell proteins are required for male meiotic cytokinesis through involvement in regulation of mitochondrial aggregation and fusion, astral spindle assembly and contractile ring formation [[PMID:20491580]]. The specific function of this domain within the full-length protein remains to be determined.

Name: YDR249C C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the TPR C-terminal domain found in uncharacterised proteins from fungi. YDR249C is an uncharacterised protein from Saccharomyces cerevisiae with no experimentally determined function. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: SHE10/OSW C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the SHE10/OSW family of outer spore wall assembly proteins found in yeasts. The domain is approximately 250-300 amino acids in length and is found in Saccharomycetaceae species. SHE10 proteins are involved in spore wall assembly [[PMID:23966878]] and may be components of the mitochondrial RNase MRP complex [[PMID:20086051]]. The proteins contain N-terminal signal peptides for mitochondrial localisation and often have coiled-coil regions and disordered C-terminal regions. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Peroxisomal and mitochondrial division factor
Description: This entry represents the coiled-coil domain found in peroxisomal and mitochondrial division factor proteins. Peroxisomal and mitochondrial division factor 1 (PMD1) and peroxisomal and mitochondrial division factor 2 (PMD2) are tail-anchored membrane proteins involved in the morphogenesis and proliferation of peroxisomes and mitochondria [[PMID:22147290]]. The coiled-coil domain is located in the central region of these proteins and is essential for protein function. PMD1 and PMD2 form homodimers and can interact with each other through this domain [[PMID:22147290]]. PMD1 is localised to both peroxisome and mitochondrial outer membranes, whilst PMD2 is specific to mitochondrial outer membrane [[PMID:22147290]]. This domain is found in plants and is conserved across the Viridiplantae.

Name: Unclassified hydrophobin dewD
Description: This entry represents a family of unclassified hydrophobins found in filamentous fungi, particularly within the Aspergillaceae family. The prototype protein dewD from Aspergillus nidulans is a small secreted amphipathic protein that contributes to the hydrophobicity of the spore surface [[PMID:24722460]]. These proteins contain four conserved disulphide bonds and are expressed after 12 to 24 hours post induction of asexual development, correlating with the development of metulae and phialides [[PMID:24722460]]. Members of this family can self-assemble to form functional amyloid fibrils called rodlets that associate laterally to form amphipathic monolayers [[PMID:24722460],[PMID:36374077]]. These proteins contribute to fungal surface hydrophobicity, germination, stress tolerance and support fungal growth, development and dispersal [[PMID:24722460],[PMID:36374077]].

Name: DDB_G0281631 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of UPF0746 family proteins found in Dictyostelium and related amoebozoans. UPF0746 proteins are classified as being involved in actin organisation and show similarity to ankyrin repeat-containing proteins. They are found primarily in social amoebae. The proteins may represent pseudogenes in some cases. The specific function of this C-terminal TPR domain remains to be determined.

Name: SHE10 family N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of the SHE10 family of proteins. SHE10 (Sensitivity to high expression protein 10) is involved in outer spore wall assembly in fungi. The full-length protein may function as a component of the mitochondrial RNase MRP (MtMRP), a ribonucleoprotein endoribonuclease involved in cleaving RNA transcripts to generate primers for DNA replication in mitochondria. SHE10 proteins are found in Saccharomycetaceae fungi and contain an N-terminal signal peptide for mitochondrial targeting. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:23749448]].

Name: YNL193W C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of YNL193W-like proteins. YNL193W is an uncharacterised protein from Saccharomyces cerevisiae with homologues found predominantly in fungi, particularly in Saccharomycotina yeasts. The protein is 558 amino acids. Homologues are found in Saccharomycetaceae, Pichiaceae, and related yeast families. The function of this protein and the specific role of this C-terminal domain remain to be determined. This domain contains a TPR-like fold.

Name: YNL193W-like protein middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle domain of YNL193W-like proteins. YNL193W is an uncharacterised protein from Saccharomyces cerevisiae with homologues found predominantly in fungi, particularly in Saccharomycotina yeasts. The protein is 558 amino acids in length. Homologues are found in Saccharomycetaceae, Pichiaceae, and related yeast families. The function of this protein family and the specific role of this C-terminal domain remain to be determined. This domain contains a TPR-like fold with a disordered region.

Name: Serine rich endogenous peptide 16
Description: This entry represents the SCOOP (Serine rich endogenous peptide) phytocytokine family. SCOOP peptides are Brassicaceae-specific plant endogenous peptides released into the apoplast that are perceived by the MIK2 receptor in a BAK1/SERK3 and SERK4 coreceptor-dependent manner [[PMID:31001913]]. These phytocytokines modulate various physiological and antimicrobial processes including growth prevention and reactive oxygen species response regulation. SCOOP precursors accumulate at the plasma membrane and are proteolytically cleaved to release the active peptide in the apoplasm. The family contains a conserved SCOOP motif and an essential SxS motif required for MIK2 binding. SCOOP proteins are found exclusively in Brassicaceae.

Name: DDB_G0280785 first TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the first TPR domain in DDB_G0280785 of proteins found in Dictyostelium discoideum. These proteins range from 804 to 1097 amino acids in length and belong to the UPF0746 family. Most family members contain an N-terminal SAP domain ([pfam:PF02037]) involved in DNA binding.

Name: MRL1, C-terminal domain
Description: This domain is found at the C-terminal end of Pentatricopeptide repeat-containing protein MRL1 from Arabidopsis thaliana, a regulator of the large subunit (LS) of RuBisCO. It is involved either in the processing or in the stabilisation of the processed transcript, probably by acting as a barrier to the 5'>3' degradation [[PMID:20097872]]. This domain is predicted to adopt an alpha-beta configuration.

Name: Succinate dehydrogenase subunit 6
Description: This entry represents succinate dehydrogenase subunit 6 (SDH6), a plant-specific component of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex II. SDH6 is a small protein of approximately 142 amino acids that localises to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a peripheral membrane protein. Complex II in plants comprises eight subunits: the four classical SDH subunits (SDH1-4) found in bacteria and other eukaryotes, plus four additional plant-specific subunits including SDH6 [[PMID:15604729],[PMID:19924544]]. SDH6 has been identified by mass spectrometry as a component of the succinate dehydrogenase complex and is involved in the tricarboxylic acid cycle. This protein family is found exclusively in plants.

Name: Defensin 258
Description: This entry represents defensin-like protein 258 and similar proteins. This protein contains conserved cysteine residues that form disulfide bonds. Members are found in Arabidopsis thaliana and are secreted to the extracellular region. Some family members lack one of the four disulfide bonds that are typically conserved features of the family [[PMID:15955924]].

Name: Putative defensin-like protein 258 defensin N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain in plant putative defensin-like protein 258 and similar proteins. This domain contains a defense signal peptide cleavage. Members are found in Arabidopsis thaliana and are secreted to the extracellular region. Some family members lack one of the four disulfide bonds that are typically conserved features of the family [[PMID:15955924]]. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: AIM39 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain found in AIM39 (Altered inheritance of mitochondria protein 39) family proteins from fungi. AIM39 proteins are mitochondrial membrane proteins that contain a mitochondrial transit peptide, a transmembrane domain, and this conserved C-terminal domain. The proteins are single-pass membrane proteins localised to mitochondrial membranes. AIM39 belongs to a family involved in mitochondrial inheritance processes. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined. This domain covers most of the structre and is found downstream a coiled coil and a disordered region.

Name: MSS2 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of the mitochondrial protein MSS2. MSS2 is required to stabilise mitochondrial cytochrome C oxidase subunit 2 (COX2) and to translocate the C-terminal domain of COX2 through the inner membrane [[PMID:11604502]]. The full-length protein contains C-terminal TPR repeats ([pfam:PF00515]). MSS2 is found in the mitochondrial inner membrane and interacts with COX18 [[PMID:11950926]]. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8500)
Description: This domain of unknown function is found in a group of bacterial sequences, functionally uncharacterised. It may adopt a beta-propeller structure.

Name: MSC6 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain of MSC6 (Meiotic sister-chromatid recombination protein 6). MSC6 is a mitochondrial protein involved in the control of meiotic sister- chromatid recombination [[PMID:10511544]]. The protein is found in fungi and localises to the mitochondrial matrix. The full-length protein contains this N- terminal TPR domain followed by additional C-terminal regions. TPR domains typically mediate protein-protein interactions. The specific function of this N-terminal TPR domain remains to be determined.

Name: ERG29 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of ERG29, an essential protein involved in ergosterol biosynthesis in fungi. ERG29 regulates the activity of the iron-containing C4-methylsterol oxidase ERG25, which is part of the C-4 demethylation complex in the late steps of ergosterol biosynthesis [[PMID:23892078]]. The protein is localised to the endoplasmic reticulum membrane and plays a crucial role in maintaining mitochondrial and plasma membrane integrity [[PMID:23892078],[PMID:29773647]]. Disruption of ERG29 causes lethality under aerobic growth conditions and affects sterol metabolism, leading to impaired Fe-S cluster synthesis [[PMID:29773647]]. While the full-length ERG29 protein is essential for ergosterol biosynthesis, the specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: TIM14 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of TIM14, also known as PAM18, a subunit of the mitochondrial import inner membrane translocase complex. TIM14 is an essential component of the PAM complex required for translocation of transit peptide-containing proteins from the inner membrane into the mitochondrial matrix in an ATP-dependent manner. The protein is localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a single-pass membrane protein with this N-terminal domain in the intermembrane space. TIM14 contains a C-terminal J domain that is essential for co-chaperone activity and stimulation of mtHSP70. The protein forms heterodimers with PAM16 and is part of the larger PAM complex including mtHsp70, MGE1, TIM44, PAM16, and PAM17. While the full-length TIM14 protein is essential for mitochondrial protein import, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:14517234]].

Name: MRX12 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the TPR-like C-terminal domain of MRX12 proteins found in fungi. MRX12 is a component of MIOREX complexes, which are large expressome-like assemblies of ribosomes with factors involved in all steps of post-transcriptional gene expression in mitochondria. The full-length MRX12 protein associates with the mitochondrial ribosome and localises to mitochondria via an N-terminal transit peptide [[PMID:25683707]]. This C-terminal domain spans approximately residues 427-519 and is conserved across fungal species including Saccharomyces and Candida. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: MRX12 middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle TPR-like domain of MRX12 proteins found in fungi. MRX12 is a component of MIOREX complexes, which are large expressome-like assemblies of ribosomes with factors involved in all steps of post-transcriptional gene expression in mitochondria. The full-length MRX12 protein associates with the mitochondrial ribosome and localises to mitochondria via an N-terminal transit peptide. This middle domain spans approximately residues 128-308 and is conserved across fungal species including Saccharomyces, Pichia and Lachancea. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: MRX12 second TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents the second TPR-like domain of MRX12 proteins found in fungi. MRX12 is a component of MIOREX complexes, which are large expressome-like assemblies of ribosomes with factors involved in all steps of post-transcriptional gene expression in mitochondria. The full-length MRX12 protein associates with the mitochondrial ribosome and localises to mitochondria via an N-terminal transit peptide. This domain is next to the C-terminal domain and a middle domain is conserved across fungal species including Saccharomyces, Pichia and Lachancea. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: MRX12 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal TPR-like domain of MRX12 proteins found in fungi. MRX12 is a component of MIOREX complexes, which are large expressome-like assemblies of ribosomes with factors involved in all steps of post-transcriptional gene expression in mitochondria. The full-length MRX12 protein associates with the mitochondrial ribosome and localises to mitochondria via an N-terminal transit peptide [[PMID:25683707]]. This middle domain spans approximately residues 5-120 and is conserved across fungal species including Saccharomyces, Pichia and Lachancea. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: MRM1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of MRM1 (rRNA methyltransferase 1, mitochondrial) proteins. MRM1 proteins are S-adenosyl-L-methionine-dependent 2'-O-ribose methyltransferases that catalyse the formation of 2'-O-methylguanosine at position 2270 in the 21S mitochondrial large subunit ribosomal RNA [[PMID:8266080]]. This N-terminal domain is found preceding the SpoU substrate-binding domain ([pfam:PF08032]) and SpoU methylase domain ([pfam:PF00588]) in MRM1 proteins. The domain is conserved across fungi and contains helical structures. While the full-length MRM1 protein catalyses ribosomal RNA methylation, the specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: YDL157C
Description: This entry represents the YDL157C-like proteins found in mitochondrial proteins. The prototype protein YDL157C is an uncharacterised mitochondrial protein from Saccharomyces cerevisiae [[PMID:8972581],[PMID:14576278]]. This domain forms a helical bundle structure and is conserved across fungal species. YDL157C shows similarity to S. pombe tam6 protein. The protein is localised to mitochondria and is present with 752 molecules per cell in log phase growth medium [[PMID:14576278]].

Name: Defensin 207 defensin domain
Description: This entry represents the defensin domain found in Defensin 207 (DF207) proteins. These proteins are small secreted peptides involved in plant defence against fungi and other pathogens [[PMID:15955924]]. The domain is characterised by a conserved pattern of cysteine residues that form disulphide bonds, typically three disulphide bonds in most family members. However, some members of this proteins lack one of the four conserved disulphide bonds. The proteins are synthesised as precursors with an N-terminal signal peptide for secretion. This domain is found across plants, particularly in the Brassicaceae, and includes over 300 genes in Arabidopsis thaliana alone [[PMID:15955924]]. The defensin domain confers antimicrobial and fungicidal activity.

Name: Suppressor of spindle checkpoint defect 1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal TPR-like domain found in suppressor of spindle checkpoint defect 1 (such-1) and related proteins. Such-1 belongs to the APC5 family and is a component of the anaphase promoting complex/cyclosome (APC/C), a cell cycle-regulated E3 ubiquitin ligase that controls progression through mitosis and the G1 phase of the cell cycle [[PMID:17237515],[PMID:20944012]]. The APC/C complex acts by mediating ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of target proteins. This domain is found in nematodes in proteins such as such-1 and gfi-3 in Caenorhabditis elegans, which are required for the metaphase to anaphase transition in meiosis. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Mitochondrial protein PET130
Description: This entry represents the PET130 protein family, found in fungi within the Saccharomycotina subphylum. PET130 is a mitochondrial matrix protein of approximately 347 amino acids. In Saccharomyces cerevisiae, disruption of PET130 results in defects in mitochondrial protein synthesis [[PMID:11907266]]. The protein has been implicated in mitochondrial tRNA 5'-end processing. PET130 localises to the matrix side of the mitochondrial inner membrane and is present at approximately 721 molecules per cell during log phase growth. The precise molecular function of this protein remains to be determined.

Name: AMN1 small helical domain
Description: This entry represents a small helical domain found in the central region of AMN1 proteins, spanning approximately 90 amino acids. This domain is found in fungi within the Saccharomycotina subphylum and is located between the disordered N-terminal region and the leucine- rich repeat domain. AMN1 is an antagonist of the mitotic exit network, acting as a negative regulator required for multiple cell cycle checkpoints [[PMID:12628189]]. The protein acts in daughter cells to inhibit the mitotic exit pathway once the mitotic exit network has executed its function. AMN1 localises to both the cytoplasm and nucleus and interacts with TEM1 to interfere with TEM1-CDC5 association. The specific function of this helical domain within AMN1 remains to be determined.

Name: Suppressor of spindle checkpoint defect 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the TPR-like C-terminal domain of suppressor of spindle checkpoint defect 1 (SUCH-1) and related proteins. The domain spans approximately 85-90 residues at the C-terminal region of these proteins. Members include SUCH-1 and GFI-3 from Caenorhabditis elegans, which are components of the anaphase-promoting complex (APC/C) [[PMID:17237515],[PMID:20944012]]. The APC/C is a cell cycle-regulated E3 ubiquitin ligase that controls progression through mitosis and meiosis by mediating ubiquitination and degradation of target proteins. These proteins are required for the metaphase to anaphase transition in meiosis and are expressed in the developing germ line and throughout embryogenesis. The function of this specific C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: RNA polymerase-associated N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents a conserved domain found primarily at the N-terminal region of predicted RNA polymerases. The domain is also found at the C-terminal region in some proteins, such as the uncharacterised 33.9 kDa protein from the mitochondrial linear 2.3 KB plasmid of maize (Swissprot:P33544). Proteins containing this domain are frequently found in association with RNA polymerase domains ([pfam:PF00940]). The domain is approximately 160-180 amino acids in length and is found across plant species, particularly in mitochondrial and plasmid-encoded proteins. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: NPP-16/NUP50 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of NPP-16/NUP50 nuclear pore complex proteins. The domain is approximately 60-65 amino acids in length and is found N-terminally in nucleoporin proteins involved in nuclear transport. NPP-16/NUP50 is a component of the nuclear pore complex that plays a direct role in nuclear protein import and is required for anoxia-induced prophase arrest [[PMID:20053678]]. The protein interacts with importin beta and various RNA polymerase III components [[PMID:24011592]]. The domain is found in metazoan species, particularly nematodes, and is distinct from the C-terminal RanBD1 domain. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: YPP1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in YPP1 cargo-transport proteins from fungal species. YPP1 is involved in endocytosis and protein targeting to the vacuole [[PMID:17576801]]. The protein interacts with ribosomes and localises to cytoplasmic granules and the cell membrane [[PMID:16702403]]. The TPR domain is characterised by multiple alpha-helical repeats that typically mediate protein-protein interactions. YPP1 belongs to a distinct protein family and this N-terminal TPR domain is found in approximately 25 fungal proteins, primarily within the Saccharomycetes class.

Name: GEP7 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of GEP7 (genetic interactor of prohibitin 7), a mitochondrial membrane protein found in fungal species. GEP7 is involved in respiratory growth and is required for cell survival in the absence of prohibitins. The protein localises to the mitochondrial membrane as a single-pass membrane protein. This C-terminal domain contains the transmembrane region and may be involved in membrane anchoring or protein-protein interactions within the mitochondrial membrane. The domain is found in proteins ranging from approximately 234 to 302 amino acids in length across various fungal species within the Saccharomycotina subphylum [[PMID:39971732]].

Name: YPP1 middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in YPP1 cargo-transport proteins from fungal species. YPP1 is involved in endocytosis and protein targeting to the vacuole [[PMID:17576801]]. The protein interacts with ribosomes and localises to cytoplasmic granules and the cell membrane [[PMID:16702403]]. The TPR domain is characterised by multiple alpha-helical repeats that typically mediate protein-protein interactions. YPP1 belongs to a distinct protein family and this TPR domain is found in approximately 25 fungal proteins, primarily within the Saccharomycetes class.

Name: Transmembrane protein 177
Description: This entry represents the transmembrane protein 177 (TMEM177) family. TMEM177 plays a role in the early steps of cytochrome c oxidase subunit II (MT-CO2/COX2) maturation and is required for the stabilisation of COX20 and the newly synthesised MT-CO2/COX2 protein [[PMID:29154948]]. The protein localises to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a multi-pass transmembrane protein with three transmembrane helices [[PMID:29154948]]. TMEM177 is found in a complex with COX20, COA6, MT-CO2/COX2, COX18, SCO1 and SCO2, and interacts with COX20, COX1, MT-CO2/COX2, SCO1 and SCO2 in a COX20-dependent manner [[PMID:29154948]]. Family members are approximately 310-333 amino acids in length and are found across metazoa and nematodes.

Name: YPP1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in YPP1 cargo-transport proteins from fungal species. YPP1 is involved in endocytosis and protein targeting to the vacuole [[PMID:17576801]]. The protein interacts with ribosomes and localises to cytoplasmic granules and the cell membrane [[PMID:16702403]]. The TPR domain is characterised by multiple alpha-helical repeats that typically mediate protein-protein interactions. YPP1 belongs to a distinct protein family and this TPR domain is found in approximately 25 fungal proteins, primarily within the Saccharomycetes class.

Name: Uncharacterised mitochondrial protein YMF1
Description: This entry represents the YMF1 protein family found in plant mitochondria. YMF1 is an uncharacterised mitochondrial protein of 168 amino acids first identified in the liverwort Marchantia polymorpha [[PMID:1731062]]. The protein is encoded by the mitochondrial genome and is conserved across various plant lineages including eudicotyledons, particularly within the asterids and rosids groups. The protein shows conservation in flowering plants from families such as Solanaceae and Asteraceae. The function of this mitochondrial protein remains to be determined.

Name: Cellulose synthase BcsB, first FD domain
Description: This entry represents the first flavodoxin-like domain (FD) of BcsB, a protein involved in cellulose synthesis [[PMID:33712813],[PMID:23222542]]. This protein consists of four domains arranged in a triangular shape; there is a structural repeat unit formed by a CBM domain connected to an alpha/beta domain referred to as flavodoxin-like domain (FD) and forms two pairs [[PMID:33712813],[PMID:23222542]].

Name: Cellulose synthase BcsB, second FD domain
Description: This entry represents the second flavodoxin-like domain (FD) of BcsB, a protein involved in cellulose synthesis [[PMID:33712813],[PMID:23222542]]. This protein consists of four domains arranged in a triangular shape; there is a structural repeat unit formed by a CBM domain connected to an alpha/beta domain referred to as flavodoxin-like domain (FD) and forms two pairs [[PMID:33712813],[PMID:23222542]].

Name: Cellulose synthase BcsB, second CBM domain
Description: This entry includes BcsB from bacteria which is involved in cellulose synthesis. Cellulose synthesis has been identified in several bacteria. In Agrobacterium tumefaciens, for instance, cellulose has a pathogenic role: it allows the bacteria to bind tightly to their host plant cells. While several enzymatic steps are involved in cellulose synthesis, potentially the only step unique to this pathway is that catalysed by cellulose synthase. This enzyme is a multi subunit complex. BcsB binds the positive effector cyclic di-GMP. This subunit is found in several different bacterial cellulose synthase enzymes. The first recognised sequence for this subunit is BcsB. In the AcsII cellulose synthase, this subunit and the subunit corresponding to BcsA are found in the same protein. This entry represents the second CBM domain, found at the N-terminal of BcsB . It consists of four domains arranged in a triangular shape; there is a structural repeat unit formed by a CBM domain connected to a alpha/beta domain referred to as flavodoxin-like domain (FD) and forms two pairs [[PMID:33712813],[PMID:23222542]].

Name: YDR381C-A mitochondrial membrane protein
Description: This entry represents the YDR381C-A protein family found in yeasts, particularly Saccharomycetaceae and related fungi. These are small proteins of approximately 100-114 amino acids that localise to the mitochondrial outer membrane [[PMID:16823961],[PMID:16407407]]. The proteins contain a single predicted transmembrane helix and are identified as single-pass membrane proteins. YDR381C-A has been implicated in mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase assembly, mitochondrial respirasome assembly, and mitochondrial respiratory chain complex III assembly processes. The specific molecular function of this protein family remains to be determined.

Name: ECM9 middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle domain of protein ECM9. ECM9 proteins are involved in cell wall organisation and biogenesis in fungi [[PMID:9335584]]. The proteins belong to the protein prenylyltransferase superfamily and show similarity to Rab-protein geranylgeranyltransferase complex components. ECM9 is found in the cytoplasm and is associated with endoplasmic reticulum to Golgi vesicle-mediated transport processes. The protein is present at approximately 846 molecules per cell during log phase growth. This domain contains a TPR fold.

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8508)
Description: This entry represents a domain of unknown function found in large multi-domain proteins. The domain is typically located between N-terminal domains, such as lamin tail domains ([pfam:PF00932]) or cadherin-like domains, and C-terminal regions containing endonuclease/exonuclease ([pfam:PF03372], [pfam:PF19580]) or calcium-binding repeat domains ([pfam:PF00353]). The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Spirochaete HEAT repeat protein
Description: This entry represents a small family of spirochaete heat repeat proteins. These proteins form a semicircular arrangement in structure predictions. The function of this family is not known.

Name: Domain of unknown function (DUF8522)
Description: This entry represents a helical domain of unknown function found in spirochaetes. The domain is approximately 50-70 residues in length and is found N-terminal in proteins containing tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domains. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: ANTKMT methyltransferase domain
Description: This entry represents the methyltransferase domain of Adenine nucleotide translocase lysine N-methyltransferase (ANTKMT) from animals. ANTKMT is a mitochondrial protein-lysine N-methyltransferase that trimethylates adenine nucleotide translocases ANT2/SLC25A5 and ANT3/SLC25A6, thereby regulating mitochondrial respiration. The enzyme contains a methyltransferase domain that uses S-adenosyl-L-methionine as a cofactor to perform lysine trimethylation [[PMID:31213526]]. The proteins are localised to the mitochondrial membrane and are found in vertebrates including humans and mice. The specific function of this N-terminal domain is for mitochondrial targeting and membrane localisation.

Name: MHP1/HER1/GIP3 LRR domain
Description: This entry represents the LRR domain of MHP1, HER1 and GIP3 from yeast and related fungal sequences. MHP1 is an essential protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae required for microtubule function. MHP1 is essential for the formation and stabilisation of microtubules, binds to microtubules in vitro, and localises to cytoplasmic microtubules and mitotic spindles. The protein contains leucine-rich repeat regions and extensive disordered regions, with a Tau/MAP repeat-like region at the C-terminal end. MHP1 undergoes extensive phosphorylation and ubiquitination modifications [[PMID:8947554]]. GIP3 is a cytoplasmic protein that regulates protein phosphatase 1 Glc7p. When it is overexpressed, it re localizes Glc7p from the nucleus and prevents chromosome segregation. GIP3 has a paralogue, HER1, that arose from the whole genome duplication [[PMID:16537909],[PMID:18667535]].

Name: NLRC4 LRR domain
Description: This domain represents the LRR domain found in the human NLR family CARD domain-containing protein 4 (NLRC4) and related proteins. NLRC4 is a key component of inflammasomes that indirectly senses specific proteins from pathogenic bacteria and fungi and responds by assembling an inflammasome complex that promotes caspase-1 activation, cytokine production and macrophage pyroptosis [[PMID:15107016]].

Name: NLRC3 LRR domain
Description: This entry represents the LRR domain found in the human NLR family CARD domain-containing protein 3 (NLRC3) and related proteins. NLRC3 is a negative regulator of the innate immune response [[PMID:15705585],[PMID:22863753],[PMID:25277106]]. It Attenuates signaling pathways activated by Toll-like receptors and the DNA sensor STING/TMEM173 in response to pathogen- associated molecular patterns, such as intracellular poly(dA:dT), but not poly(I:C), or in response to DNA virus infection, including that of Herpes simplex virus 1 [[PMID:22863753]].

Name: CARMIL family, LRR domain
Description: This LRR domain is found in the CARMIL family, whose members are potential regulators of actin capping proteins.

Name: 2-deoxy-glucose resistant protein 1, mitochondrial
Description: This protein family includes 2-deoxy-glucose resistant protein 1, mitochondrial from Saccharomyces cerevisiae (DGR1), a 48 amino acids microprotein [[PMID:12566404]].

Name: YphG-like protein, second domain
Description: This entry represents the second domain found in YphG-like proteins from bacteria. This domain of approximately 80-90 residues adopts a beta-sandwich fold and is located between an N-terminal domain ([pfam:PF17128], DUF5107) and C-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) regions. YphG from Escherichia coli is an uncharacterised protein of 1093 residues. The protein is found across diverse bacterial taxa including Pseudomonadati, Bacillati, Actinomycetota, Bacteroidota, and Bacillota. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Type I toxin-antitoxin system toxin ShoB
Description: This entry represents ShoB, a small toxic membrane protein of approximately 26 amino acids found in Escherichia coli. ShoB is the toxic component of a type I toxin-antitoxin system [[PMID:18710431]]. The protein contains a single transmembrane helix. Overexpression causes cessation of growth and rapid membrane depolarisation [[PMID:18710431]]. Expression is regulated by the antisense small RNA OhsC, which represses translation of the toxin [[PMID:18710431]].

Name: APC1 fifth domain
Description: This entry represents the fifth domain found in anaphase-promoting complex subunit 1 proteins. This domain is part of the multi-domain architecture of APC1, which is a component of the anaphase-promoting complex/cyclosome (APC/C). The APC/C is a cell cycle-regulated E3 ubiquitin ligase that controls progression through mitosis and the G1 phase of the cell cycle [[PMID:11134076],[PMID:11832245]]. The full-length APC1 protein mediates ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of target proteins and plays roles in metaphase to anaphase transition, embryonic development, and neurotransmitter regulation. This domain is found across eukaryotes including metazoans, fungi and plants. The specific function of this domain within APC1 remains to be determined [[PMID:11134076],[PMID:11832245],[PMID:11861581],[PMID:15556870]]. This domain contains a TPR-like fold.

Name: Sperm-activating peptide C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents a C-terminal domain found in sperm-activating peptide precursor proteins from Strongylocentrotus purpuratus. This precursor protein contains multiple copies of speract and speract-like peptides that cause stimulation of sperm respiration and motility through intracellular alkalinisation, transient elevations of cAMP, cGMP and calcium levels in sperm cells [[PMID:2334699],[PMID:7061446],[PMID:6256397]]. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined. This domain contains a pectate lyase-like fold.

Name: BAR-1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of BAR-1 and related beta-catenin proteins found in nematodes. BAR-1 is a beta-catenin homologue that participates in the Wnt signalling pathway in Caenorhabditis elegans [[PMID:9716532]]. The protein contains armadillo repeats covering its whole structure. BAR-1 has been shown to be involved in transcriptional activation [[PMID:11560894]]. BAR-1 functions as a transcriptional coactivator in association with the transcription factor POP-1 and is required for vulval development, Q neuroblast migration, and other developmental processes [[PMID:9716532],[PMID:11560894]]. This domain is found predominantly in nematodes and may contribute to the transcriptional regulatory function of these beta-catenin proteins.

Name: ECM19
Description: This entry represents the ECM19 protein, originally identified as an extracellular mutant protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae [[PMID:9335584]]. ECM19 is a single-pass transmembrane protein localised to the mitochondrial membrane [[PMID:16823961]]. The protein may be involved in cell wall organisation and biogenesis [[PMID:9335584]]. ECM19 was identified in large-scale screens for genes involved in cell surface biosynthesis and architecture, though its specific function remains unclear [[PMID:9335584]]. The protein is found in fungi, particularly Saccharomycotina, and represents a small protein that contains a transmembrane helix. The function of this protein remains to be determined.

Name: Altered inheritance of mitochondria protein 44
Description: This entry represents AIM44 (Altered inheritance of mitochondria protein 44), a protein localised to the bud neck in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and related fungi [[PMID:11309124]]. AIM44 is involved in regulating mitochondrial inheritance, with disruption leading to increased frequency of mitochondrial genome loss [[PMID:19300474]]. The protein is found at the cell division site, cellular bud neck, septin collar, and split septin rings. AIM44 expression is controlled by the transcription factor SWI5 [[PMID:11309124]]. The protein contains coiled-coil regions and multiple disordered regions, and belongs to the AIM44 protein family. AIM44 is found in fungi, particularly Saccharomycotina, where it plays a role in maintaining proper mitochondrial inheritance during cell division. The function of this protein is to ensure proper inheritance of mitochondria.

Name: Autophagy-related protein 33
Description: This entry represents the ATG33 family of autophagy-related proteins. ATG33 is involved in the selective degradation of mitochondria via autophagy during starvation and at post-log phase. The protein is localised to mitochondrial membranes and functions as a multi-pass membrane protein. ATG33 was identified through genomic screens for yeast mutants defective in mitophagy [[PMID:19793921],[PMID:20364111]]. This family is found in fungi and belongs to the ATG33 protein family. The protein undergoes phosphorylation at serine residues and is essential for the selective autophagy of mitochondria.

Name: Small ribosomal subunit protein uS17m C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of the small ribosomal subunit protein uS17m found in mitochondria. The uS17 protein is a component of the mitochondrial ribosome small subunit which comprises a 12S rRNA and approximately 30 distinct proteins. This C-terminal domain is found in metazoan mitochondrial ribosomes. The protein belongs to the universal ribosomal protein uS17 family and functions as a structural constituent of the ribosome involved in mitochondrial translation.

Name: Mitochondrial 15S rRNA processing factor CCM1, C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal region of the mitochondrial 15S rRNA processing factor (CCM1) found in yeast. CCM1 regulates the maturation of the mitochondrial small ribosomal subunit by controlling 5'-end processing of the 15S rRNA. It binds to the 5'-precursor region of the 15S rRNA at the site later occupied by mS47 in the mature mitochondrial small subunit (mtSSU). Through structure- and sequence-specific RNA recognition, CCM1 interacts with a single-stranded segment of the precursor, specifically recognising nucleotides -6 to -1. The replacement of CCM1 by mS47 is linked to the irreversible removal of the 15S rRNA precursor and is accompanied by conformational changes in mitoribosomal proteins uS5m and mS26. These structural rearrangements mark the completion of 5'-end rRNA processing by protecting the mature 5'-end and stabilising mS47. The 5'-precursor removal, together with the dissociation of CCM1, may be catalysed by the 5'-3' exoribonuclease Pet127. This domain adopts a TPR-like fold .

Name: Uncharacterized mitochondrial protein AtMg01050
Description: This entry represents uncharacterized mitochondrial protein AtMg01050 found in plants. This protein contains an all helical fold followed by a disordered region. This protein is predicted to be located in the mitochondrion.

Name: Uncharacterized mitochondrial protein AtMg00660
Description: This entry represents Uncharacterized mitochondrial protein AtMg00660 found in Arabidopsis thaliana. This protein is predicted to be located in the mitochondrion.

Name: Putative defensin-like protein 134
Description: This entry represents defensin-like proteins (DEFL) found in plants. These small secreted proteins belong to a large gene family with over 300 members in Arabidopsis thaliana [[PMID:15955924]]. The proteins contain four conserved disulphide bonds that stabilise their structure. DEFL proteins are characterised by antimicrobial and antifungal activities and play roles in plant defence responses.

Name: Mitochondrial association factor 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of mitochondrial association factor 1 (MAF1) found in Toxoplasma gondii and related Apicomplexa. MAF1 is a parasitophorous vacuole membrane protein that mediates tethering of the parasitophorous vacuole to host mitochondria during infection [[PMID:26920761],[PMID:22167473]]. The protein interacts with host TOMM70 and SAMM50 to disrupt the host mitochondrial intermembrane space bridging complex, promoting formation of stress-induced mitochondrial outer membrane vesicles [[PMID:28787542],[PMID:24781109]]. The C-terminal region is necessary but not sufficient for host mitochondrial association [[PMID:29505111]]. The domain shows structural conservation across Apicomplexa. The specific molecular function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: PRP6, TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents a TPR repeat domain found in pre-mRNA splicing factor 6 (PRP6) found in mammals, yeast and Dictyostelium discoideum. PRP6 is involved in pre-mRNA splicing as component of the U4/U6-U5 tri-snRNP complex, one of the building blocks of the spliceosome. It enhances dihydrotestosterone-induced transactivation activity of AR, as well as dexamethasone-induced transactivation activity of NR3C1, but does not affect estrogen-induced transactivation [[PMID:20118938],[PMID:21549338],[PMID:28781166]].

Name: Cilia and flagella-associated protein 70 middle TPR-like domain
Description: This entry represents the middle TPR-like domain of cilia and flagella-associated protein 70 (CFAP70). CFAP70 is an axoneme-binding protein that plays a role in the regulation of ciliary motility and cilium length [[PMID:30158508]]. The protein localises to the cilium axoneme and flagellum, with particular concentration at the base of the outer dynein arm. While the full-length CFAP70 protein is involved in ciliary motility regulation, the specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: CERLI2 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain found in CERLI2 (cytosolically exposed rhoptry leaflet interacting protein 2) and related proteins from apicomplexan parasites. CERLI2 is a rhoptry surface protein that plays an important role in rhoptry physiology and is essential for merozoite invasion of host erythrocytes [[PMID:35140336]]. The protein localises to the cytosolic face of the rhoptry bulb membrane in merozoites. While the full-length CERLI2 protein is essential for merozoite invasion, the specific function of this domain remains to be determined. This family is found in apicomplexan parasites, particularly in Plasmodium species. This domain contains a pectate lyase-like fold.

Name: Cilia and flagella-associated protein 70 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of cilia and flagella-associated protein 70 (CFAP70). CFAP70 is an axoneme-binding protein that plays a role in the regulation of ciliary motility and cilium length [[PMID:30158508]]. The protein localises to the cilium axoneme and flagellum, with particular concentration at the base of the outer dynein arm. While the full-length CFAP70 protein is involved in ciliary motility regulation, the specific function of this domain remains to be determined. This domain contains a TPR-like fold.

Name: Biotrophy-associated secreted protein 3RN
Description: This entry represents the biotrophy-associated secreted protein 3 (BAS3). BAS3 is a secreted effector protein involved in biotrophic colonisation of plant cells by the rice blast fungus Pyricularia oryzae [[PMID:19357089]]. The protein localises to the appressorial penetration site and outlines the primary hyphae during fungal invasion. Expression is highly up-regulated in invasive hyphae, suggesting a specific role in the biotrophic phase of infection [[PMID:19357089]]. This family is found in filamentous fungi, particularly in plant pathogens of the order Magnaporthales.

Name: BEM4 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal TPR-like domain found in BEM4 family proteins. BEM4 from Saccharomyces cerevisiae is a 633-residue protein that acts as a GTPase-GDP dissociation stimulator for Rho family GTPases including CDC42, RHO1, RHO2 and RHO4 [[PMID:8754839],[PMID:8754840]]. The protein is involved in cell polarity control and cell wall organisation [[PMID:8754839],[PMID:8754840]]. BEM4 localises to both nucleus and cytoplasm and interacts directly with multiple small GTPases [[PMID:8754839],[PMID:8754840]]. This N-terminal domain contains TPR-like repeats and is found in yeasts including Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Schizosaccharomyces pombe. The specific function of this TPR-like domain within BEM4 remains to be determined.

Name: AtMg01310-like mitochondrial protein
Description: This entry represents a family of small uncharacterized proteins found in plant mitochondria. The prototype member is AtMg01310 from Arabidopsis thaliana, a 136 amino acid protein encoded by the mitochondrial genome. This gene is also present as a duplicated copy in the nuclear chromosome 2 centromere due to a 270 kb mitochondrial genome duplication event. The family is found primarily in eudicotyledonous plants, particularly in the Brassicales and Solanales orders. Members are typically small proteins of approximately 130-150 amino acids. The function of this protein family remains to be determined [[PMID:11309509]].

Name: MRP13-like N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of MRP13 and related mitochondrial ribosomal proteins. MRP13 is a component of the small ribosomal subunit (mS27) of the mitochondrial ribosome. This N-terminal domain follows the mitochondrial transit peptide and comprises approximately 90 amino acids of the mature protein. The domain contains multiple alpha-helical regions and is part of the structural framework of the 37S mitochondrial small ribosomal subunit. The full-length protein is involved in mitochondrial protein synthesis and is found in fungi. The specific function of this N-terminal domain within the ribosomal context remains to be determined [[PMID:25609543],[PMID:28154081]].

Name: MRP13-like C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of MRP13 and related mitochondrial ribosomal proteins. MRP13 is a component of the small ribosomal subunit (mS27) of the mitochondrial ribosome. The domain contains a TPR-like fold and is part of the structural framework of the 37S mitochondrial small ribosomal subunit. The full-length protein is involved in mitochondrial protein synthesis and is found in fungi. The specific function of this N-terminal domain within the ribosomal context remains to be determined [[PMID:11309509],[PMID:28154081]].

Name: NXF2 LRR domain
Description: This entry represents a LRR domain found towards the N-terminal of NXF2 from Drosophila and C.elegans, which is found adjacent [pfam:PF29450]. NXF2 may mediate the export of the majority of mRNAs from the nucleus to the cytoplasm. NXF2 forms a complex with piwi and Nxt1 which acts as effectors of cotranscriptional transposon silencing [[PMID:31384064],[PMID:31368590],[PMID:31570835]]. On recruitment to a target transcript, interacts with single stranded RNA, thereby anchoring the complex via the nascent target transcript to chromatin and allowing Panx to recruit silencing effectors to establishing repressive heterochromatin at transposon loci [[PMID:31384064],[PMID:31368590],[PMID:31570835]].

Name: Hydrophobin-like domain
Description: This entry represents a hydrophobin-like domain found in fungi. Hydrophobins are small amphipathic proteins that can self-assemble at hydrophobic-hydrophilic interfaces. The domain contains four disulfide bonds that are characteristic of hydrophobin proteins. In Botrytis cinerea, despite being called hydrophobin-like, this protein is not involved in conferring surface hydrophobicity to conidia and aerial hyphae [[PMID:21232149]]. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: AIM36 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of AIM36 (Altered inheritance of mitochondria protein 36), also known as FMP39 (Found in mitochondria protein 39). This domain is found in fungi and represents the cytoplasmic portion of a mitochondrial membrane protein. AIM36 contains a single transmembrane helix at its N-terminal region followed by this C-terminal domain. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined, though it likely plays a role in mitochondrial inheritance processes.

Name: Y' element helicase middle domain
Description: This entry represents the middle helical domain of Y' element ATP-dependent helicases from Saccharomyces cerevisiae. These helicases catalyse DNA unwinding and are involved in telomerase- independent telomere maintenance. The proteins are induced in the absence of telomerase TLC1 and belong to the yeast subtelomeric Y' repeat subfamily of helicases. The helicases contain helicase ATP-binding and C-terminal domains in addition to this middle domain [[PMID:9837911]].

Name: Y' element helicase C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of Y' element ATP-dependent helicases from Saccharomyces cerevisiae. These helicases catalyse DNA unwinding and are involved in telomerase- independent telomere maintenance [[PMID:9837911]]. The proteins are induced in the absence of telomerase TLC1 and belong to the yeast subtelomeric Y' repeat subfamily of helicases. This domain is found alongside helicase ATP-binding domains and Sir1 domains in these proteins.

Name: BEM4 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of BEM4, a GTPase-GDP dissociation stimulator from Saccharomyces cerevisiae. BEM4 acts as a guanine nucleotide exchange factor for Rho family small GTPases and is involved in control of polarised cell growth via CDC42- mediated signalling and cell-wall organisation via RHO1-mediated signalling [[PMID:8754839],[PMID:8754840]]. BEM4 interacts directly with CDC42, RHO1, RHO2 and RHO4, and also interacts with CDC11. The protein is localised to both nucleus and cytoplasm.

Name: Small integral membrane protein 2
Description: This entry represents SMIM2, a small integral membrane protein found in mammals. SMIM2 is a single-pass membrane protein with a transmembrane helix and a disordered C-terminal region. The protein interacts with UBQLN1 and UBQLN2, according to IntAct database. SMIM2 is tissue enriched in testis and belongs to the small mitochondrial integral membrane protein family. The function of this protein remains to be determined.

Name: Hymenoptaecin
Description: This entry represents hymenoptaecin, an antimicrobial peptide with antibacterial activity found in Hymenoptera [[PMID:7961803]]. The peptide is synthesised as a precursor with a signal peptide and propeptide that are cleaved to produce the mature antimicrobial peptide. Hymenoptaecin is part of the innate immune response and defence against bacterial infection. Some proteins contain multiple copies of this domain in tandem repeats.

Name: Defensin 287
Description: This entry represents defensin 287 and similar proteins found in plants, particularly in Brassicaceae and Fabaceae. These proteins belong to the DEFL group and are secreted antimicrobial peptides involved in plant defence responses against fungi and other pathogens. The proteins contain characteristic cysteine residues that form disulfide bonds, although some members may lack one or more of the typical disulfide bonds. Arabidopsis contains over 300 defensin-like genes organised throughout the genome [[PMID:15955924]].

Name: Serine rich endogenous peptide
Description: This entry represents serine rich endogenous peptide precursors found in Brassicaceae species. These proteins are phytocytokines that are processed to release small peptides into the apoplast [[PMID:34535661],[PMID:33514716],[PMID:30715439]]. The mature peptides are perceived by the MIK2 receptor kinase and trigger immune responses and regulate plant growth [[PMID:33514716],[PMID:30715439]]. SCOOP peptides modulate various physiological processes including root growth prevention, reactive oxygen species response regulation, and defense against pathogens and herbivores [[PMID:34535661],[PMID:33514716],[PMID:30715439],[PMID:33514716]]. The precursor proteins contain signal peptides and are proteolytically cleaved to release the active SCOOP peptides [[PMID:30715439]]. These peptides interact with MIK2 via its extracellular leucine-rich repeat domain and trigger formation of receptor complexes with BAK1/SERK3 and SERK4 coreceptors [[PMID:33514716],[PMID:30715439]].

Name: Little elongation complex subunit 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of little elongation complex subunit 1 (Ice1). The full-length Ice1 protein is a component of the little elongation complex (LEC), which is required to regulate small nuclear RNA gene transcription by RNA polymerase II and III [[PMID:22195968],[PMID:23932780]]. Ice1 acts as a scaffold protein that promotes LEC complex formation and recruitment and RNA polymerase II occupancy at snRNA genes in subnuclear bodies. The complete protein is located in the nucleus and belongs to the ICE1 family. The specific function of this second domain remains to be determined. This domain contains a TPR-like fold.

Name: Little elongation complex subunit 1 first domain
Description: This entry represents the first TPR-like domain of little elongation complex subunit 1 (Ice1). The full-length Ice1 protein is a component of the little elongation complex (LEC), which is required to regulate small nuclear RNA gene transcription by RNA polymerase II and III [[PMID:22195968],[PMID:23932780]]. Ice1 acts as a scaffold protein that promotes LEC complex formation and recruitment and RNA polymerase II occupancy at snRNA genes in subnuclear bodies. The complete protein is located in the nucleus and belongs to the ICE1 family. The specific function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: Probable citrulline exporter
Description: This entry includes SPBC365.16, a mitochondrial membrane protein from fission yeast, predicted to be a mitochondrial citrulline exporter in L-arginine biosynthetic pathway (arginine auxotroph) [[PMID:32896087]].

Name: CBP1/cbp8
Description: This entry includes cbp8 from fission yeast and its orthologue from Baker's yesst CBP1. cbp8 is a translation factor for cob1/cytochrome b and it plays a role in cob1 mRNA stabilization and required for correct folding of the protein [[PMID:34634819]]. CBP1 is responsible for conferring a stable 5'-end on cytochrome b mRNA [[PMID:6325407],[PMID:22842922]].

Name: DCT6 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal beta-propeller domain found in DCT6 proteins. DCT6 (Daf-16/foxo controlled, germline tumor- affecting protein 6) may have a role in tumor suppression. The domain is found in nematodes including Caenorhabditis elegans and Caenorhabditis briggsae. The protein contains WD40/YVTN repeat- like structural features suggesting a beta-propeller fold. DCT6 proteins are controlled by the DAF-16/FOXO transcription factor pathway. The specific function of this central beta-propeller domain within DCT6 proteins remains to be determined [[PMID:17934462]].

Name: Anaphase-promoting complex subunit 5 central TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the central tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in anaphase-promoting complex subunit 5 (APC5) and related proteins. APC5 is a component of the anaphase promoting complex/cyclosome (APC/C), a cell cycle-regulated E3 ubiquitin ligase that controls progression through mitosis and the G1 phase of the cell cycle. The APC/C complex acts by mediating ubiquitination and subsequent degradation of target proteins. This central TPR domain contains multiple tetratricopeptide repeats that are six to seven residues longer than canonical TPR motifs. The domain is found in proteins across eukaryotes, including human ANAPC5, C. elegans gfi-3 and such-1. The specific function of this central TPR domain within the APC5 protein remains to be determined.

Name: DCT6 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain found in DCT6 proteins. DCT6 (Daf-16/foxo controlled, germline tumor- affecting protein 6) may have a role in tumor suppression. The domain is found in nematodes including Caenorhabditis elegans and Caenorhabditis briggsae. The protein contains WD40/YVTN repeat- like structural features suggesting a beta-propeller fold. DCT6 proteins are controlled by the DAF-16/FOXO transcription factor pathway [[PMID:17934462]].

Name: DCT6 central helical domain
Description: This entry represents the central helical domain found in DCT6 proteins. DCT6 (Daf-16/foxo controlled, germline tumor- affecting protein 6) may have a role in tumor suppression. The domain is found in nematodes including Caenorhabditis elegans and Caenorhabditis briggsae. The protein contains WD40/YVTN repeat- like structural features suggesting a beta-propeller fold. DCT6 proteins are controlled by the DAF-16/FOXO transcription factor pathway [[PMID:17934462]].ED A0A158PHV7.1/164-340; A0A158PHV7.1/164-325;

Name: Cytochrome c oxidase subunit 26
Description: This entry represents cytochrome c oxidase subunit 26 (COX26), a component of the mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase complex (complex IV). Cytochrome c oxidase is the terminal enzyme in the mitochondrial electron transport chain that catalyses the reduction of oxygen to water. The complex is composed of a catalytic core of three subunits (COX1, COX2 and COX3) encoded in mitochondrial DNA, and nine supernumerary subunits encoded in the nuclear genome. COX26 is one of these supernumerary subunits and is a single-pass transmembrane protein localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane. The protein contains an N-terminal mitochondrial transit peptide that is cleaved upon import. COX26 belongs to the fungal cytochrome c oxidase subunit 26 family and is found in yeast and related fungi [[PMID:30598554]].

Name: TRY5 central TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the central tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in TRY5 family transcriptional regulators and related proteins. TRY5 proteins are transcription factors involved in yeast cell adherence and biofilm formation. This protein is found in fungal species, particularly in the Candida/Lodderomyces clade and related Saccharomycotina. The specific function of this central TPR domain in TRY5 proteins remains to be determined.

Name: TRY5 C-terminal TPR domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) domain found in TRY5 family transcriptional regulators and related proteins. TRY5 proteins are transcription factors involved in yeast cell adherence and biofilm formation. This protein is found in fungal species, particularly in the Candida/Lodderomyces clade and related Saccharomycotina. The specific function of this central TPR domain in TRY5 proteins remains to be determined [[PMID:22359502]].

Name: RRG1 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of RRG1 family proteins. RRG1 (Required for respiratory growth protein 1) is essential for respiratory growth and required for mitochondrial protein synthesis. The protein is also required for vacuolar acidification and mitochondrial genome maintenance. RRG1 is localised to the mitochondrion and may be N-glycosylated, with glycosylation being important for correct localisation. The N-terminal domain is found across fungal species including Saccharomyces, Candida, Lachancea and related Saccharomycetaceae. The specific function of this N-terminal domain within RRG1 proteins remains to be determined [[PMID:36482135],[PMID:19562342],[PMID:19751518]].

Name: Defensin-like DF298 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain found in plant defensin-like proteins from the DEFL family. These proteins belong to a large family of more than 300 defensin-like genes in Arabidopsis thaliana. DEFL proteins are secreted antimicrobial peptides involved in plant defence responses, particularly against fungal pathogens. They are characterised by multiple disulphide bonds that stabilise their structure, though some family members contain six disulphide bonds instead of the typical four. The proteins are found in the extracellular region where they function in killing cells of other organisms and defence against fungi. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Defensin-like protein DF298 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain found in plant defensin-like proteins from the DEFL family. The domain is approximately 35 residues in length and is located at the N- terminal region and includes the signal peptide. These proteins belong to a large family of more than 300 defensin-like genes in Arabidopsis thaliana. DEFL proteins are secreted antimicrobial peptides involved in plant defence responses, particularly against fungal pathogens. They are characterised by multiple disulphide bonds that stabilise their structure, though some family members contain six disulphide bonds instead of the typical four. The proteins are found in the extracellular region where they function in killing cells of other organisms and defence against fungi. The specific function of this N-terminal domain remains to be determined [[PMID:15955924]].

Name: Defensin-like protein 306 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of defensin-like protein 306 and related proteins found in plants. The full-length proteins belong to the DEFL family and are secreted antimicrobial peptides involved in plant defence [[PMID:15955924]]. These proteins contain multiple disulphide bonds. The function of this domain remains to be determined.

Name: SKP2 LRR domain
Description: This entry represents the LRRs domain of SKP2 from human and its homologues. It is the substrate recognition component of a SCF E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase complex which mediates the ubiquitination and subsequent proteasomal degradation of target proteins involved in cell cycle progression, signal transduction and transcription [[PMID:37028429],[PMID:32267835],[PMID:22770219]]. It specifically recognises phosphorylated CDKN1B/p27kip and is involved in regulation of G1/S transition.

Name: Chemotaxis protein MotC
Description: This entry represents chemotaxis protein MotC (motility protein C) from Alphaproteobacteria including Sinorhizobium, Rhizobium and related species from the Hyphomicrobiales order. MotC is approximately 430-436 amino acids in length. The protein is required for the rotation of the flagellar motor and might control the energy flux or coupling that drives flagellar rotation. MotC is localised to the periplasm and contains an N-terminal signal peptide. Members are classified in NCBIfam as PRK12798 and have been studied in the context of quorum sensing regulation. The function of this protein family remains to be fully determined.

Name: ASTRA-associated protein 1
Description: This entry represents the ASA1 family of proteins found in fungi. ASA1 is a component of the ASTRA complex involved in chromatin remodelling. The protein is localised to the nucleus and contains multiple WD40 repeats that form a beta-propeller structure. The WD40 repeats are characteristic structural motifs that facilitate protein-protein interactions. ASA1 functions as part of the ASTRA chromatin remodelling machinery complex.

Name: Conotoxin Cl14.12
Description: This entry represents the CUEC family of conotoxins found in gastropod molluscs. These are secreted neurotoxic peptides expressed by the venom duct of cone snails [[PMID:20363338]]. The mature toxin contains a cysteine framework XIV with four cysteine residues and two disulphide bonds [[PMID:20363338]]. Conotoxins are derived from larger precursor proteins that contain a signal peptide and propeptide region [[PMID:20363338]]. These toxins exhibit neurotoxic activity and are important components of cone snail venom [[PMID:20363338]].

Name: MICOS complex subunit MIC19 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of MICOS complex subunit MIC19 proteins found in nematodes [[PMID:21248201]]. MIC19 is a component of the mitochondrial contact site and cristae organising system complex that plays a role in maintaining mitochondrial morphology. The protein is localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane and is lipid-anchored on the intermembrane side. Disruption of the gene results in abnormal mitochondrial morphology with localised swellings and tubular extensions. The N-terminal domain precedes the characteristic CHCH domain that contains two disulphide bonds [[PMID:21248201]].

Name: YxzG family
Description: This entry represents an uncharacterised protein family of approximately 150 amino acids found in bacteria, including YxzG from Bacillus subtilis. The function of this family remains to be determined. The structure prediction of this protein shows it to form three beta propeller blades.

Name: ScaA C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents a TPR (tetratricopeptide) repeat found in ScaA, the Sca1 complex scaffold protein from Dictyostelium discoideum. ScaA contains four TPR repeats that contribute to its scaffolding function within the Sca1 complex [[PMID:20493808]]. The Sca1 complex regulates cell motility, chemotaxis and signal relay by controlling RasC activation and the TORC2-Akt pathway. ScaA acts as a molecular scaffold bringing together gefA, gefH, phr and PP2A subunits within the complex [[PMID:20493808]]. This TPR repeat is found in amoebozoan species and contributes to protein- protein interactions essential for complex assembly and function.

Name: MRX9 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of MRX9 (MIOREX complex component 9), a mitochondrial protein found in fungi. MRX9 is a component of MIOREX complexes, which are large expressome-like assemblies of ribosomes with factors involved in post- transcriptional gene expression [[PMID:25683707]]. The full-length protein is a multi-pass transmembrane protein localised to mitochondrial membranes and associates with the mitochondrial ribosome. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: CLEC16A helical domain
Description: This is a central alpha solenoid domain of C-type lectin domain family 16, member A (CLEC16A, the Drosophila orthologue Ema and GOP-1 in C. elegans), an evolutionarily conserved endosomal membrane protein required for trafficking of fluid-phase and receptor-mediated endocytic cargos. It is required for mitophagy, autophagy and endosome maturation [[PMID:22493244],[PMID:20194640],[PMID:24949970]]. This protein has been identified as a susceptibility gene for autoimmune diseases like type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis and adrenal dysfunction. This domain is also present in GFS9/TT9 (TRANSPARENT TESTA 9) a protein from Arabidopsis required for vacuolar development through membrane fusion at vacuoles and for membrane trafficking machinery and accumulation of flavonoids in the seed coat [[PMID:25116949]].

Name: Defensin-like protein 78/80
Description: This entry represents a defensin-like domain found in plants, particularly in the Brassicaceae and other eudicotyledon groups. The domain is characterised by a conserved pattern of four disulphide bonds and is found in small secreted proteins. These proteins belong to the DEFL group and are involved in antimicrobial defence responses, particularly against fungi [[PMID:15955924],[PMID:17565583]]. The mature protein is secreted following cleavage of an N-terminal signal peptide. This defensin-like domain forms part of a large group of over 300 related genes in Arabidopsis thaliana.

Name: J domain-containing protein 1 C-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the C-terminal domain of J domain-containing protein 1 (JID1) and related proteins found in fungi. JID1 is a mitochondrial membrane protein that functions as a probable chaperone and belongs to the DnaJ protein group. The protein contains an N-terminal J domain and a transmembrane helix region, with this C-terminal domain located downstream. JID1 is localised to the mitochondrial inner membrane as a single-pass membrane protein. The specific function of this C-terminal domain remains to be determined.

Name: Defensin-like protein 109-like
Description: This entry represents a defensin-like domain found predominantly in the Brassicaceae, particularly in Arabidopsis, Brassica and Capsella species. The domain is characterised by a conserved pattern of four disulphide bonds and is found in small secreted proteins. These proteins belong to the DEFL group and are involved in antimicrobial defence responses, particularly against fungi [[PMID:15955924],[PMID:17565583]]. The mature protein is secreted following cleavage of an N-terminal signal peptide. This defensin-like domain forms part of a large group of over 300 related genes in Arabidopsis thaliana.

Name: Spindle defective protein 3 middle domain
Description: This entry represents a middle domain of spindle defective protein 3 (SPD-3) found in nematodes. SPD-3 is a multi-pass mitochondrial outer membrane protein required for mitotic spindle alignment and asymmetric cell division [[PMID:9649522],[PMID:17947426]]. The protein is also essential for motor-driven chromosome movement and homologous chromosome pairing during meiosis [[PMID:23671424]].

Name: Spindle defective protein 3 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of spindle defective protein 3 (SPD-3) found in nematodes. SPD-3 is a multi-pass mitochondrial outer membrane protein required for mitotic spindle alignment and asymmetric cell division [[PMID:9649522],[PMID:17947426]]. The protein is also essential for motor-driven chromosome movement and homologous chromosome pairing during meiosis [[PMID:23671424]].

Name: DDB_G0287209 N-terminal domain
Description: This entry represents the N-terminal domain of transmembrane domain-containing protein DDB_G0287209 from Dictyostelium discoideum. This protein is predicted to be a multi-pass membrane protein localised to cellular membranes and contains multiple transmembrane helical regions.
